> Is this shippable without addressing the language server? Feels like it should be included in this PR.

What needs to be addressed in the language server?
author: ry

body:
Currently the lsp requires an explicit option of `"config"` to be set in the client configuration for a configuration file to apply:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/c487b7ed54a36edcf324005a6ff6e76663544e06/cli/lsp/config.rs#L157-L159

And when there is something impacted in the environment, like when the client configuration or the configuration file iteself changes, then the language server processes an update:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/c487b7ed54a36edcf324005a6ff6e76663544e06/cli/lsp/language_server.rs#L524

If we have automatic behaviour to identify the file when `deno run`, then it only seem logical that the behaviour would be similar by default for the language server, not requiring explicit configuration, which mirrors the command line today, and I would assume that delivery of that functionality would be part of this feature, since a fair amount of people utilise the language server.

Generally this should be fairly straight forward with just a "workspace" and looking in the "workspace root", but when you have "workspace folders" (multi-root workspaces), I suspect it could get a bit convoluted.

Side note, I just realised we are only watching JSON files in the workspace (and not JSONC):

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/c487b7ed54a36edcf324005a6ff6e76663544e06/cli/lsp/language_server.rs#L722-L728

And not watching for changes in `.jsonc` files, but if a configuration file is identified, it can be registered for changes, I believe.

Another discussion at one point, which could be delivered later, is that if we automatically detect a `deno.json`/`deno.jsonc` it would imply that the project/workspace was implicitly Deno "enabled" not requiring explicit enablement as it does today.

body:
This still isn't right because I'm using `diagnosticsMessagesToIgnore=1` and the numbers I'm getting are 287ms instead of 200ms. But I think it's getting closer to quick-lint-js's benchmark.

```
bench: 287,436,395 ns/iter (+/- 12,993,664)
```
author: ry

body:
> This still isn't right because I'm using `diagnosticsMessagesToIgnore=1` and the numbers I'm getting are 287ms instead of 200ms. But I think it's getting closer to quick-lint-js's benchmark.
> 
> ```
> bench: 287,436,395 ns/iter (+/- 12,993,664)
> ```

Well, it will be 200ms + tsc diagnostic request time + maybe blocking time if there is some other request in tsc.  The whole purpose of the debounce was to allow other "high priority" tsc requests to get in front of the diagnostic request, but requests are processed in the order they are received:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/eda6e58520276786bd87e411d0284eb56d9686a6/cli/lsp/tsc.rs#L100-L111

Given the test case though I can't specifically think of another request that might be going on, but it could be.

I did check on a reasonably sized project and I am getting:

|measurement|time|
|-|-|
| update_diagnostics_deps | 10ms |
| update_diagnostics_lint | 10ms |
| update_diagnostics_ts | 69ms |

So getting 287ms for a tsc diagnostics publish on a large file is not unreasonable or unexpected.

The first one being < 200ms I believe is actually another side effect all together of a design limitation of the test harness. We tried and tried to find a non-blocking way to "poll" for messages, but haven't, so any unread unsolicited messages will be read and returned, but if you try to poll for a message that isn't there, it will just hang.  So you are likely reading something that was previously sent.
author: kitsonk

body:
I've updated the benchmark to wait until we get the first "deno-lint" diagnostic (as described in the source field of the diagnostics. After this lands I'll send a PR to quick-lint-js to change their benchmark.

```
# cargo bench --bench lsp_bench_standalone
   Compiling deno v1.17.3 (/Users/ry/src/deno/cli)
    Finished bench [optimized] target(s) in 7.03s
     Running unittests (target/release/deps/lsp_bench_standalone-50969af938e69a9b)

running 1 test
test incremental_change_wait ... bench: 288,701,433 ns/iter (+/- 6,529,414)

author: ry

body:
> It's surprising but each iteration is still quite a bit longer than 200ms.

There are other factors at play that try to keep it from becoming a "blocking" thing, but as I mentioned above, I think it can be refactored so that there is no debouncing of lint and "deno" diagnostics, we only debounce the TypeScript ones, and then we would get a "real" picture of how long it takes to issue lint diagnostics as well as provide a more responsive experience for users.

author: bartlomieju

body:
As @andreubotella this PR will start crashing after https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/13387 is merged. The proper fix is to store a `v8::Global<v8::Value>` on `MainWorker`, that will point to `globalThis`. This global should be created just after bootstrapping a worker. Then when `load` and `unload` events are dispatched they should call `dispatchEvent` method directly on a `v8::Local<v8::Value>` obtained from the stored global, instead of using `self.js_runtime.execute_script()`.

EDIT: Another solution suggested by Andreu:
```
btw, since fields of the global are accessible at all times from temporary scripts, you could just do dispatchEvent(new Event('load'))
```

body:
Are we really sure we want to do this... All "diagnostic" output of a test goes to stderr, including error messages.  This means that every lsp test will output some 10 lines or so of "setup" information, even if there isn't an error.

Wouldn't a better solution be to have a flag that goes to the `LSPClient` that enables or disables it?
author: kitsonk

body:
> Are we really sure we want to do this... All "diagnostic" output of a test goes to stderr, including error messages. This means that every lsp test will output some 10 lines or so of "setup" information, even if there isn't an error.
> 
> Wouldn't a better solution be to have a flag that goes to the `LSPClient` that enables or disables it?

That's a valid point, I just run the tests locally and indeed there's a lot of output being printed. To be honest it's quite surprising to me, I though that this output would be caught by the test harness and only printed on test failures.
author: bartlomieju

body:
> To be honest it's quite surprising to me, I though that this output would be caught by the test harness and only printed on test failures.

That would make more sense, but personally I don't know how to accomplish that.  The `LSPClient` would hold onto the `stderr` and make it available, but I don't know how to automatically expose it on an assertion failure.  I suspect some sort of "test macro" could work, where if it panics, it dumps the `stderr`.

That being said the "problem" was that there were errors in the logs that were not impacting the outcomes of the tests, which meant in theory the tests weren't actually testing what we thought they were testing.  That wasn't the case in the end, but, the point is valid... always swallowing the `stderr` isn't a good idea, sometimes it should be inspected.

It should improve the responsiveness though (well, I mean 200ms to 8ms is not really an improvement practically in an editor setting). It just doesn’t do it all independently at the moment so if there is a long type check after debouncing then it could delay lint diagnostics still. I guess I could spend a bit more time making them all completely independent as it wouldn’t be that much extra effort (I think a few hours… I’ll do it tomorrow). That said, this could probably be merged anyway if it’s desired to be in the release.

Edit: actually, I just got this working, but I want to take a look more at this `DiagnosticsCollection`. I'll do it tomorrow.

Edit 2: This is now done.

body: I had objections to the platform-dependent signal list we were using in the past, I think this is fine.
author: piscisaureus

body: Is there any reason you're not using the standard EventTarget but rather a custom implementation here?
author: benjamingr

body:
@benjamingr
The section 2.8 of DOM spec (ref https://dom.spec.whatwg.org/#observing-event-listeners) says:

> In general, developers do not expect the presence of an event listener to be observable. The impact of an event listener is determined by its callback. That is, a developer adding a no-op event listener would not expect it to have any side effects.

So `addEventListener` shouldn't have any side effects, but we can't implement signal handlers in that way (Listening to signals remove the default behavior of getting that signal). We think implementing signal handler like
```
window.addEventListener("SIGINT", handler);
```
will break/reduce the conformance to the web spec. So we chose this non-standard name

More context: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/11158#issuecomment-871186326

body:
There is a change in behaviour in `contains_specifier()`, specifically in calling `get()` that isn't ever needed by the `op_exists`, which is why I broke it out.  `op_exists` really only looks for local files on disk, mostly probing.  Therefore it abandons the locking of the `file_system_docs` and the calculation of the "freshness" of the document before returning a result.  It simply determines if the file exists on the local file system (or is open in the editor).  "op_exists" and therefore "exists()" are not called by tsc when resolving modules, it is only for probing the filesystem for things like the existance of package.json and other environmental files which might affect its module resolution, which we override anyways.  Very seldom does the file even exist and even more rarely the file is read.

While the other two locations where we use `contains_specifier()`, there is dependence on the "freshness" of the file and likelihood the file will will be otherwise accessed in subsequent calls.

We can make the comments more clear, but I think if we change `contains_specifier()` to the same logic, we will end up with some esoteric edge cases... `exists()` is really only a low level optimisation of `op_exists`, because when opening a file now in 4.5, tsc will issue ~1000 op_exists requests for files that almost never exist in a Deno context and locking the `file_system_docs` just to find out the file doesn't exist is quite unnecessary.
author: kitsonk

body:
> We can make the comments more clear, but I think if we change contains_specifier() to the same logic, we will end up with some esoteric edge cases... exists() is really only a low level optimisation of op_exists, because when opening a file now in 4.5, tsc will issue ~1000 op_exists requests for files that almost never exist in a Deno context and locking the file_system_docs just to find out the file doesn't exist is quite unnecessary.

@kitsonk I see.

> While the other two locations where we use contains_specifier(), there is dependence on the "freshness" of the file and likelihood the file will will be otherwise accessed in subsequent calls.

This is ok. The `file_system_docs` collection is intended to just help prevent loading the same document multiple times across the `Documents` collection and snapshots. Actually, looking at the code for file_system_docs there seems to be a bug in its logic in one place... I've fixed that in this PR.

Also, I renamed the method to `exists` as that seems to be how it's used in all cases.

body: @bartlomieju ok, I've got a few more challenges to fix, but there is one that I see what the problem is, but I am not totally sure why it is occurring now and the best way to fix it.  If you run test `native_modules_as_global_vars` you will get a panic that the file being passed is not local.  If you look at the stack trace, when the graph is being built, the node resolver is getting a request to resolve a remote URL and then is "blowing up".  I believe the remote URL is being injected to the graph as part of the injected modules, but I am not totally sure why when building the graph, these injected dependencies weren't resolved before and why this isn't working now, nor why the compat code doesn't handle resolution of these "gracefully".
author: kitsonk

author: kitsonk

body:
> @bartlomieju ok, I've got a few more challenges to fix, but there is one that I see what the problem is, but I am not totally sure why it is occurring now and the best way to fix it. If you run test `native_modules_as_global_vars` you will get a panic that the file being passed is not local. If you look at the stack trace, when the graph is being built, the node resolver is getting a request to resolve a remote URL and then is "blowing up". I believe the remote URL is being injected to the graph as part of the injected modules, but I am not totally sure why when building the graph, these injected dependencies weren't resolved before and why this isn't working now, nor why the compat code doesn't handle resolution of these "gracefully".

@kitsonk there is a single path that injects resolved specifier for built-in Node modules (pointing to `https://deno.land/std/node`). Unfortunately I made a mistake when mapping these specifiers to `ResolveResult`. I now fixed it and the tests should pass. I think I will need to reorganize this code further when working on the CJS/ESM integration, because it is indeed unfortunate that there a single "special" code path that needs to handle `http`/`https` imports inside that resolution algorithm. Most likely it would be removed in the future if we decided to move `std/node` into an extension crate.

body:
Hey @lucacasonato, thanks for taking the time to explain 🙌 . I'm sorry I've written such a long explanation for the misunderstanding 😅 

With regards to the problem you describe, I strongly agree with your assessment here:

> It would be confusing if async and sync environments behaved differently in this regard to termination.

Considering we'd like the above to be true, I think indeed the only choices would be to (1) either make individual tests the runner's unit of execution or simply (2) not to run the rest of the tests in a file if one of them times out.

IMO (2) could be a bit frustrating as that's different from most runners out there and could make debugging/fixing take longer. On the other hand, what you said about executing the global scope only once per module being less confusing also makes a lot of sense. Personally, I'm slightly more inclined towards (1), but I think your opinion here would be much more valuable as you're more well-informed given you've been much more involved with the project.

What's your preference here?

Do you have any thoughts on what should be Deno's philosophy with regards to testing? I think this might be a case of thinking whether we'd like to give users control and be more hands-off (1) or take control and prevent mistakes ourselves(2).

Please let me know what you think and how you'd like to proceed in terms of creating separate issues, discussing on Discord, or this PR etc...

Thank you!
author: lucasfcosta

> @tracker1 I can't be sure, but seems feasible, given that our tests use `sqlite3` module.

Seems reasonable... it's one of the few areas that I wish the in the box experience worked a bit better.  I work with SQLite, PostgreSQL and MS-SQL quite a bit... SQLite isn't covered well, and MS-SQL isn't covered at all... so it's been harder to advocate for using it with some infrastructure bits.

Of course MS-SQL (npm: mssql) isn't a native module, but the layers of modules needed, and a few areas of the node compatibility interfaces still missing are a separate issue.

body:
I wanted to try this branch out on lmdb-js. Everything built fine, but whenever I attempt to load a NAPI binary using `Deno.core.opSync('op_napi_open', 'test.node')` I get `LoadLibraryExW failed` (running on Windows). This is the case even with a .cpp file with bare minimum NAPI_MODULE_INIT function that returns undefined (a napi_value set to undefined). The only thing I could do to get by this error was explicitly define an extern exported `napi_register_module_v1` function, but I couldn't really get it to interact with the exports, and I don't think a NAPI module is supposed to directly use that function. So I guess I have few questions:

- Should this be working Windows yet?
- Should it work to compile my C/C++ code with node-gyp and run on Deno? (or are there ABI incompatibilities?)
- Should I still expect NAPI_MODULE_INIT (or NODE_API_MODULE with node-addon-api) to be the main entry?
- Is there any other debugging that can be done to determine what is wrong with `LoadLibraryExW` failed? I did notice that it takes about one second for it to fail; not sure if that long of time to failure indicates a timeout somewhere (waiting for a callback?)
- Could you point me to any working examples of NAPI module that is working with this branch?

Anyway, thank you for your work on this, looking forward to trying this out.
author: kriszyp

body:
hi @kriszyp 👋  thanks for looking into it

I'm getting similar error on macOS with lmdb 2.3.0-alpha.1. It seems fail to instantly for me with `dyld[9373]: missing symbol called`. This PR does not implement weak references yet so there is a chance I forgot to define a stub symbol.

> The only thing I could do to get by this error was explicitly define an extern exported napi_register_module_v1 function, but I couldn't really get it to interact with the exports, and I don't think a NAPI module is supposed to directly use that function

Oh, IIRC `napi_register_module_v1` is the _new way_ to define an entry point for NAPI modules, it's used in [napi-rs](https://napi.rs/) and [neon](https://neon-bindings.com/). The other way is `napi_module_register` (which I think the `NAPI_MODULE_INIT` macro generates for you in C/C++)

> Should this be working Windows yet?

yup. It should - I haven't tested on Windows since the last rebase :-)

> Should it work to compile my C/C++ code with node-gyp and run on Deno? (or are there ABI incompatibilities?)

everything should work as compiled for Node.

> Should I still expect NAPI_MODULE_INIT (or NODE_API_MODULE with node-addon-api) to be the main entry?

both should work. The current implementation tries to look for `napi_register_module_v1` and falls back to the `NAPI_MODULE_INIT` way.

> Is there any other debugging that can be done to determine what is wrong with LoadLibraryExW failed? I did notice that it takes about one second for it to fail; not sure if that long of time to failure indicates a timeout somewhere (waiting for a callback?)

Hmm, I'm not near a Windows machine atm. (to debug, I just attach `lldb` and let it crash, then move up the frames). Will take a look at the issue.

> Could you point me to any working examples of NAPI module that is working with this branch?

`test_napi/` has unit tests and `test_napi/third_party_tests/test.js` has some real world tests. Run `yarn` in `test_napi/third_party_tests` and  then `cargo test -p test_napi`

body:
One other issue I noticed is how the `process.versions.modules` is reported in Deno's `compat` mode. I recognize that Deno is trying to closely match Node's behavior with the `process.versions` object and the versions reported, which is fine and good, but I don't think it should extend to `process.versions.modules` since this is specifically reporting the ABI version of the NAN/V8 interface, which Deno specifically does _not_ export or support. When I use prebuild/node-gyp-build to load the binary, this looks to determine if a binary compiled for a specific Node ABI can be used, and if `process.versions.modules` is "93" (as reported in Deno), it will load the NAN binary, but this is where we want the loader to be aware that NAN is _not_ available, and to fall back to the NAPI binary (`process.versions.napi` is properly reported as "8", which is a bit of a fib in the main branch, but significantly more truthful in this branch). I'd suggest removing `process.versions.modules` or making it null or empty or something.

BTW, are there built binaries outputted/stored from any of the builds done on this branch  that I can access or should I wait for the experimental release? (I have tried building this branch locally on 2 windows machines, one mac, and one linux and they have all failed to build or run the unit tests. But I can certainly wait for a working build if it is coming).

I don't think this is going to make it for 1.19. There are 2 hours left before `denoland/deno` freezes, and I'd really like to have the ref handling work correctly before we merge this. Also we need to figure out how this integrates with test sanitizer.

I think it would make more sense if we don't actually start the timer until the first listener is registered. We should also cancel the timer once the last listener unregisters.

Let's get this landed for 1.20 :-)
author: lucacasonato
andreubotella

body: Could be solved with a macrotask callback I think.
author: lucacasonato

body:
> Could be solved with a macrotask callback I think.

Okay, so let's say that someone checks if the signal has aborted, and the timeout has indeed passed. Do we queue a macrotask now? Because if it has expired a while ago and the event loop hasn't been blocked during that time, the signal should have aborted by the time we're checking. (The spec technically would allow for this, but it's far from ideal.)
author: andreubotella

body: I'm not sure that an implementation that only has an active timer while there are listeners is workable, so I've implemented this with `Deno.unrefTimer()` and added tests. I guess that idea could still be pursued after this implementation is merged.

body:
> This is looking great! @kitsonk what do you think about adding an option to disable this behaviour to a `Deno.serveHttp` options bag?

I thought about it when I paired with @ry and he argued against it.  It does add complexity to it. Is there a compelling case where someone would want to disable it, other than "because they can"?  Doesn't setting `Cache-Control: no-transform` effectively provide that functionality without adding to the API surface?  That is how CFW supports disabling it.
author: kitsonk

body: I could imagine that one has an nginx in front of Deno that is responsible for on the fly encoding. In that case you want Deno to leave the responses as "pure" as possible. I don't think we per-se _need_ to add this for the PR to land, but I think it would be useful to give users an opt-out. The implementation complexity seems rather trivial.
author: lucacasonato

body:
> Also do we need to have special handling for `Range`?

Actually, after doing a bit of research, if a `Content-Range` header is present in the response, we should disable automatic compression, as the user HTTP server has processed a range request, with the range being based off the uncompressed range headers it negotiated.

body:
> Do you think it is better to have different functions use_color and is_tty and check directly in the main? Or do you think it is better to add a new variable in bootstrap options called is_tty?

I think we need both. First check `IS_TTY` and store its value (you already did it), and expose it as `is_tty` function. The main module checks `is_tty` function and pass it as bootstrap option next to noColor option (You need to extend `BootstrapOptions` struct). Finally set the no color option of console module to `noColor || !isTty` in `runtime/js/99_main.js` script ref: https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/4bea1d06c7ddb177ed20e0f32b70d7ff889871ab/runtime/js/99_main.js#L583

Apparently, Rust's libstd is built against a GLIBC with `copy_file_range`. We must use the `copy_file_range` syscall on older GLIBC, it's supported on Linux 4.5 and above.

body:
> > Thank you @bartlomieju, it seems fine now. Is it better to also add test for the described behavior in Windows?
> 
> That would be great! I'll try to get this landed for 1.20

Test for Windows behavior added and CI seem fine. I leave here a quick reference about the tests implemented:

- `netTcpReuseAddr` opens a socket using the default setting for **SO_REUSEADDR** of each OS, then the socket is closed, 
putting it in the **TIME_WAIT** state; a second socket is successfully opened
- `netTcpNoReuseAddr` performs the same steps of the previous test but **SO_REUSEADDR** is set to `false`, in order to test the behavior in OS different from Windows. In this case the test has to fail with an `AddrInUse` error. This test can fail in two point, proving the same property, the first point being the first listener creation (it can fail since sockets from previous tests that occupied the required port are in the **TIME_WAIT** state) while the second point is the creation of the second listener (since the first one is in the **TIME_WAIT** state)
- `netTcpTrueReuseAddrWindows` set **SO_REUSEADDR** to `true` and is run only in Windows. In this case two listeners are bound to the same port, which is usually forbidden for the default setting of **SO_REUSEADDR** equal to `false`

The tests `listenTlsWithReuseAddr`, `listenTlsWithNoReuseAddr` and `listenTlsWithTrueReuseAddrWindows` follow the same 
logic but are for `listenTls`

body:
The changes introduced up to now concern only TCP sockets. With the last two commits I have extended the possibility of using the **SO_REUSEADDR** even for UDP sockets. Since they have a different behavior compared to TCP sockets, I have also updated the docstring of the **reuseAddress** optional argument. This argument now can be specified through `Deno.listenDatagram`. In addition to that, 2 new test have been added: `netUdpReuseAddr` and `netUdpNoReuseAddr`. 

To enable this option, the Socket2 crate has been used, using the same settings of the [std::net](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/6343edfa11c80ffed02de7e059178d4c1637ecdb/library/std/src/sys_common/net.rs) implementation of `UdpSocket`.

Similarly to node ([win udp](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/e46c680bf2b211bbd52cf959ca17ee98c7f657f5/deps/uv/src/win/udp.c), [unix udp](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/e46c680bf2b211bbd52cf959ca17ee98c7f657f5/deps/uv/src/unix/udp.c)), the options always defaults to `false`, and for both Windows and Linux setting **SO_REUSEADDR** actually uses that option under the hood. For the other *nix systems **SO_REUSEPORT** is actually used.

For the other *nix systems **SO_REUSEPORT** is actually used.

author: bartlomieju

body:
I have rebased this PR.

I have removed the `reuseAddress` option for TCP sockets. For TCP, this option is unconfigurably `true` on all platforms but Windows. This behaviour matches Node.js and Go. Neither of these systems support disabling `SO_REUSEADDR` on socket listeners.

For UDP, `reuseAddress` is supported. It has the same port stealing behaviour on all systems we support, but to achieve this it uses different syscalls on different platforms. macOS and other BSDs use `SO_REUSEADDR`, while Linux and Windows use `SO_REUSEPORT`. This behaviour matches libuv.

As a follow up PR we can add a `reusePort` option to enable the kernel connection load  balancing behaviour found in Linux, for TCP sockets.

I think this PR is now good to go.
author: lucacasonato

body:
For `Deno.core.refOp` and `unrefOp`, currently if something other than a `PromiseId` is passed, the Rust binding never gets called because that id is not in the promise map. However, in this PR it's directly passed to the binding, which tries to convert it into a `PromiseId`, and throws if it can't.

Combined with changing `PromiseId` to be a type alias of `u32` rather than `i32`, this seems to break the following test: https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/cli/tests/unit/ref_unref_test.ts

Edit: Turns out this is not why the test was breaking. It seems like any numbers passed to `Deno.core.{un,}refOp` –whether they fit in `u32`, `i64` or even if they're infinities or `NaN`– will not throw, but they will get cast in perhaps weird ways. If this is an issue, the arg could be cast into a `v8::Uint32` instead.

body: My original idea was to take the `JoinHandle`s and collect them in a per-isolate vec so they can all be joined after the event loop finishes (since at this point all of these workers will have closed or been terminated, after dropping the `WorkerThread` struct). However, simply ignoring the `JoinHandle`s seems to work just fine. This works even in cases where a panic happens in the worker thread, because the panic handler will kill the process, so there's no need to propagate the panic to the main thread. But I can change it back to that original idea if you think that's better.

body:
Fails the following WPT tests:

```
  "/fetch/api/response/response-error.any.html - Throws RangeError when responseInit's status is 100"
  "/fetch/api/response/response-error.any.html - Throws RangeError when responseInit's status is 199"      
  "/fetch/api/response/response-error.any.worker.html - Throws RangeError when responseInit's status is 100"
  "/fetch/api/response/response-error.any.worker.html - Throws RangeError when responseInit's status is 199"
```

If we cared for marketing reasons to pass as much WPT tests as possible, then we could just allow status code 101 or 101-198.

I think there's also a better argument to be made for allowing *only* 101:
* status codes 100/102/103 are useless in our current model, because these status codes indicate that the server is going to send *another* response *in addition to* the 1xx response, but in our API there is no support for sending multiple `Response`s for a single `Request`.
* status codes 104-199 don't exist
author: piscisaureus

body:
I would like to challenge this PR:

Imagine an FFI library that has been loaded for the purpose of using a single API call. That API call will return a function pointer that should be later called periodically.

To guard the safety of the environment running this code, the library user revokes the FFI permission after loading the library. This way no malicious foreign code (dependency or other) can use the FFI permission to load eg. their custom DLL payload (if they had rw rights to some folder) or just load some well-known DLL that offers more APIs they need to further their attack.

Now, if the FFI permission is needed to call the received function pointer, it means that the permission cannot be revoked and still keep the code running. Unfortunately this same issue currently applies to `UnsafePointerView` reads as well, which are likely to be necessary for some FFI work.

I feel that the FFI permission may need to be more granular (load vs use), or otherwise somehow rethought to allow "load, revoke, use" sort of flow.
author: aapoalas

body: I think that security model is pretty hard to implement in reality, loading a library vs using pointer APIs are both very unsafe on their own. Even if we are to add it, that would be for another PR.

body:
@littledivy 
> structs-by-value are not an FFI "primitive", instead just a bunch of neatly arranged bytes.

Yep, I could've used a name like `bytes` but decided to match with libffi which calls them `structure`.

Actually this also allows to pass arrays by value (ex. `[u32; 6]`). Should we add a more specific API for those, such as `{ array: { length: 6, type: "u32" } }`, or just encourage using `{ struct: ["u32", "u32", "u32", "u32", "u32", "u32"] }`?

> This passes the ownership of the value to the ffi function. The array buffer backing memory owned by JS is always deallocated by Rust?

~~Hmm did not think of this. I'll test this, and if that is the case we can clone the data.~~
Edit: No, it does not deallocate the backing memory. I was able to use the buffer just fine after it returned from an FFI call that used it as a struct. Added a test as well.

And yes, this is an already "solved" problem using pointers. But that solution only works for native libraries that _you_ write, not when using system libraries or existing ones. As I have mentioned before, I'm trying to use Objective C runtime library which exposes C API to interface with ObjC classes. Some methods of those classes take/return structs by value instead of pointers. One example of such API is [NSWindow's `initWithContentRect:styleMask:backing:defer:`](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/appkit/nswindow/1419477-initwithcontentrect?language=objc). Here the `contentRect` argument takes an `NSRect` struct by value that basically consists of 4 doubles. So yes, this is a blocker, at least for my project.

Generally, I avoid writing wrappers for system libraries to add "workarounds" since they add more friction in usage of a module that makes use of such wrappers - distributing (cross platform) binaries of that wrapper, caching the library.
author: DjDeveloperr

body: Why is the `fastci` failing? It looks unrelated to the changes

body:
> Why is the `fastci` failing? It looks unrelated to the changes

It's failing when compiling the `FfiLoadArgs` struct which internally uses `NativeType`. This was added to `NativeType` in this PR:
```rs
NativeType::Struct(fields) => libffi::middle::Type::structure(
  fields.iter().map(|field| field.clone().into()),
),
```
Something in `fastci` doesn't like that, I guess?
author: aapoalas

body: Is it really that? This PR builds fine for me, and even tests pass (on Linux). Could it be a [compiler bug as the logs say](https://github.com/denoland/deno/runs/5671688027?check_suite_focus=true#step:25:136)?
author: DjDeveloperr

body:
It's a rustc ICE - #13935 

A workaround is to update the CI cache keys. https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/.github/workflows/ci.yml#L239

body:
> Edit: No, it does not deallocate the backing memory. I was able to use the buffer just fine after it returned from an FFI call that used it as a struct. Added a test as well.

@DjDeveloperr That is not true. It does deallocate the struct - V8 now holds the pointer to freed memory, accessing it causes immediate UB. You are just lucky that in the test freed memory is not overridden by future allocations.

body: @iugo Does rf mean 'refactor'? BTW this doesn't change runtime. `fetch(new URL(...))` still works. This just shows deprecation info in editors
author: kt3k

body:
> @iugo Does rf mean 'refactor'? BTW this doesn't change runtime. `fetch(new URL(...))` still works. This just shows deprecation info in editors

Although not a design refactoring of whatwg.fetch, this should be a design refactoring of deno ext/fetch.

Yes, just deprecate instead of remove helped us a lot. We'll adjust the code accordingly soon.

Also, to follow the whatwg standard more strictly, the first argument to fetch should start with " "about", "blob", "data", "file", "http" or "https". https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#url
author: iugo

body:
> Also, to follow the whatwg standard more strictly, the first argument to fetch should start with " "about", "blob", "data", "file", "http" or "https". https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#url

That might make sense, but probably we don't want to follow that in typing because we don't want to diverge our `fetch` typing from `lib.dom`'s typing (which is maintained by TypeScript team)

Some applications use `lib.dom` instead of `lib.deno.ns`. If we have 2 different typings of `fetch` in those 2 type libs, that will cause the confusion in the ecosystem, which we'd like to avoid
author: kt3k

body: I think this is looking great now. I think the place you slotted in the retrieval of `explicit_terminate_exception` is pretty good. I have pushed one little change that renames some stuff, and changes the isolate state slot to store the raw exception rather than a `JsError`. That last thing aligns this just a tad bit more to the regular error handling paths.
author: lucacasonato

body:
> That last thing aligns this just a tad bit more to the regular error handling paths.

I wanted to avoid the `cancel_terminate_execution(); re_terminate_execution()` in this code path because I didn't know what consequences it would have. Looks like it _sometimes_ causes us to re-enter `exception_to_err_result` and get a generic termination error.

> changes the isolate state slot to store the raw exception rather than a `JsError`

I tried that previously and got a cryptic panic at the `JsError::from_v8_exception()`. I assumed it had to do with the different scopes or something.
author: nayeemrmn

body:
> I wanted to avoid the cancel_terminate_execution(); re_terminate_execution() in this code path because I didn't know what consequences it would have. Looks like it sometimes causes us to re-enter exception_to_err_result and get a generic termination error.

That is the strangest thing! I just tried it locally, and indeed! How strange! I will debug this further. It is very strange.
author: lucacasonato

body:
I have figured it out:

- the first invocation of `exception_to_err_result` happens in `mod_evaluate`.
- the second invocation of `exception_to_err_result` happens in `JsRuntime::poll_event_loop` (called by `JsRuntime::run_event_loop`).
- `mod_evaluate` is called in parallel with `JsRuntime::run_event_loop`.
- Because of poll order, the error from `JsRuntime::run_event_loop` can sometimes be returned instead of the one from `mod_evaluate()`.

I am not sure if the current code in this PR is susceptible to this issue too.
author: lucacasonato
lucacasonato

body: @lucacasonato https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14159/commits/4e8df5c3e3470a5da67fe244fdf015529e377046 most likely removes any non-deterministic behaviour that isn't warranted (no IO etc). Without that you sometimes get a single `poll_event_loop()` call even though the module evaluated synchronously.

body: Can we assert that `Vec<u8>` and `SmallVec<[u8; 16]>` have the same size?
author: andreubotella

body:
> Can we assert that Vec<u8> and SmallVec<[u8; 16]> have the same size?

`assert_eq!(sizeof::<Vec<u8>>(), sizeof::<SmallVec<[u8; 16]>>())`
author: lucacasonato

body:
> > Can we assert that Vec and SmallVec<[u8; 16]> have the same size?
> 
> `assert_eq!(sizeof::<Vec<u8>>(), sizeof::<SmallVec<[u8; 16]>>())`

They only have the same size when using `smallvec`'s [`features = ["union"]`](https://docs.rs/smallvec/latest/smallvec/#union)

> When the union feature is enabled smallvec will track its state (inline or spilled) without the use of an enum tag, reducing the size of the smallvec by one machine word. This means that there is potentially no space overhead compared to Vec. **Note that smallvec can still be larger than Vec if the inline buffer is larger than two machine words.**

This isn't a safety or correctness guarantee, just a matter of mem efficiency that's not super obscure and documented by `smallvec`

We aren't technically truncating but saturating, which was our previous behaviour except it's now consistent/correct and tested.

So this is already a good improvement over the present with no breaking change in intended behaviour. Erroring on OOB numbers would be sane IMO but we should discuss that as part of a broader topic of serde_v8/op-layer strictness.

body:
The last 6 errors are all due to this panic. 
```
thread 'worker-0' panicked at 'Must not use a decoder that has finished.',
encoding_rs-0.8.29/src/lib.rs:4203:43
```
Basically, it happens when a decoder wants to query a worst-case UTF-16 length in order for the dst buffer to allocate the required space.

Its state is DecoderLifeCycle::Finished when performing the query which causes the panic. 
```rust
DecoderLifeCycle::Finished => panic!("Must not use a decoder that has finished."),
```
I am still in the process of trying to isolate the problem.

body:
I have implemented the first three requested changes.
1) Flag is now named --eval-file.
2) Multiple files are now supported. I was not sure what syntax to use, so I copied the argument parsing code for allow-lists (e.g. deno repl --eval-file=./a.js,http://localhost:8000/b.js,http://localhost:8000/c.js). Note that equal sign is now required.
3) tools::repl::run takes a ProcState, which has a FileFetcher as member. I am using this to load scripts. It handles both local and remote scripts. It also respects --reload. Due to our use of deno_core::resolve_import, local script paths now need to start with ./, ../, or /.

I will look into type-stripping and tests tomorrow. Let me know if you have any feedback on the above.
author: najumancheril

body:
@bartlomieju and @dsherret, I don't think I need to make any additional changes to deno in order to transpile .ts script files passed via --eval-file into JavaScript. The REPL entry point that I am using to inject the script code (ReplSession.evaluate_line_and_get_output) already does TypeScript transpilation. Here is the sequence of events:

1. tools::repl::run loads each eval file's source code (JavaScript or TypeScript code) as a String and invokes ReplSession.evaluate_line_and_get_output on that code String
2. evaluate_line_and_get_output invokes ReplSession.evaluate_line_with_object_wrapping on the code
3. evaluate_line_with_object_wrapping invokes ReplSession.evaluate_ts_expression on the code
4. evaluate_ts_expression creates a ParsedSource object with the code and invokes .transpile(). **This does the TypeScript to JavaScript conversion.**
6. evaluate_ts_expression invokes ReplSession.evaluate_expression on the transpiled code,.

Does that explanation make sense? If so, then I will proceed with adding the integration tests.
author: najumancheril

body:
> @bartlomieju and @dsherret, I don't think I need to make any additional changes to deno in order to transpile .ts script files passed via --eval-file into JavaScript. The REPL entry point that I am using to inject the script code (ReplSession.evaluate_line_and_get_output) already does TypeScript transpilation. Here is the sequence of events:
> 
> 1. tools::repl::run loads each eval file's source code (JavaScript or TypeScript code) as a String and invokes ReplSession.evaluate_line_and_get_output on that code String
> 2. evaluate_line_and_get_output invokes ReplSession.evaluate_line_with_object_wrapping on the code
> 3. evaluate_line_with_object_wrapping invokes ReplSession.evaluate_ts_expression on the code
> 4. evaluate_ts_expression creates a ParsedSource object with the code and invokes .transpile(). **This does the TypeScript to JavaScript conversion.**
> 5. evaluate_ts_expression invokes ReplSession.evaluate_expression on the transpiled code,.

body:
I played around and here's some feedback.

Given this file:
```ts
my_repl.ts
export function foo() {
    return "foo";
}

export class Bar {
    #bar;
}
```

I first try running like this:
```
❯ cargo run -- repl --eval-file=my_repl.ts
    Finished dev [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 0.18s
     Running `target/debug/deno repl --eval-file=my_repl.ts`
error in --eval-file file my_repl.ts. Relative import path "my_repl.ts" not prefixed with / or ./ or ../ from "<unknown>"
Deno 1.20.5
exit using ctrl+d or close()
> close()

```

As can be seen above it results in an error. I think we should resolve passed arguments relative to CWD if they don't start with `./`, `../`, `/` or URL scheme (`file://`, `http://`, etc).

Fixing my mistake from above:
```
❯ cargo run -- repl --eval-file=./my_repl.ts
    Finished dev [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 0.17s
     Running `target/debug/deno repl --eval-file=./my_repl.ts`
Deno 1.20.5
exit using ctrl+d or close()
> foo()
"foo"
> Bar
[Function: Bar]
>
```
Works nicely, though I suspect some users will be surprised that these files are executed as plain old scripts in the same context (ie. errors will be thrown on `import ... from ...` statements). That said, this is quite a powerful feature that will allows people to build custom REPLs more easily. 

Nice work @najumancheril!
author: bartlomieju

body:
Some feedback:

The `$name\n== output ==\n$output\n== output end ==\n$status ($time ms)\n` formatting seems weird to me. I think we should do something more like Rust does, where we capture and buffer by default, unless the test fails (in which case we dump the output with the error messages). A user could disable this behavior with a `--no-capture` flag or something (just like libtest in Rust).

The `running 3 tests from b_test.ts` messages are still very useless when running with `-j` flag. In parallel mode each test needs to be prefixed by test name, or test completions per file need to be buffered.

I don't like the many colors in the final output. I think there should only be colors for the FAILED / PASS label, not for the number of failures and passes.

---

Overall I think this takes some good inspiration from Vitest, but I think we should be more liberal with adopting some of their nice test output. I think for example that the extra left indentation and line numbers on the per test failure report is very nice:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7829205/163690875-226d7b05-b7df-4e31-a339-695ffa896e7b.png)

---

I think we should also really consider more "radical" changes to the test runner output. For example I think including test names for each file by default is really not that important. If we instead had an "interactive mode" version of this output that showed the test file name, and then a live updating count of tests already run / left to run that'd be great IMO. That would also work out much better with the `-j` flag.

body: This can be used to leak heap contents into the isolate. As such I am not in favour. If we want to do I/O reads using non zeroed buffers then create an op that takes a length to read and does the allocation and reading inside of that op. You can then return the clean buffer to the runtime.

body:
> @bcheidemann Thanks for the PR. I see this fixes the issue, but ideal fix would be like the below in my view:
> 
> ```js
> Deno.inspect([], { compact: false, trailingComma: true });
> // [
> //   
> // ]
> ```
> 
> (This has less effect to the existing code. Also this will produce better diff when comparing, for example, `[]` with `[1]` in snapshot testing)
> 
> What do you think?

I can see the benefit of this from both perspectives - better snapshot diffs and minimising impact on existing code.

However, I would suggest that the following would make more sense.

```js
Deno.inspect([], { compact: false, trailingComma: true });
// [
// ]
```

This way the diff would be:

```
Expected + / Actual -

   [
-    3
   ]
```

Instead of:

```
Expected + / Actual -

   [
+
-    3
   ]
```

Let me know which you would prefer @kt3k - happy to implement either 🙂

author: bartlomieju

body:
> That PR removed promiseIdSymbol and made it so a promise's ID isn't accessible to JS. This, however, makes Deno.core.{un,}refOp useless, since it takes a promise ID. In this PR, calling an async op returns a [promise, id] tuple, and Deno.core.opAsync sets that ID on the promise object with promiseIdSymbol.

Does that mean that `Deno.core.(un)refOp()` doesn't work anymore?
author: bartlomieju

body:
> Does that mean that `Deno.core.(un)refOp()` doesn't work anymore?

With @AaronO's PR, the promise ID's weren't exposed to JS at all, so there was no way to use `Deno.core.{,un}refOp()`, since they take the promise ID. This PR instead passes both the created promise and the new promise ID to the JS side, so the ID can still be exposed with the `promiseIdSymbol` symbol.

Edit: Updated the OP to make this clearer.
author: andreubotella

body:
This PR is slower than main, I feel we should optimize this to be on par with the JS promise ring before landing.

`main`:
```
cargo bench -p deno_bench_util --bench op_baseline

test bench_op_async   ... bench:     258,880 ns/iter (+/- 4,305)
```

This patch:
```
cargo bench -p deno_bench_util --bench op_baseline

test bench_op_async   ... bench:     322,266 ns/iter (+/- 5,645)
```
author: littledivy

body: We should be able to avoid `promiseIdSymbol` in JS altogether with embedder internal fields. See https://github.com/denoland/rusty_v8/pull/967
author: littledivy

body:
> We should be able to avoid `promiseIdSymbol` in JS altogether with embedder internal fields. See [denoland/rusty_v8#967](https://github.com/denoland/rusty_v8/pull/967)

Ideally an op's `v8_func` would return a promise with the embedder field set. However, currently we handle result unwrapping and op call tracing by creating new promises with `Promise.prototype.then` and similar in JS. In order to use this, we'd need a binding to set the returned promise's embedder field, which doesn't sound like it'd be much of an improvement, performance-wise.

It doesn't seem like op call tracing would be that hard to move to Rust. `unwrapOpResult`, though, would be a problem. I'll experiment with exporting `unwrapOpResult` and calling it from Rust, though.
author: andreubotella

body: This PR gets us some of the way towards supporting async ops in realms, but it would still need additional changes, some of which are already implemented in the alternative PR #14734. Considering that, and the performance loss, I think it's better to close this PR and focus on the other one.
author: andreubotella

body:
@dsherret With this change, is it no longer possible to utilize the first element of `Deno.args` for any other value than `"--"` in a script that is run as a task command?

If that is the case, can you provide some clarity on why this change was requested and implemented?

Here's an example to demonstrate my inquiry:

`./log_args.ts`:
```ts
console.log(Deno.args);

```

`./deno.jsonc`:
```jsonc
{
  "tasks": {
    "log_args": "deno run log_args.ts"
  }
}

```

Console:
```
% deno --version
deno 1.21.1 (release, x86_64-apple-darwin)
v8 10.0.139.17
typescript 4.6.2

% deno run log_args.ts
[]

% deno run log_args.ts --
[ "--" ]

% deno run log_args.ts -1
[ "-1" ]

% deno task log_args
Warning deno task is unstable and may drastically change in the future
Task log_args deno run log_args.ts
[]

% deno task log_args --
Warning deno task is unstable and may drastically change in the future
Task log_args deno run log_args.ts
[]

% deno task log_args -- -1
Warning deno task is unstable and may drastically change in the future
Task log_args deno run log_args.ts "--" "-1"
[ "--", "-1" ]

% deno task log_args -1   
error: Found argument '-1' which wasn't expected, or isn't valid in this context

	If you tried to supply `-1` as a value rather than a flag, use `-- -1`

USAGE:
    deno task [OPTIONS] [ARGS]

For more information try --help

```

Observations:

1.
    - command:`deno run log_args.ts`
    - result: `[]`
    - note: Expected.

2.
    - command:`deno run log_args.ts --`
    - result: `[ "--" ]`
    - note: Expected.

3.
    - command:`deno run log_args.ts -1`
    - result: `[ "-1" ]`
    - note: Expected.

4.
    - command:`deno task log_args`
    - result: `[]`
    - note: Expected.

5.
    - command:`deno task log_args --`
    - result: `[]`
    - note: Surprised. I expected to see `"--"` based on this change.

6.
    - command:`deno task log_args -- -1`
    - result: `[ "--", "-1" ]`
    - note: Expected based on the description of this change.

7.
    - command:`deno task log_args -1`
    - result: Error
    - note: Not surprised because I understand why, but I don't understand why the args can't be handled the same way that they are in `deno run`: where the double hyphen is not necessary because order is important, and flags to `deno task` must be provided _before_ the task name.
author: jsejcksn

body:
@jsejcksn
> With this change, is it no longer possible to utilize the first element of Deno.args for any other value than "--" in a script that is run as a task command?

Right, we can't pass the 1st arg to the internal script, and that looks like a bug to me.

I think we shouldn't parse anything in `<task_args>` and just pass everything to the internal command (like `yarn` does)

> deno task log_args -1

I think this should invoke `deno run log_args.ts -1` instead of throwing an error

What do you think? @dsherret @bartlomieju
author: kt3k

body: In additional testing, this change also appears to break results from the [`std/flags`](https://deno.land/std@0.137.0/flags) parser module. 😕
author: jsejcksn

body: It's correct that it's forwarding `--` now, but it should allow running `deno run log_args.ts -1`

@dsherret I agree with that, and nothing in my comment was specifically about `emit` or unstable APIs: rather it was just about factors to consider when making any breaking changes. When considering a "small number of projects", one must consider not just direct dependents but **all dependents of dependents recursively**, which might be _very_ different than the former.

body: I think it is important for #13452 to be completed before this lands. It is core to Deno's security model that code is run in a sandbox with only explicitly granted permissions, and if config files are automatically detected (with no way of disabling that behavior), and config files include permissions, then that means that programs will be run with implicit permissions (with no way of disabling that). ⚠️
author: jsejcksn

body:
> I think it is important for #13452 to be completed before this lands. It is core to Deno's security model that code is run in a sandbox with only explicitly granted permissions, and if config files are automatically detected (with no way of disabling that behavior), and config files include permissions, then that means that programs will be run with implicit permissions (with no way of disabling that). ⚠️

@jsejcksn opened https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14555

body:
> the usecase for this is when you need to start a process in a tty other than the currently running tty. Instead of output being sent to the main processes stdout and stderr it is sent to the PTY's stdout and stderr. And it won't receive any input unless you specifically write to the PTY. This can allow you to run two child processes at once in the same Deno session, showing two outputs, switching between which process get data from stdin. You can send the WINCH signal to resize the amount of rows and columns the PTY has and so on. With this you can essentially make tmux on Deno
https://discord.com/channels/684898665143206084/688040863313428503/973217262913867786

body:
@AaronO Please don't mark PRs as pure "cleanup" if they rewrite code to cause behavioral changes. Some examples:

- much more loose about invalid headers
- compresses irregardless of if `content-encoding` is already set on the response
- handles invalid UTF8 vary headers differently
- has an early return **true** inside of the `should_cache` function when a header is invalid

At least half of this should have been caught in review.

Please revert this PR, or fix the above issues.
author: lucacasonato

body:
> * compresses irregardless of if `content-encoding` is already set on the response

My bad, submitted a regression fix here https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14641

> much more loose about invalid headers

Could you elaborate ?

> handles invalid UTF8 vary headers differently

Not sure this is true, logic is effectively (which I believe is preserved in both cases):
```rs
vary =
  | utf8 && lowecase -> add prefix,
  | utf8 && !lowercase -> untouched,
  | _ -> "Accept-Encoding",
```

> has an early return true inside of the should_cache function when a header is invalid

I believe this behaviour is also unchanged. We're ultimately impacting the predicate of compressing based on headers, which is negatively controlled by `cache_control_no_transform`:

```rs
cache_control_prevents_compressing =
  | utf8 && Some(c) => c.no_transform,
  | utf8 && None => false,
  | !utf8 => true,
  | _ -> false,
  
headers_allow_compression = !cache_control_prevents_compressing && ...;
```
author: AaronO

body:
> has an early return true inside of the should_cache function when a header is invalid

I mean this early return. If the `cache-control` header is invalid UTF-8, we compress irregardless of if `content-range` and friends are set. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14552/files#diff-90dfe8e7faf01dad4a43d5f274862d01e5c6c53c07ec35ec28f6d73189b2f52fR659

> Not sure this is true, logic is effectively (which I believe is preserved in both cases):

Sorry, you're right I misread the code here.


> > much more loose about invalid headers
>
> Could you elaborate ?

I mean this `filter_map` here. We just skip over invalid header names and values now, rather than propagating the error of the header being invalid like we did previously. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14552/files#diff-90dfe8e7faf01dad4a43d5f274862d01e5c6c53c07ec35ec28f6d73189b2f52fR507-R513
author: lucacasonato

body:
> I mean this early return. If the `cache-control` header is invalid UTF-8, we compress irregardless of if `content-range` and friends are set. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14552/files#diff-90dfe8e7faf01dad4a43d5f274862d01e5c6c53c07ec35ec28f6d73189b2f52fR659

The early return is in an inner function not the outer, will clarify these predicates in a follow-up PR so they're easier to grok

> I mean this filter_map here. We just skip over invalid header names and values now, rather than propagating the error of the header being invalid like we did previously. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14552/files#diff-90dfe8e7faf01dad4a43d5f274862d01e5c6c53c07ec35ec28f6d73189b2f52fR507-R513

Discarding invalid headers could indeed be fair, but I'll revert back to erroring on invalid headers

body:
> Please add a unit test that exercises this change (connecting to an ip address)

Not sure if I understood the idea correctly, since it seems that `--unsafely-ignore-certificate-errors` option is being tested apart from common SSL unit tests which are located in TS-files. However, I added integration test of IP-address unsafe connectability. Will it be enough?
author: albnnc

body:
Eventually, I added [these lines](https://github.com/seanmonstar/reqwest/blob/d24a5286c7bd101f82c90e39cb2ccc21ae854da8/src/tls.rs#L405) from reqwest to handle a few more cert errors when unsafe option presented.
 
I think we can't map webpki errors, since they are getting [mapped](https://github.com/rustls/rustls/blob/4fc3a3af8cadd4d414560f79b3428c6c93aa11eb/rustls/src/verify.rs#L579) by rustls anyway and, as you said in Discord, seem to occur only if we skipped validation for invalid cert previously.

I failed to add ipv6 tests, since reqwest seems to fail such requests.

<details>
<summary>Example</summary>

Example uses `danger_accept_invalid_certs`, since it's the default way to make completely unsafe client. I think we won't be able to handle things that couldn't be handled with that option. Not without upstream fix at least. 

```rust
use reqwest::blocking::Client;
use reqwest::Method;

fn main() {
  let client = Client::builder()
    .danger_accept_invalid_certs(true)
    .build()
    .unwrap();
  let resp = client
    .request(Method::GET, "https://[::1]:3000")
    .send()
    .unwrap();
  println!("resp {:?}", resp.status());
}
```

- The code listed above fails with error.
- Target address (`https://[::1]:3000`) exists and available in browser and postman.
- The same code with request to `https://localhost:3000`, `https://127.0.0.1:3000` and `http://[::1]:3000` works. 

The trace is

```
thread 'main' panicked at 'called `Result::unwrap()` on an `Err` value: reqwest::Error { kind: Request, url: Url { scheme: "https", cannot_be_a_base: false, username: "", password: None, host: Some(Ipv6(::1)), port: Some(3000), path: "/", query: None, fragment: None }, source: hyper::Error(Connect, Custom { kind: Other, error: "invalid dnsname" }) }', main.rs:12:6
```

</details>

I think that for now we can just handle ipv4 addresses – this is by far the most common case and it works only with unsafe cli option anyway. Are we going to be happy with that?
author: albnnc

body:
> I think we can't map webpki errors, ... [and] as you said in Discord, seem to occur only if we skipped validation for invalid cert previously.

That's not quite what I said. There are two types of relevant WebPKI errors here: ones resulting from invalid signature verification (these are **not** OK to ignore), and ones resulting from invalid certificates (these are OK to ignore).

I think we should selectively ignore `webpki::Error::BadDER` derived errors, or all errors if the "unsafe" flag was passed with no arguments.
author: lucacasonato

body:
> That's not quite what I said. There are two types of relevant WebPKI errors here: ones resulting from invalid signature verification (these are not OK to ignore), and ones resulting from invalid certificates (these are OK to ignore).

Misunderstood, sorry. Now I hopefully got it.

> I think we should selectively ignore webpki::Error::BadDER derived errors, or all errors if the "unsafe" flag was passed with no arguments.

I've updated PR.
author: albnnc

author: lucacasonato

body: This is very unfortunate. SQLite is a fundamental piece of technology that is useful in almost every software. @lucacasonato  Instead of asking for use-cases (there are too many and it is pointless to list them all), what are the reasons to don't have it in core? Using a third party module adds friction (which one? will it be maintained in the long run? it is optimal or a subpar WASM version?) that should be avoided.
author: mikrosystheme

body:
@lucacasonato, of course, user-land modules are a viable option, but I agree with @mikrosystheme observations.

Landing SQLite would allow fast prototyping in Deno with a database out of the box; if/when the prototype _grows_, you then can change the db with a more scalable solution. Same idea behind including TypeScript in the runtime (JS -> TS).
Then, with Jupyter integration, it would help answer the question _"why not Python?"_, which is always hard to answer.

But then again, I suppose it is not sustainable to add everything shiny into Deno. 😅 It is just that seeing this PR growing got me excited about the possibilities that Deno could provide with _just_ a single executable.

To answer your question, I have no solid use case that third-party modules cannot solve. But it would be a tangible improvement to DX, and more solid ground to push for Deno adoption.
author: fdionisi

body:
> > Could this be re-considered? 
> 
> It's was the third time we had reconsidered this feature in 6 months 😅
> 
> What is your use-case that requires this to be built in, instead of using a user-land module?

Well basically the out of the box feature is something that most people will like. I know there are third party modules that would solve the issue but they will not be as performant as a native built in integration. Also there is a library that uses SQLite bindings using FFI that I think is pretty cool but unfortunately cannot be used in Deno deploy. So having the SQLite built in Deno would be useful for both fast prototyping and secure ACID compliant operations.

> We discussed this PR during latest design meeting. The team believes that it should be an async API, ie. the actual API call should happen in side a threadpool.
> 
> There's also a question regarding return value, @cmorten can you give some context why you went with this interface? Is it required for Node compatbility?

Agree, think should mirror the interface of resolveDns if implemented, be async etc.

Please regard this PR as more of a PoC than anything, wouldn’t expect this to be the final implementation - it was just to add some colour to the issue (find easier to critique when there’s something to contrast against!). Sadly not quite there with rust to be able to offer the best / most advised implementation 😅

Primary reason for suggestion is for Node compat (e.g. https://nodejs.org/api/dns.html#dnslookupserviceaddress-port-callback, covered in the associated issue). If team feel it’s not a priority / something worth covering in compat then happy to close the issue out 🙂
author: cmorten

body: This is currently blocked by a failing WPT (webidl/ecmascript-binding/global-immutable-prototype.any.html) that modifies the `window` object's prototype. This causes the brand checking added in this PR to fail.
author: cjihrig

body:
> This is currently blocked by a failing WPT (webidl/ecmascript-binding/global-immutable-prototype.any.html) that modifies the window object's prototype.

Oh, that's tricky..

I can think of 2 ways to fix it:
- make global prototype chain immutable (as tested in that test case)
- store global prototype chain somewhere and restore them before `Deno.exit` is called in `tools/wpt/testharnessreport.js`
author: kt3k

body: I opened https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14659 to try freezing the prototype chain. I didn't want to bake the fix into `tools/wpt/testharnessreport.js` because the problem would still exist elsewhere.

Since we might not be able to freeze the `globalThis` prototype anytime real soon, we addressed the issue with the test harness. Does this still look good to you?

Do we need to follow it? cc @lucacasonato @bartlomieju

body:
> * The format for declaring function type parameters has two objects. I'd like to turn this into a tagged object eg. `{ "type": "function", "parameters": [], "result": "void" }` but didn't find a way to serde work with me on that.

Did `#[serde(tag = "type")]` not work?
author: piscisaureus

body:
> Did `#[serde(tag = "type")]` not work?

I'll have to recheck, but when I tried originall serde did not like mixing untagged (plain strings) and internally tagged (`tag = "type"`) enum members, or so it seemed.
author: aapoalas

body:
> Did `#[serde(tag = "type")]` not work?

Okay, I tested this and indeed there doesn't seem to be a clear way to do this.

We'd need the same enum to handle both of these: `"u8"` and `{ "function": { "parameters": [...], "result": "..." } }` or `{ "type": "function", "parameters": [...], "result": "..." }`. If `serde(tag = "type")` is used then it would imply that `U8` should be presented in JSON as `{ type: "u8" }`. On the other hand, If we want `{ type: "function", parameters: [...], result: "..." }` to get parsed then that would imply the `serde(tag = "type")` setting.

So, these two obviously do not coexist. Or at least I am not aware of a way for the two to coexist. Luckily there's also a benefit for the current predicament. `Function {}` requires the JSON format to be `{ "function": { ... } }`, where we of course use the `{ ... }` to be `{ "parameters": [...], "result": "..." }`. That object format then happens to exactly align with the declaration that we expect `UnsafeCallback` to be given, meaning that if so wanted FFI users can reuse the same objects for both callback parameter declaration AND the `UnsafeCallback` declaration that will eventually get passed as the parameter.
author: aapoalas

body: Yeah, cannot mix different tag representations in serde.
author: littledivy

body: Even for the API, mixing `"u8"` and `{ type: "function", ... }` doesn't seem desirable at all, since it's an inconsistent tagging scheme. That would be the third best option after (1) what we have now aligning with serde's defaults and (2) internally tagged as `{ type: "u8" }` + `{ type: "function", ... }`.
author: nayeemrmn

body:
> Even for the API, mixing `"u8"` and `{ type: "function", ... }` doesn't seem desirable at all, since it's an inconsistent tagging scheme. That would be the third best option after (1) what we have now aligning with serde's defaults and (2) internally tagged as `{ type: "u8" }` + `{ type: "function", ... }`.

Yeah, I originally wanted to avoid the extra object needed for `{ function: {} }`, but now I'm very happy with it, since as said it allows the inner object to be reused as-is for both the parameter and the `UnsafeCallback`'s definition.

body: @lucacasonato I think it's worth it to do this at the source because then we don't need to deal with this encoding detail anywhere else for the most part and we do this before it gets put in an `Arc<str>` so we reduce cloning. I think once we have the source text in an `Arc<str>` then we shouldn't touch it anymore to reduce memory usage since files can be quite large.
author: dsherret

body: We can't do this in the CLI source fetcher though, as we don't know at this point if a given asset is WASM or JS/JSON. We don't want to strip BOM out of WASM, as that isn't UTF8
author: lucacasonato

body:
> We can't do this in the CLI source fetcher though, as we don't know at this point if a given asset is WASM or JS/JSON. We don't want to strip BOM out of WASM, as that isn't UTF8

@lucacasonato all Wasm files start with a module which has a four byte magic value that's `\0asm` so I believe we are ok doing this.

https://webassembly.github.io/spec/core/binary/modules.html#binary-module

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1609021/169522158-66101afa-32e0-47dd-86a5-1ec10f7e3d9d.png)
author: dsherret

body: I think we'll have to update the code in deno graph to specify if it's loading binary data or utf8 data and have it up to the `Loader` implementation to convert to utf8 w/o BOM. That way we don't duplicate files in memory, which could be expensive (ex. this caches files in memory if I'm reading it correctly).
author: dsherret

body: We're going to explore an alternate solution in deno_graph.

body:
> Hi! We have some interest in this feature. Can we help with it somehow?

Hi. Sorry for the late reply. I'm currently working on this on my spare time, and I've been a bit busy for the last two weeks.

I wonder what is driving your interest in this PR. Is it having `ShadowRealm` available in Deno, or are you users of `deno_core` and want to be able to use contexts for some reason? In either case, there is still some work needed before contexts in `deno_core` are completely usable, unless you don't care at all about making ESM imports possible outside the main realm (see denoland/deno_core#911). And I guess some of the bullet points in that issue might be done in parallel by someone else.
author: andreubotella

body:
> I wonder what is driving your interest in this PR. Is it having `ShadowRealm` available in Deno, or are you users of `deno_core` and want to be able to use contexts for some reason? In either case, there is still some work needed before contexts in `deno_core` are completely usable, unless you don't care at all about making ESM imports possible outside the main realm (see denoland/deno_core#911). And I guess some of the bullet points in that issue might be done in parallel by someone else.

Hi, Andreu. We are users of `deno_core` and would like to execute code concurrently in the same `Isolate` without sharing the same global object between executions. ESM imports aren't necessary for our use case. We can use `deno_core` as it is today, but it would be nice to use contexts to further insulate the executions without using an `Isolate`.  

As you, I have been busy. I wanted to try out your PR and write some tests, but didn't have time to do it yet.
author: lmalheiro

body:
This approach won't work. We can only serialize things that are `v8::Value` using `serde_v8` and `v8::Module` is `v8::Data` and not `v8::Value`. 

Other approach to explore is to manually serialize `ModuleMapInner` into a V8 object - since all `v8::Value` are also `v8::Data` we could then embed it into the snapshot using `SnapshotCreator::AddData` method. This will require some manual labor for serialization, but seems doable. The disadvantage is that when creating a snapshot from an existing snapshot all embedded data is lost, so we'll need to figure out how to make it work with #14744

author: justinmchase

body:
> A couple questions:
> 
> 1. Suppose I have some native modules, perhaps with threading, will they be stopped and freed appropriately when the core is shutdown too?

No, that would require https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/13093

> 2. Can I get the `isFileWatcher` variable as code? Such as `Deno.isFileWatcher` ?

I asked some team members about it and there's no support for adding such API at the moment. What's your use case for the API? Mine was to automatically enable client side HMR if we're running in watch mode.

> 3. Will the process itself still respond to signals such as SIGINT correctly and exit as normal? This will not affect that code?

The process will, but since the V8 isolate is terminated your JS code for handling these things will not work anymore.

> 4. Can I also intercept the exit function? It would be useful in tests to be able to call exit without it stopping all the tests.

You'd have to manually overwrite `Deno.exit` function. Actually `deno test` will not allow you to call `Deno.exit()` - it will instead throw an error from "exit sanitizer".
author: bartlomieju

body:
> What's your use case for the API?

I'm just writing tests for some CLI code, and in some places `Deno.exit(1)` is called, so just trying to test that functionality with `Deno.test`.

I thought if I could detect that it was running in watch mode then I could simply not call exit and have it do something else instead. I could of course abstract that call and inject the exit functionality but it would likely be easier to just not actually call exit.

I'm hoping that `Deno.exit(1)` isn't actually just throwing an Error now? That may affect my tests... It looked like the error throwing was happening internally somewhere and being handled by the runtime, this will not manifest as a throw in my test code will it?
author: justinmchase

body:
> I'm hoping that Deno.exit(1) isn't actually just throwing an Error now? That may affect my tests... It looked like the error throwing was happening internally somewhere and being handled by the runtime, this will not manifest as a throw in my test code will it?

It is, if you try to `Deno.exit()` inside a `Deno.test()` you will get an error like this:
```
exit(0) => ./test/exit_sanitizer.ts:[WILDCARD]
error: AssertionError: Test case attempted to exit with exit code: 0
  Deno.exit(0);
       ^
    at ....
```
author: bartlomieju
justinmchase

body:
> Hmm, that's a little confusing but I guess so long as its deterministic, so long as that error is one that I can catch and detect in the test then I can add an expectation that it throws an ExitError or whatever its called. How would it look to write such a test?

No, it doesn't throw an error that you can catch - if you call `Deno.exit()` inside a test it will be caught by exit sanitizer (https://deno.land/manual/testing/sanitizers#exit-sanitizer) - it's an internal helper in `deno test` that prevents accidental calls to `Deno.exit()` that might lead to false positives (imagine a test case calls `Deno.exit(0)` somewhere, even though you had a failure in previous test case, on CI that would case the pipeline to pass when it shouldn't). 

Since you are writing a CLI I suggest to spawn subprocesses using (`Deno.run` or `Deno.spawn` API), in the test case to check for proper behavior and exit codes. If you need to check for output you might use [snapshots](https://deno.land/manual/testing/snapshot_testing) to verify that output matches your expectations.
author: bartlomieju

body:
> cc @bartlomieju This runtime state cache extends beyond caching across frame lookups for a single stack, and we don't need to accept a list of locations in op_apply_source_map. ([from discord](https://discord.com/channels/684898665143206084/684911491035430919/983518187570229369))

I was thinking about that, when reading through source code. On one hand that seems like a no-brainer, but on the other these sources will be cached indefinitely in memory (ie. until the program exits). I'm not sure what will be the memory impact, but we should be careful about that. Additionally it appears we are doing a lot of copying when acquiring the source map:
https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/94068b7109af64eba2cb9fd299e62705a5358069/cli/proc_state.rs#L697-L703

@dsherret @kitsonk @lucacasonato do you have thoughts on the topic?
author: bartlomieju

body:
> I'm not sure what will be the memory impact, but we should be careful about that.

We already cache all sources in memory, this makes it also cache the source map for the subset of those which are referenced in any stack trace. I think programs have a finite and small amount of unique rendered stack traces. With the source line cache I made sure to move the 150 character cap to core so we don't cache anything ridiculous like from minified code.
author: nayeemrmn

body:
> > I'm not sure what will be the memory impact, but we should be careful about that.
> 
> We already cache all sources in memory, this makes it also cache the source map for the subset of those which are referenced in any stack trace. I think programs have a finite and small amount of unique rendered stack traces. With the source line cache I made sure to move the 150 character cap to core so we don't cache anything ridiculous like from minified code.

Do we actually cache source maps in memory? Could you point me to relevant code?
author: bartlomieju

body:
> Do we actually cache source maps in memory?

We cache _sources_ already, I'm saying adding the source map for a small (and ~constant wrt program size IMO) subset of those wouldn't be a big deal

body:
> > @cd-work please sign the CLA
