> Will do as soon as I get the okay.
> 
> Would you mind if I add the following patch to this PR, rather than opening a new one? It fits somewhat well because it's also a documentation/example improvement, but it's otherwise completely unrelated.
> 
> ```diff
> diff --git a/runtime/examples/hello_runtime.rs b/runtime/examples/hello_runtime.rs
> index 07e42f0ff..1ce7cde4b 100644
> --- a/runtime/examples/hello_runtime.rs
> +++ b/runtime/examples/hello_runtime.rs
> @@ -70,7 +70,5 @@ async fn main() -> Result<(), AnyError> {
>      permissions,
>      options,
>    );
> -  worker.execute_main_module(&main_module).await?;
> -  worker.run_event_loop(false).await?;
> -  Ok(())
> +  worker.execute_main_module(&main_module).await
>  }
> ```
> 
> The patched code should be more idiomatic as far as I can tell, since `execute_main_module` already calls `run_event_loop` internally, thus doing it twice is unnecessary. I'm not 100% certain on this though, because I'm not sure if there's any reason to call `run_event_loop` when module loading failed before execution was possible.

This is actually correct - `Worker::evaluate_module` returns result when the last top-level promise resolves, but there can be more asynchronous work to do (eg. code in the module started timeouts) that will be done only if event loop is run.
author: bartlomieju

body:
> This is actually correct - Worker::evaluate_module returns result when the last top-level promise resolves, but there can be more asynchronous work to do (eg. code in the module started timeouts) that will be done only if event loop is run.

Good thing I asked, that sounds interesting. By "top-level promise" I'd assume you mean that once the module itself is executed and awaited, `evaluate_module` will return, but `setTimeout(..., "1000")` inside that main module code might cause future side-effects that require running the event loop again until everything is done?

And I'd assume the `evaluate_module` function itself doesn't just await both because a user might actually want to run more stuff and it's unnecessary to await all futures when the goal is to just start running another module?

author: nayeemrmn

body:
One question that I thought of and tested that _locally_ this caused no issues is the response `sync_channel`. Currently I create it with a bound of 1, meaning that if somehow theoretically the isolate thread manages to call the `do_ffi_callback` and then send a response before the "async" thread calls `response_receiver.recv().unwrap()` then the response can be buffered.

This seems unnecessary, as the `sync_channel` receiver side is literally this:
```rs
async_work_sender.unbounded_send(fut).unwrap(); // Send the message
response_receiver.recv().unwrap(); // Block on a response
```
The isolate thread would need to be lightning fast to poll the async work channel and do the work, and the calling thread would need to be absolutely massively busy to not get to call `.recv()` before the response sent. This does not seem to be a particularly realistic case.

As such the bound could perhaps be lowered to 0, making the channel a "rendezvous channel" which brings with it some perf optimisations I believe.

Opinions?

EDIT: I indeed changed this to a rendezvous channel with blocking on both ends for the other side to become available. This means that even if the receiving thread is very fast and the sending thread is very busy, they'll wait for each other on the channel and pass the response directly without using the buffer.

I hit an issue trying https://github.com/deepkit/deepkit-framework/tree/master/packages/type-compiler, which is based on 4.7.4. author: DjDeveloperr

body:
> How does ownership of the buffer work? Are we copying the buffer? 

When passing buffer as struct argument, we directly pass pointer from backing store. Libffi doesn't  seem to take any ownership of it.
Buffer is probably internally copied only once, like pushed onto stack at time of call or something according to ABI.

See: https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15060#discussion_r1009281028

When returning struct we pre allocate buffer in JS side and pass its pointer to libffi so return value can be written to it.
author: DjDeveloperr

body:
> Buffer is probably internally copied only once, like pushed onto stack at time of call or something according to ABI.

Ah ok that makes sense. libffi pushes the struct onto the stack.

What happens when structs are too big? On aarch64, pass-by-value structs large enough are actually passed by reference. 

From https://devblogs.microsoft.com/oldnewthing/20220823-00/?p=107041 "The AArch64 processor (aka arm64), part 20: The classic calling convention"

> If a parameter is a large structure (larger than 16 bytes), then it is passed by address. Small structures are passed by value, packed into integer registers. (Execption: If the small structure consists entirely of floats or entirely of doubles, then it is passed in floating point registers, one for each member.)
author: littledivy

body:
> What happens when structs are too big? On aarch64, pass-by-value structs large enough are actually passed by reference.

I'm not sure how libffi handles this internally, but it would be pretty weird (and problematic) if it is taking ownership of arguments only in certain cases.

body:
> I want to land this, let's get the CI green. @DjDeveloperr Do you need help with something?

Passing structs in nonblocking calls doesn't seem to work, I couldn't find the reason though. In normal sync calls, struct is passed correctly by value but in nonblocking calls the struct value is actually a pointer to another pointer of struct's memory, which is weird because both type of calls use same function for parsing struct arguments.

After some debugging, found that when we pass the same struct buffer as argument to both sync & async calls, literally same libffi `Arg` structs are created (which is expected). But I don't get why at the time of call the value is becoming like that only in async calls.

Also, the windows fastci is failing for unknown reason...

EDIT: Commented out the test case for it. CI is green now but it's still a bug. Seems windows fastci had seg fault because of this (unlike on Linux where I tested, it had no segmentation fault).

Perhaps realpathing should be handled more like redirects as well in being the response URL not the request URL, to get even more alignment with the web, despite the pains of that in double instancing. I can possibly try to push those two changes through Node.js process.

body:
@dsherret I remembered why I wanted to do this, last time it came up was discussing #14632. We could fix shortly fix that just by including the check scope in the config digest. But looking at the cache validation code I strongly think we need better separation between things that bust the emit cache, when a type check needs to be reperformed and tracking if the source has actually changed. As described in https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15090#discussion_r915235504. One of the reasons is that invalid emits from changed sources need to be checked for each module in the graph uniformly, whereas checked state can only correctly be stored at the roots. Another is that some files aren't emitted at all but changes need to be tracked only for type checking.

There have always been odd cache validation bugs and I attribute it to the current structure which conflates invalid emits and yet-to-be-performed type checks and tries to store it all in one version hash. But again I can make a better case and implementation for this _after_ checking and emitting are properly decoupled with swc. Closing for now.
author: nayeemrmn

body: @nayeemrmn yeah, I think decoupling the two would be really good. I was planning on starting work tomorrow on #13302. Maybe at the same time I will look a bit at decoupling it.
author: dsherret

body: I looked into this today and the type checking cache seems like it won't be invalidated in more scenarios than mentioned in #14632. I'm updating the code to cache a hash of all the inputs per successful type check which is only cleared when changing CLI versions, so it will solve this issue too.

author: bartlomieju

body:
> Sure, anything that exercises this code path is good +1

I added two tests that verify that, with the current changes, the order of logging is as one might expect.

Once the current round of tests is done I'll push a change to remove the microtask execution order to show that the order of logging becomes unexpected, and revert it later.

EDIT: Here's the unexpected logging order failure:
```
---- event_loop_integration stdout ----
ExitStatus(unix_wait_status(0))
thread 'event_loop_integration' panicked at 'assertion failed: `(left == right)`
  left: `"SYNCHRONOUS\nSync\nSTORED_FUNCTION called\nAsync\nTimeout\nTHREAD SAFE\nSync\nAsync\nSTORED_FUNCTION called\nTimeout\n"`,
 right: `"SYNCHRONOUS\nSync\nSTORED_FUNCTION called\nAsync\nTimeout\nTHREAD SAFE\nSync\nSTORED_FUNCTION called\nAsync\nTimeout\n"`', test_ffi/tests/integration_tests.rs:193:3
note: run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable to display a backtrace


failures:
    event_loop_integration
```
So, as can be teased out from the output, we expect (right) the order of logs to be:
> THREAD SAFE
> Sync
> STORED_FUNCTION called
> Async
> Timeout

but it's actually (left)

> THREAD SAFE
> Sync
> Async
> STORED_FUNCTION called
> Timeout

So we can indeed see that the `Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log("Async"))` call gets resolved **before** the callback returns. This can be quite unexpected.

body:
> Won't this cause a problem that if one worker writes to this buffer the other will see the changes?

Turning a V8 `ArrayBuffer` or `TypedArray` into a serde_v8 `DetachedBuffer` will detach the original `ArrayBuffer`, so that trying to read from it will fail, so there's no shared memory. This is fine because for the `postMessage` method in workers and `MessagePort`, the buffer is created in internal runtime code and is never used again after sending it.

This is not the case for `BroadcastChannel`, which is why I'm leaving that for a separate PR.

author: ghost

body:
> If the first parameter is now the receiver, does this mean that C functions with empty parameters would now supported?

0-ary C functions were already supported. eg. Here're a few lines from benchmarks I ran on a PR of mine which changes nothing related to normal FFI calls:
```
benchmark                              time (avg)             (min … max)       p75       p99      p995
------------------------------------------------------------------------- -----------------------------
nop()                                3.56 ns/iter    (3.45 ns … 19.23 ns)    3.5 ns   4.37 ns   4.75 ns
add_u32()                            7.91 ns/iter    (7.17 ns … 18.92 ns)   7.64 ns  13.32 ns  15.29 ns
nop_u8()                             7.88 ns/iter     (7.4 ns … 34.05 ns)    7.4 ns  12.53 ns  16.59 ns
```

This just changes how those calls are called: Currently they're given an extra parameter that they do not expect but luckily also do not care about.

body:
Should I add offset parameters to these APIs? I think they'd be useless but it'd then align with the existing APIs.

Reasons for uselessness:
1. `getCString`: I would presume any char pointer with a prefixed header would be quite uncommon. It could be handled using `getArrayBuffer` for the header + subarray to the end of the header, to `UnsafePointer.of()` + `getCString`.
2. `getArrayBuffer` / `copyInto`: Any offset can be handled with a subarray.

body:
@dsherret I figured out where the race condition was, it was this code: https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/fad8d7d32a08095c82af39f77e3280c52a7ab2ae/cli/tools/test.rs#L1157 which wrongly assumes the registration events for the relevant tests have been _received_, when it is only guaranteed that they have been sent. So sometimes those tests wouldn't be present in `tests` yet.

I've moved that behaviour to the receiving side where it belongs. Now it can be reliably checked that the sending code has no dependency on the receiver state.

body:
Regarding the `op_func.call(...args)` usage: Would the normal calling convention of `ops.op_func(...args)` work if the `ops` object was created as an `External` from the `OpCtx`? (IIRC it's that pointer that we need for ops, right?) ie. Does the "this" object matter for Fast API data receiving?

I would presume it does not matter and I remember reading something about how there's a "Holder" object for Fast API functions which is not the same thing as the "this" argument which is sometimes called "holder" in V8 code. Anyway, there are two possibilities that I can see:
1. Either the `this` argument is relevant, in which case `ops` should be created as a `v8::External` to retain the `ops.op_func(...args)` calling convention.
2. Or (more likely) the `this` argument is irrelevant, in which case I would presume that Fast Calls get the `data` object anyway, and the `ops.op_func.call(...args)` calling convention is unnecessary.

@divy I presume you've tested that the `.call()` method usage is truly necessary? How hard would it be to test the `ops` as `v8::External` case?

body:
Before merging this PR, a decision must be taken (and documented) on the open question described in the PR summary:
>V8 does not currently follow this Apple's policy, and instead aligns all arguments to 8 Byte boundaries.
A decision needs to be taken:
> 1. leave it broken and wait for v8 to fix the bug
> 2. Adapt to v8 bug and follow its ABI instead of Apple's. When V8 fixes the implementation, we'll have to fix it here as well

Discussion in https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15305#discussion_r963002793.

body: @piscisaureus could you take a look? I discussed this PR with Luca and we're mostly happy with it, the only question is if it's guaranteed on Windows that PIDs of subprocesses will not be recycled during the program run (ie. if you spawn a subprocess that has PID X and the subprocess finishes, can system recycle that PID and use it in other subprocess spawned by entirely different process). It appears this is true on Linux and Mac, but we're unsure about Windows.
author: bartlomieju

body:
> ie. if you spawn a subprocess that has PID X and the subprocess finishes, can system recycle that PID and use it in other subprocess spawned by entirely different process

I think this is an extreme edge case that affects the window between the subprocess finishing and the `.wait().await` in rust being resolved and continued after. If in that window the pid is already reallocated to some other process and `child.kill()` in JS is called, that other process will be killed instead.
author: nayeemrmn

body:
> @piscisaureus could you take a look? I discussed this PR with Luca and we're mostly happy with it, the only question is if it's guaranteed on Windows that PIDs of subprocesses will not be recycled during the program run (ie. if you spawn a subprocess that has PID X and the subprocess finishes, can system recycle that PID and use it in other subprocess spawned by entirely different process). It appears this is true on Linux and Mac, but we're unsure about Windows.

I don't think that guarantee exists on any platform.
author: piscisaureus

body: For both platforms, the pid is freed up for reuse at the end of `tokio::process::Child::wait()` where implicitly the os handle to the subprocess is dropped/reaped. Ideally we could delay that until _we_ drop the tokio child from the resource table (currently there is a `.await` in between) but this is the best we can do with the API. It's better than the current code right?
author: nayeemrmn

body: What if we try_wait on the child just prior to doing the actual killing? If that returns Ok() we don't perform the kill. Due to process zombies on Unix, this should be a non-racy fix (because the pid is only reaped after a successful wait/try_wait (and async wait can't complete in parallel with the killing).
author: lucacasonato

body:
> What if we try_wait on the child just prior to doing the actual killing? If that returns Ok() we don't perform the kill.

The `Child` refcell is already borrowed by `op_spawn_wait()` so we can't access it there. That's why I had to copy the pid out from the refcell.

body:
Sure, for example, somebody wants to make some analytics on top of coming requests to the server using the referrer values something like:

**Internal**
Internal referrers occur when a visitor navigates between two pages of the same domain. Example: http://site.com/ => http://site.com/about

**Link**
If there is a referrer present but it's unrecognized above, we'll just call it a link referrer.

**Direct**
When a visitor navigates to a site by typing in the url into the address bar, document.referrer is blank.

Developers can do so right now by getting the header value, but if we have the value, it's not hard to implement it as the spec says for the [Request WEB API](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Request/referrer).
`Request.referrer`
Maybe some libraries are relying on that.

Using this example, the developers might want to test their tool using Deno by setting the needed referrer value. By spec, they can do it like:
`fetch('http://site.com/about', { referrer: 'http://site.com/', referrerPolicy: 'unsafe-url' });`

Or some web scraper functionality might be wanting to set the referrer to get different page and so different data if sites are determining what to serve by the referrer value.

But I got your point that other than 'unsafe-url' for the ReferrerPolicy, there potentially are no other use cases. Maybe it's better to get rid of the determination logic and just allow to set the referrer.

What do you think?
author: sevenwithawp

body: I think users could already set the referrer by setting the appropriate header, so even unsafe-url may be slightly misguided.
author: lucacasonato

body: Fair enough. So what about adding `referrer` and `referrerPolicy` getters to the `Request` class due to the spec and allowing setting `referrer` from `fetch` options(also due to the spec) and removing the referrerPolicy logic?

body:
Feel free to ignore/dismiss/hide this suggestion if it's off topic.  I don't use deno personally outside of some play projects, so I don't have a huge stake in this really, but I think this direction is cool, and have a bit of experience in this space.

One thing occurs to me, though, you mentioned that the dependency resolver isn't complete, doesn't support peer dep resolution, etc.  Have you considered the approach of porting @npmcli/arborist to rust?

It'd be a relatively straightforward way to ensure that you are working with the same approach that npm uses, which for better or worse, _has_ had a ton of play-testing, reflecting well over a decade of focused time and energy.  So, like, even the less "elegant" ways it approaches some things got to be that way by modeling how users actually expect it to work (which is, quite often, completely irrational and inelegant!  But humans gonna human.)

Plus, it seems like there's a super good chance that a rust implementation of Arborist would be significantly faster and more memory efficient, and perhaps even be a way to speed up npm (that is, if it replaced the JS version entirely or in part).  And even taking it on and failing would be a really great way to get fresh eyes on a lot of what it does, some of which quite frankly has probably never really gotten a thorough review.  Probably a lot more work, but the result could be amazing for the whole JS community, and it'd be a very impressive project.

If you do decide dig into it and have questions (or just complaints lol), I'd be happy to provide answers if I can.
author: isaacs

body: Hey @isaacs, thanks for the suggestion! We're planning on improving the approach to align more with npm's resolution in the future, but for this experiment we just want something quick to try it out. What we're building overall, doesn't exactly align with how npm works (more details in the coming weeks) and so I don't believe a straight port of arborist would work in this case, though it would be beneficial to look at the code to align the resolution more. With our upcomming approach, there will be packages that won't work, so we want to get an idea of just how incompatible it is (that said, I would expect it to be a lot more compatible than Deno currently is). If we find it doesn't work then we can pivot.
author: dsherret

body: Ah, I should have probably specified, I'm only suggesting the abstract tree building/resolution aspects of arborist might benefit from being ported for deno's use. The reification, packing, etc is probably mostly inapplicable.
author: isaacs

body: @isaacs yeah, definitely! We’d like to align the resolution as much as possible. We’re just going to get the high level pieces in place first, evaluate the approach, then start diving into these details. Probably won’t be for a few weeks though.
author: dsherret

body:
> Probably won’t be for a few weeks though.

No rush.  Given my pace of OSS engagement lately, "a few weeks" may as well be "right now" 😅

body: If you have any questions about how npm does any of the other stuff, though, or just run into sticky puzzling situations, I'm happy to help if I can.

body: Thanks! We'll definitely keep that in mind.

body:
> Is the current API similar to Node's API?

Node's API is implemented in C++ [here](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/325ad3b875eec665a28333a42f24ffe6d84544e8/src/async_wrap.cc#L185-L193). That code calls [this code](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/325ad3b875eec665a28333a42f24ffe6d84544e8/src/env.cc#L68-L84).

So it seems to be different from Node's, although this is also not a public API in Node.
author: cjihrig

body:
This is intended as an internal low-level API on top of which a proper public API can be later constructed.

Node.js has the `async_hooks` API that is somewhat akin to this one, but Node also tracks async work scheduled from IO events. This module is marked as "experimental" and [its future is uncertain](https://github.com/nodejs/diagnostics/issues/437).

There is also the `async_context` API that is stable and more high-level. It defines the `AsyncLocalStorage` and `AsyncResource` classes. Some equivalent to those could be defined on Deno's stdlib.

---

This PR provides `setPromiseHooks`, which is useful on itself and enables tracking promises. I believe some effort will be required to also be able to track other callbacks such as `setTimeout`.

body:
The only thing that still isn't obvious to me is why the events in the test are different from the ones in #8209, even though the test code appears to be the same. Did something change in V8, or was the original implementation buggy?

In #8209, the first few events are:

init
init
resolve
init
init
init <-- this is a before event in this PR
author: cjihrig

body:
When a promise first begins to resolve it will also trigger the creation of the promise related to the `then` block of other promises anywhere in the code that are ready to resolve. My explanation is that there are some promises from the test runner runtime that get a chance to run at that point and are noticed by the test itself.

Previously there was some number of init events. With a more recent version of Deno something in the test utils changed and this number increased.

This is why I'm doing a bogus await at the beginning of the test now, to ensure that any unrelated promise that is waiting to run gets a chance to run before the test starts.

See this section: https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15475/files#diff-4ba8d96f30b74cf0a30189b1b111204e319d27a7785519bfec3941f6c5941190R26-R28

Now every event has an explainable reason to be there.

body:
Disallowing it seems harsh.  _maybe_ warning, but again, why be cruel to people?  It was originally simply reading the key of `compilerOptions` from `--config` for tsconfig.json, where we were already ignoring excess keys, and `deno.json`/`deno.jsonc` was implemented in a way that people could continue to use/maintain a `tsconfig.json` and have it work with Deno.  Restricting or warning would effectively break/change this behaviour.  Then if you allow all the `tsconfig.json` keys without breaking or warning... 😕 

So I am quite 👎  for this.
author: kitsonk

body:
I forgot about the initial taconfig.json implementation. Given that and the way this has worked, I agree that an error is too harsh. Given that people might use this with an existing tsconfig, yeah maybe even a warning wouldn’t be ideal. The issue with opening up configs to piggy backing is you leave a lot of trust up to other tools to be respectful with that piggy backing. It only takes one other tool to piggy back in a way that makes the owner of the config (the cli) no longer free to use certain config keys because of the ecosystem breakage that would happen. The chance of that happening is low, but it’s there.

If we were designing this from the ground up I’d argue for this, but given this is not actually an oversight, I’m going to close this PR and just hope other tools are respectful.

body:
With these changes, `deno bench` is now hanging forever after the benchmarks are run. This seems to be because the code that drives the benchmarks waits on a mpsc receiver until all senders have been dropped, and one of them is being kept alive:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/16dbf4adc390c9fb7656372b42811c1929e755dd/cli/tools/bench.rs#L407

That one sender is being kept alive because now a realm's module map is stored in the context slot (rather than in the isolate slot), and because of denoland/rusty_v8#1066 it ends up not getting dropped even after the isolate is dropped.

Edit (October 8th): This is fixed now.
author: andreubotella

body: denoland/deno#16286 changed the module map, that was in a slot in the isolate backed by the `IsolateAnnex`, to use a raw V8 slot in the isolate. But this PR changes the module map to be a slot in the context's annex, so some of the perf gains from that PR will regress. But I expect denoland/deno#16215 to land before this PR, after which the module map could be stored in a raw slot in the context.
author: andreubotella

body: While starting to look into writing tests for this, I realized that in this PR I'm using a single `ModuleLoader` instance for all of an isolate's realms. There module maps are still separate per realm, even if they share a loader, but since `ModuleLoader` implementations might have state, we might need to consider the implications. If we add an `Option<Fn() -> Rc<dyn ModuleLoader>>` field to `RuntimeOptions` (plus the current `module_loader` field for the main realm), we would also let embedders disallow module imports in `ShadowRealm`s, which might be worth considering. And the returned module loaders might be references to the main module loader, of course.
author: andreubotella

body: This PR got stuck for a while while most of the realm code got reverted because it was blocking performance work. All of the PRs that were preconditions for modules have now been relanded, so I have rewritten this PR from scratch and will continue to work on it now.

author: bartlomieju

body:
> The question about `ModuleLoader` is an interesting one and I don't have a good answer right now. Does spec say anything about it?

The HTML spec requires a level of "caching" in module fetches between a page's main and shadow realms, such that if a module is imported in the main realm it should not be re-fetched when imported from the shadow realm, and vice versa. I haven't looked at this code in a long time, but last time I checked Deno did some caching for module sources that would already take care of this.

But as I was looking at the spec, I realized that import maps were recently merged into the HTML spec, and it's not clear if anyone has looked in any depth at how they interact with `ShadowRealm`. Merging the HTML PR as is would mean that import maps don't apply in `ShadowRealm`s, which seems reasonable, [but I asked in a comment just in case](https://github.com/whatwg/html/pull/5339#issuecomment-1409604285). In this case, shadow realms would need a different module loader from the main realm, since they'd have to have a flag to not use import maps.

So I guess we should probably change `RuntimeOptions` to have a module loader for the main realm, and one for all shadow realms. Since they're `Option<Rc<dyn ModuleLoader>>`, both can be references to the same module loader object, for embedders that don't support import maps.


> Also we need to figure out what we're gonna do with snapshotting. I think for the first pass we should not care about additional realms and just make sure that snapshotting still works with a "global realm"

WFM
author: andreubotella

body: Since `JsRuntime::create_realm` can be used to create "regular" (non-shadow) realms, for example if we ever support the Node.js `vm` module, I thought it was better to require the realm creator to provide a module loader. Then in denoland/deno#16211 (when I find time to rebase that PR) we can have a module loader that gets used for every shadow reallm.
author: andreubotella

body: Let's wait on https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/17648 before we merge this PR.

Only because `state` is created before the isolate, so you can't populate that field at that point.

Maybe we could put an empty `v8::Global` there?
author: bartlomieju

body:
> > > Could you remind me of the reason why we need `global_realm` to be an `Option`? If we removed the `Option` we could get rid of a lot of clones.
> > 
> > 
> > Only because `state` is created before the isolate, so you can't populate that field at that point.
> 
> Maybe we could put an empty `v8::Global` there?

Appears that's currently not possible, because `v8::Global` APIs still require a valid `Isolate` handle. I'll think about it more.
author: bartlomieju

body:
> Appears that's currently not possible, because `v8::Global` APIs still require a valid `Isolate` handle. I'll think about it more.

I think I'd be fine with initializing `global_realm` to `MaybeUninit::zeroed().assume_init()`. This would need care when setting it, since we don't want to call the `Drop` impl of `v8::Global`.
author: andreubotella

body:
> > Appears that's currently not possible, because `v8::Global` APIs still require a valid `Isolate` handle. I'll think about it more.
> 
> I think I'd be fine with initializing `global_realm` to `MaybeUninit::zeroed().assume_init()`. This would need care when setting it, since we don't want to call the `Drop` impl of `v8::Global`.

Oh, never mind, that by itself would be undefined behavior since `v8::Global` contains a `NonNull` field.

Recommendation needed: 
I tried to write a custom error message for empty URLs. I put `Missing url. Check for extra commas.` as a placeholder for now.

In an ideal world, I think it would be great to have better error messages for the scenarios below, but I think that's out of scope.
1. Missing definition after = `--reload=`
2. Found comma without URLs `--reload=,`
3. Invalid URL `--reload=thisisinvalid`
4. Leading comma not allowed `--reload=,http://a/`
5. Trailing comma not allowed `--reload=,http://a/`
6. Adjacent commas not allowed `--reload=http://a/,,,,,,http://b/`

author: cjihrig

body: In Node.js itself, this is handled by always treating `default` as the `module.exports` CJS exports object, even in the case of `__esModule`. The reason we do this in Node.js is that because the static exports detection is not always fully accurate, having the ability for `import mod from 'mod'` to always directly correspond to the `module.exports` CJS object in every single case is important to maintain as a baseline. This is also the source of interop frictions unfortunately, but the ecosystem and tooling have already started to accept and deal with this!
author: guybedford

body: @guybedford yeah, the node esm -> node cjs in Deno works that way like Node. I wanted to see how far we could get with the deno esm importing node cjs doing it with the __esModule way as it seems to be more compatible with the ecosystem.
author: dsherret

body: Okay, just remember this is another moving target for bundlers and other tooling to follow as well. Is the Deno exports analysis accurate enough for it that you don't risk masking the default / losing access to bindings at all?
author: guybedford

body:
> Okay, just remember this is another moving target for bundlers and other tooling to follow as well.

I don't think this specific part will be too much trouble, but yes it's yet another thing.

> Is the Deno exports analysis accurate enough for it that you don't risk masking the default / losing access to bindings at all?

I'm not sure and we'll find out. I'm hoping that in combination with this new runtime check it will be accurate enough. I was thinking that the runtime check should also fall back to using the module when the "default" export doesn't exist as well, but TypeScript doesn't do that and I was trying to align with TypeScript's esmodule interop here.
author: dsherret

body:
> I don't think this specific part will be too much trouble, but yes it's yet another thing.

Configuring bundlers to bundle with ESM - CJS interop in "Deno mode" as distinct from "legacy" and "nodeesm" is definitely a new layer of complexity to handle.

> I'm not sure and we'll find out.

Is there some way to lower the risk in the process?

> I'm hoping that in combination with this new runtime check it will be accurate enough.

__esModule cases can be highly dynamic by it's nature of being a runtime check. Static analysis will always have cases missed I think. And in those cases, if there is a `default` export, you risk not being able to get access to the bindings _at all_. Unless I'm missing particular details about how Deno is supporting this?

> I was thinking that the runtime check should also fall back to using the module when the "default" export doesn't exist as well, but TypeScript doesn't do that and I was trying to align with TypeScript's esmodule interop here.

So if there is a `exports.__esModule = true; exports.name = 'name';` with no `default` export, `import m from 'm'` would be the direct module you mean? I think that would be worthwhile to have for Node.js compat.

When there are names and default and __esModule, default interop is very variable between bundlers and Node.js so that most authors are naturally tending towards not doing it anyway. Leaving all the interop issues to this particular combination can be fine to do as well, although I would obviously prefer full Node.js parity on interop for Node.js compat!
author: guybedford

body:
> __esModule cases can be highly dynamic by it's nature of being a runtime check. Static analysis will always have cases missed I think. And in those cases, if there is a default export, you risk not being able to get access to the bindings at all. Unless I'm missing particular details about how Deno is supporting this?

Yes, this is the one risk case—where it has identified a default export and __esModule === true, and the analysis fails to identify a named export. In this case, the named export would be lost. I think you're right that we should probably just revert to doing exactly as node does in its cjs interop and make the default export always the module. I was hoping to see though how big of an issue this was and if we'd be able to overcome it.

> So if there is a exports.__esModule = true; exports.name = 'name'; with no default export, import m from 'm' would be the direct module you mean? I think that would be worthwhile to have for Node.js compat.

Yes, exactly. I updated the PR to include this for now.

body:
`@rarkins` is the head maintainer for the Renovate bot. And I'm helping with the docs for Renovate.

### You're not getting 71 updates all at once

> "With your current configuration, Renovate will create 71 Pull Requests:"

This sounds overwhelming, but you're really getting:

- 2 Renovate PRs each hour
- Once you have 10 open Renovate PRs, Renovate stops making more PRs

### What's in the default `config:base` preset?

The default [`config:base` preset](https://docs.renovatebot.com/presets-config/#configbase) has:

```json
{
  "extends": [
    ":dependencyDashboard",
    ":semanticPrefixFixDepsChoreOthers",
    ":ignoreModulesAndTests",
    ":autodetectPinVersions",
    ":prHourlyLimit2",
    ":prConcurrentLimit10",
    "group:monorepos",
    "group:recommended",
    "workarounds:all"
  ]
}
```

#### Explanation of `config:base` contents

| Preset name | Explanation |
| --- | --- |
| `:dependencyDashboard` | Enable Renovate Dependency Dashboard creation.|
| `:semanticPrefixFixDepsChoreOthers` | If Renovate detects semantic commits, it will use semantic commit type fix for dependencies and chore for all others. |
| `:ignoreModulesAndTests` | Ignore `node_modules`, `bower_components`, `vendor` and various test/tests directories. |
| `:autodetectPinVersions` | Autodetect whether to pin dependencies or maintain ranges. |
| `:prHourlyLimit2` | Rate limit PR creation to a maximum of two per hour. |
| `:prConcurrentLimit10` | Limit to maximum 10 open PRs at any time. |
| `group:monorepos` | Group known monorepo packages together. |
| `group:recommended` | Use curated list of recommended non-monorepo package groupings. |
| `workarounds:all` | A collection of workarounds for known problems with packages. |

### Dependency Dashboard Approval workflow

> - You can for example suppress every update so that PRs only appear "on demand" once you select checkboxes in an issue

Renovate calls this the [Dependency Dashboard Approval workflow](https://docs.renovatebot.com/getting-started/use-cases/#dependency-dashboard-approval), and you control this behavior with the [`dependencyDashboardApproval` config option](https://docs.renovatebot.com/configuration-options/#dependencydashboardapproval).

### Renovate is too noisy

> - Renovate can be tuned to group, schedule or simply suppress updates so that you get less PRs. Myself and the Renovate team would be happy to help you with a config in future if you reconsider

See the [Renovate docs about Noise reduction](https://docs.renovatebot.com/noise-reduction/).

author: billywhizz

body:
I'm trying to understand _why_ it's faster. Is computing the SHA-256 of 1,000 bytes a meaningful amount of CPU-intensive work or are you merely timing thread pool round-trip times?

tokio-rayon is really just rayon that sends the result back over a oneshot channel (i.e., very thin shim) so I can think of two reasons for the stark difference:

1. tokio has many  threads per core, rayon is thread per core, resulting in fewer threads fighting for CPU time. That should get reflected in the number of (in)voluntary context switches in `/usr/bin/time -v deno run bench.js`.

2. tokio's job queue is FIFO, rayon's is LIFO. Does `spawn_fifo()` make a difference? LIFO can, theoretically, result in starvation.
author: bnoordhuis

body: @bnoordhuis yes, my understanding was that rayon will create one thread per core and pin those threads so any work on them should have less context switches. it may just be in this benchmark there is no other work happening and there is cache affinity between the main thread and the thread doing the async work? this makes a big difference for these kinds of micro benchmarks, but might not have much impact in real world on a system under load?

author: littledivy

body:
> We can not trust the `content-length` header. This code needs to be written in a way that `content-length` can be used as a size indication as a performance optimization, but the final size of the buffer is still determined by the size and number of actual chunks.
> 
> Specifically we need to deal with the cases:
> 
> * totalLength > `content-length`
> * totalLength < `content-length`

Undici, Chrome, Firefox use `content-length` header, the size of the buffer is determined by the `content-length` header in all three. One can argue that if `content-length` header does not contain the right value, the response is corrupt.
And in those cases the user can still consume the body by iterating through the response stream. 

It's too big of optimization to ignore it. 

In all runtimes I've tested:

- if totalLength > `content-length`, the returned buffer is of `content-length` size.
- if totalLength < `content-length` the request hangs until it's aborted due to timeout.
author: marcosc90

body:
If we want to behave differently, we can grow the buffer if more bytes are read and slice the buffer if less bytes are read, at a performance penalty for cases where `content-length != totalLength`.

FYI, if `content-length` > totalLength, Deno currently hangs. (same as the other runtimes/libs)
author: marcosc90

body:
I am confused by your comment. I am **not** suggesting we do not do this optimization. I am suggesting that your current implementation is placing too much trust in the `content-length` header, and that the implementation needs to be able to handle when the real body size deviates from the "known, well-formed, and correct `content-length`" fast path. There needs to be a way for the fast path to downgrade to the slow path if it detects that some assumptions made for the fast path do not hold anymore.

My concern here is not about the HTTP transport decoding which in HTTP/1 needs to place trust in the `content-length` header like you describe. `hyper` handles this for us, and I trust it to be correct. What I am worried about us re-interpreting these headers _differently_ to the transport layer, which can lead to issues.

For example, if a server returns a HTTP response (on a http/1 connection) containing both a `transfer-encoding: chunked` header, and a `content-length` header, Hyper may use the `transfer-encoding` header and ignore the `content-length` when decoding the stream. In the process it will return more data than the `content-length` indicates. On the JS side however we trust that the `content-length` is correct and just blindly assume that `hyper` returns the exact number of bytes specified in the `content-length` header. These are the scenarios where `content-length != totalLength`.

This same scenario described above is especially prevalent on HTTP/2 and HTTP/3, where body framing happens completely independently from headers such as `content-length`, so they have explicit meaning to the transport layer at all there.

Also instead of manually re-interpreting the `content-length` header ourselves, we should use use the value returned from `reqwest::Response::content_length()`. This value will automatically also take into account response body compression (e.g. `transfer-encoding: gzip`).
author: lucacasonato

body: If you need guidance on the Rust side changes for this, I'd be happy to help out! :)
author: lucacasonato

body:
Valid points. I meant to open the PR as a draft, to showcase the performance improvements. I'll add the code for downgrading to the slow path in case there's more data than reported by `content-length` header, as well as ignoring it when `transfer-encoding: chunked` is present as per the spec

https://greenbytes.de/tech/webdav/rfc2616.html#rfc.section.4.4
> If a message is received with both a Transfer-Encoding header field and a Content-Length header field, the latter must be ignored.

It'll take me a while to write any good rust code, so if someone wants to pitch in with adding `reqwest::Response::content_length()` as mentioned, I can handle the JS stuff.
author: marcosc90

body: @marcosc90 I have wired through the `content-length` from the Rust side into the `InnerBody` constructor now :)
author: lucacasonato

body: Oh, that was easy :P, I really need to start learning Rust. Thanks @lucacasonato, I'll start working on the slow path.
author: marcosc90

body:
@lucacasonato Deno **debug** crashes if `content-length != totalLength`

```
thread 'tokio-runtime-worker' panicked at 'payload claims content-length of 10240, custom content-length 
header claims 20480', 
/home/marcos/.cargo/registry/src/github.com-1ecc6299db9ec823/hyper-0.14.19/src/proto/h1/role.rs:675:37
note: run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable to display a backtrace
```

Using release build, it does not crash but it's also impossible to test the downgrade since it seems that Hyper stops reading after `content-length` bytes. If I send more data than `content-length` that extra data is clipped.
If `content-length` is greater, the request never ends, it keeps waiting for the extra data that will never be sent. (The only thing I could test in this scenario is the request aborting by using an abort controller after N milliseconds)

I added a test, with `ignore: true` in the meantime. I performed manual tests by hardcoding `this.#contentLength`. We can enable the test if there's a way to mock `this.#contentLength` or `res.content_length()` in rust.

Here's the test: https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15925/commits/5f9b57ab2b952e8282718310d792ef6f7728b28c if you have any ideas on how to enable it.

tl;dr: It seems that the downgrade scenario will never happen with current Hyper implementation.
author: marcosc90

body: You can not have the test use a real hyper webserver as the server side. Hyper is smart enough to prevent common attack vectors like these. You'll have to use something similar to the `bufferServer` tool used in tests like `fetchRequest`. Ie you'll have to manually construct a valid but logically corrupt wire serialized HTTP/1.1 response.
author: lucacasonato
marcosc90

body:
After more testing:

- If `transfer-encoding` and `content-length` are present. `res.content_length()` is `null`.
- if `content-length`  > `totalLength`,  the request is never terminated, or the following error is thrown: ` Error: request or response body error: error reading a body from connection: end of file before message length reached`. (Depends on whether the server closes the request or not). 
- if `content-length`  <  `totalLength` Hyper reads a maximum of `content-length` bytes, the rest is discarded. In this case `res.content_length()` equals `content-length` header
- if several `content-length` headers are sent with different values, an error is triggered.

This makes the downgrade path impossible to occur with the current Hyper version since all cases are being handled correctly by Hyper (client).

We can leave the downgrade path as a safeguard (without unit tests, just manual testing), or we can remove it and trust Hyper.

body:
Ah, that makes sense actually:

> A request or response that includes a payload body can include a content-length header field. A request or response is also malformed if the value of a content-length header field does not equal the sum of the DATA frame payload lengths that form the body. A response that is defined to have no payload, as described in [RFC7230], Section 3.3.2, can have a non-zero content-length header field, even though no content is included in DATA frames.

From h2 spec.

If they are both piped, is there even a console? I might have the concept wrong!

The use case I came looking for consoleSize was for finding the word-wrap width for displaying help text from a command-line utility. author: shadowspawn

body:
> If they are both piped, is there even a console?

@shadowspawn huh, I thought there was some low level api to still get it based on the parent process (or console group or whatever it's called) because I had seen api's like `let size = terminal_size()?` or in the case of .net `var width = Console.WindowWidth;`, but looking at all those implementations I found they're all providing stdout's handle. Additionally, it seems it's possible to get the console size via stdin (https://stackoverflow.com/a/59717851/188246).

That said, I still think we should consider just making this `Deno.consoleSize()` and perhaps have it try to get the size via stdout, stderr, then stdin, and if not then throw. Otherwise, then perhaps it should be on all three (`Deno.stdout.consoleSize()`, `Deno.stdin....`, `Deno.stdin....`) if that indeed works on stdin. Making it `Deno.consoleSize()` seems easiest for users, but maybe it's not versitle enough?

> The use case I came looking for consoleSize was for finding the word-wrap width for displaying help text from a command-line utility. The help is being displayed to either stdout or stderr, and I was expecting to find the width of the one being used, which might be a pipe (and hence width not known).

Perhaps in a future api (with `isatty` moved to `Deno.stdout.isatty()`) the code would do:

```ts
const size = Deno.stdout.isatty() ? Deno.consoleSize() : undefined;
```

What you can do is write an empty file `$DENO_DIR/last_update_check`. We can use the mtime of that file to log last update, allowing us to get away with just a single extra stat syscall.

> I think that check_for_upgrades() routine should be async and be called with tokio::spawn() using tokio::select!() with get_subcommand(flags).

That won't work. We want to present "There's a new version 1.65.2" before anything else gets printed to stdout. Otherwise it will be quite confusing.
author: ry

body:
> > I think that check_for_upgrades() routine should be async and be called with tokio::spawn() using tokio::select!() with get_subcommand(flags).

> That won't work. We want to present "There's a new version 1.65.2" before anything else gets printed to stdout. Otherwise it will be quite confusing.

I want to argue that startup time is way more important than printing this banner. We can have a cake and eat it too by doing the check in the background and printing the banner at the top on the subsequent run.
author: bartlomieju

body:
`DenoDir::new` is >40% of overall overhead _even before_ the update checker starts up. 

Even if `DenoDir::new` was fully optimized away, this PR will add enough overhead to overpower all optimizations suggested in #15945. 

IMO we should reconsider doing this check & display in `deno run` and move it to some someplace where startup time does not matter (like `deno eval` / `deno`).

body: Regarding the writing and out buffers I started wondering if it wouldn't be possible to do a lot of ZeroCopyBuf returning ops as pairs of ops where the first does the work and saves the return as a Resource, returning a ResourceId plus, using an out ptr, the required buffer size. The second call would then be called with a newly allocated buffer based on the out ptr received buffer size.
author: aapoalas

body:
> Regarding the writing and out buffers I started wondering if it wouldn't be possible to do a lot of ZeroCopyBuf returning ops as pairs of ops where the first does the work and saves the return as a Resource, returning a ResourceId plus, using an out ptr, the required buffer size. The second call would then be called with a newly allocated buffer based on the out ptr received buffer size.

That will be useful in other cases, yes.

When compressing, its hard to estimate size of the buffer which makes it very easy to lose performance by allocating way too much as "pre-allocation". When decompressing, its going to be larger than the input buffer and we have the same problem with estimation here. ZeroCopyBuf is probably the best in this situation. 

Also, we cannot reuse a single buffer for multiple chunks, they need to be cloned / allocated before enqueue.
author: littledivy
billywhizz

body: oh wait i misunderstood @aapoalas...makes sense now! yeah we should try this
author: littledivy

body:
> oh wait i misunderstood @aapoalas...makes sense now! yeah we should try this

what i was basically thinking is being able to allocate an arrabyffer as a resource and pass that around. then Rust side can have some certainty around properties of the buffer without lots of checks. should also help with data locality/cpu cache as reusing same memory repeatedly. there may be security implications to think about.
author: billywhizz

body:
> what i was basically thinking is being able to allocate an arrabyffer as a resource and pass that around. then Rust side can have some certainty around properties of the buffer without lots of checks. should also help with data locality/cpu cache as reusing same memory repeatedly. there may be security implications to think about.

One issue I can see with this is that it is observable and somewhat spooky on a usage level.

### Observability
If an API returns an `ArrayBuffer` and that buffer is one of the "resource buffers", then its length will not be exactly the size that is needed to store the data, but whatever the resource buffer was allocated with. The only way around this is to make a sized copy of the buffer using `buffer.slice(0, dataByteLength)` but now we're making an extra copy.

Alternatively, if an API returns eg. `Uint8Array`, its size can be sized to the data size but the user can still observe the disparity in data size and buffer size by using `uint8Array.buffer` to get access to the backing `ArrayBuffer`.

### Spooky
If copies are avoided and the same `ArrayBuffer` is being reused and returned, then users have to just know that they don't really own the buffer they're given.

eg. This seems like a safe thing to do:
```ts
const someBuffer = Deno.readFileSync("./myFile.txt");
await mutateBufferData(someBuffer);
Deno.writeFileSync("./myFile.mutated.txt", someBuffer);
```
You receive a buffer, mutate its data and then write the result into a file. But what if during your `await` handling another `Deno.readFile` call happens to be made and it reuses the `ArrayBuffer` backing `someBuffer.buffer`? Suddenly your `someBuffer` contents will be something entirely different.
author: aapoalas
billywhizz

body: @aapoalas yes - i can think of lots of issues with this especially when doing async ops. i was thinking more of it as something we might be able to use internally when we are doing synchronous things like encoding/escaping/crypto. definitely needs some further discussion and experimentation, but the potential perf improvements make that worth doing i think?

body: Towards #16046
author: marcosc90

body:
Thank you for starting work on this. I think this is a good direction, but I would like to see some changes to the implementation to make it align with #16046 more closely.

Namely, I think this optimization should be decoupled from `InnerBody`. What I mean with this, is that the `rid` is a properties of the underlying `ReadableStream`, not the `InnerBody`. If we change these properties to be properties of the `ReadableStream`, then any stream annotated will this property will be opted into a fast path, rather than just the streams inside of an `InnerBody` created by `fetch` itself. In addition, the `knownExactLength` is a property of the underlying `Resource`, not of the `ReadableStream`. As such it should be stored on the resource Rust side, not JS side.

Specifically:
- All resource backed `ReadableStream` should be branded with a `[_rid]` property.
  - The response body for `fetch` is such an object, so it should be branded.
  - Resource backed `ReadableStream` objects can be created from a rid using the `readableStreamForRid` function in `ext/web/06_streams.js`
- `InnerBody#consume` should check if the body is a resource backed `ReadableStream` and if so call `op_read_all(rid)` to read the entire stream in one go.
  - The `getReadableStreamRid` function in `ext/web/06_streams.js` can perform this brand check.
  - This entire "read all" codepath can be moved into a `readableStreamCollectIntoUint8Array` function in `ext/web/06_streams.js` that can handle any `ReadableStream`, including non resource backed streams. The entire fast & slow path can be contained within this function.
- Make the `knownExactLength` a property of the Rust side resource, and don't send it to JS.
  - This way other resources that may have a fixed known size can also make use of this optimization.
  - The exact length only needs to be known Rust side, as the underlying buffer for `op_read_all` is allocated in Rust.
  - To do this, add a new `fn size_hint(&self): (u64, Option<u64>);` method to the `deno_core::Resource` trait. This method returns to the caller a hint about how large the resource is in the form `(minimum, maybe_maximum)`. The trait method can have a default implementation that returns `(0, None)`, as this bound is valid for any resource.
  - Add a custom `Resource::size_hint` implementation to `FetchResponseBodyResource` that returns the exact length if known up front (from the `reqwest::Response::content_length()` method).
  - Update `op_read_all` to use the `size_hint` method on the resource to pre-allocate an underlying buffer with the correct size if possible. If no size hint is available, we can fall back to a slower allocation heavy path in Rust, that will still be faster than a read-all implemented in JS.
- In a follow up, make this work with `HttpStreamResource`.
  - This requires that `HttpStreamResource` implements the `Resource::read`/`Resource::read_return` trait method, and use the regular `op_read` instead of a specialized `op_http_read`.
  - A  custom `Resource::size_hint` implementation for `HttpStreamResource` is also necessary then.

With these changes the improvements would be noticeable for _all_ resource backed streams, not just `Request` and `Response` returned from `fetch` or `Deno.serveHttp`. The following would also get a nice speed up for example (because `file.readable` is a resource backed stream):

```ts
const file = await Deno.open("./my_very_large_file.txt");
const resp = new Response(file.readable);
const txt = await resp.text();
```

body:
Ran some benchmarks:

- Reading a 80mb file using `await Deno.readAll()` is identical (within 1%)
- Reading a 80mb file using `Response(file.readable).arrayBuffer()` is 12% faster
  -  Now ~10% faster than `await Deno.readAll()`, previously ~15% slower
- `fetch`ing a 100mb file, and calling `resp.arrayBuffer()` on it is 5-6% faster
- File server (File->http) throughput is identical (within 1%)
- Proxy server (fetch->resp) throughput is 4% higher

While benchmarking I thought of another optimisation that can make streaming HTTP req/resp bodies from Rust -> JS maybe around 5% faster. Also another optimisation to make the file server ~3% faster. I'll do these in a follow up.
author: lucacasonato

body:
@bartlomieju 
> Is there an immediate need for this API? If so, I would love to see examples

I have been working on https://github.com/Suficio/bevy_js_prototype which basically embeds Deno in a Bevy context.

I would like to guarantee to the user that at any stage of execution of the program they can have exclusive access to the Bevy world. Since `Extensions` are initialized during the creation of `JsRuntime` they will not have access to the resource that facilitates communication between Bevy and Deno which is what this PR allows them to do.

It is possible to achieve this using the current implementation, but the synchronization code will quickly become un-maintainable.

In the linked repo I am using the resource to determine `TypeIds` of reflectable types as static properties of their constructors which will be the basis for type management across Bevy and Deno. Overall, it's a huge ergonomic improvement for my use case.

If you would like me to provide an example of this functionality as a `deno_core` example, I would be glad to.

body: One option to handle this issue would be to use `Option<&mut [u8]>` instead of a plain slice in ops.
author: aapoalas
andreubotella

body:
> In V8, empty `ArrayBuffer`s will (always, I think) return a null pointer for the data. In Rust, zero-sized slices can't be created from a null pointer, _but they can be created from a dangling pointer_. In fact, we fixed this same bug in the `Deref` implementation of `v8::BackingStore` by using dangling pointers: https://github.com/denoland/rusty_v8/blob/main/src/array_buffer.rs#L315-L320.
> 
> I opened [denoland/rusty_v8#1131](https://github.com/denoland/rusty_v8/pull/1131) to have `ArrayBuffer::data` return an `Option<NonNull>`, like `BackingStore::data` does. And with it, you could do:
> 
> ```rust
> let store = b.data().unwrap_or_else(NonNull::dangling).cast::<u8>();
> // SAFETY: rust guarantees that lifetime of slice is no longer than the call.
> unsafe { ::std::slice::from_raw_parts_mut(store.as_ptr(), byte_length) }
> ```

The unfortunate thing is that for FFI at least, we need the null pointer. A dangling pointer will cause the `UnsafePointer.of` static method to lie about the pointer address of a given buffer.

So, to some degree the solution cannot be quite so clear cut, we also need something done in the ops macro stage.

author: bartlomieju

body:
I needed to make unref'ing the callback asynchronous. Additionally, closing a ref'ed callback resource is internally-asynchronous.

The reason for this is that the Future that controls the ref'ing is dependent on the resources continued existence, and the resource cannot be dropped while a ref is still active. If it gets dropped, a panic will occur.

As a result, any tests that rely on a ref'ed callback disappearing from the resource table synchronously after a `close()` call will fail. The proper way to "synchronously" close a callback resource is to first `unref()` it, and only call `close()` on it once the `unref()` method resolves with `true`.

body:
After further investigation, it seems that there's a bug in WPT, it expects a `TypeError` to be thrown for the following value:

```
\n/x
```
```
assert_throws_js(TypeError, () => new Request("about:blank", { headers: [["Content-Type", val.input]] }));
```

While `\n` is not a valid value for a header, the specification states that the header value must be normalized, so AFAIK we're doing the right thing by not throwing in that scenario.

> To normalize a [byte sequence](https://infra.spec.whatwg.org/#byte-sequence) potentialValue, remove any leading and trailing [HTTP whitespace bytes](https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#http-whitespace-byte) from potentialValue.

body:
> Max is an upper bound. We don't necessarily want to or can allocate that much space. The idea behind this logic is that we allocate the exact size we need if we know it, and otherwise we start at the lesser of `min` or `grow_length`. This prevents initial over-allocation.
> 
> As such I don't think this change is necessarily beneficial.

If max is known, we should allocate that much space, otherwise, it shouldn't be set. both fetch & http `size_hint` is returning the exact size of the body in `max` (the only two resources setting max currently), which is being ignored completely by `read_all` (if greater than 64kb). What's the use of `max` if we're not allocating the max size at once, currently unnecessary resizes are being done. Setting `max` is specifically for that fast path.

We know exactly the body size, we should use that fast path when available.
author: marcosc90

body:
Furthermore this patch increases `fetch` performance when consuming a body (the most common operation when using `fetch`)

**main**

```
cpu: Intel(R) Core(TM) i9-9980HK CPU @ 2.40GHz
runtime: deno 1.26.1 (x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)

benchmark       time (avg)             (min … max)       p75       p99      p995
-------------------------------------------------- -----------------------------
fetch 1mb   957.65 µs/iter   (538.74 µs … 4.36 ms) 949.99 µs   2.99 ms   3.91 ms
fetch 5mb     4.06 ms/iter     (3.25 ms … 7.66 ms)   4.11 ms   7.51 ms   7.66 ms
fetch 128k  265.63 µs/iter   (162.47 µs … 3.33 ms) 289.15 µs 914.62 µs   1.14 ms
fetch 16kb  127.55 µs/iter (105.26 µs … 953.49 µs) 130.66 µs 255.74 µs 324.11 µs
```

**this patch**

```
cpu: Intel(R) Core(TM) i9-9980HK CPU @ 2.40GHz
runtime: deno 1.26.1 (x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)

benchmark       time (avg)             (min … max)       p75       p99      p995
-------------------------------------------------- -----------------------------
fetch 1mb   613.06 µs/iter   (398.42 µs … 3.29 ms) 715.54 µs   1.42 ms   1.71 ms
fetch 5mb     2.82 ms/iter     (1.69 ms … 6.26 ms)   3.08 ms   5.82 ms   6.26 m
fetch 128k  194.14 µs/iter   (145.34 µs … 1.93 ms) 208.52 µs 454.15 µs 581.11 µs
fetch 16kb  122.34 µs/iter (103.14 µs … 949.53 µs) 119.81 µs 248.41 µs 332.82 µs
```

> This prevents initial over-allocation.

The only two resources overriding `size_hint` set `max` to the exact body size, so it doesn't prevent initial over-allocation, current logic only causes extra resize operations.


|  |128kb|1mb|5mb|2gb
|--|--|--|--|--|
| main: resizes |1|6|14|4100
| patch: resizes |0|0|0|0|0
author: marcosc90

body:
> Sorry for not responding to this earlier. The logic in `op_read_all` is correct. The size_hint for the HTTP resources is just not. It currently is:
> 
> ```
>   fn size_hint(&self) -> (u64, Option<u64>) {
>     (0, self.size)
>   }
> ```
> 
> but it should be
> 
> ```
>   fn size_hint(&self) -> (u64, Option<u64>) {
>     (self.size.unwrap_or(0), self.size)
>   }
> ```
> 
> If the **exact** size is known (`self.size == Some(_)`), then the upper and lower bound should both be set to that exact size. Once you do this the existing `op_read_all` optimisation starts working.
> 
> This is already the case for the `size_hint()` in `ext/http`.

While that will fix the performance issue, IMO that's patching incorrect logic of `op_read_all`. There's no need to set `max` at all if we're using `min` instead and ignoring `max` when it's set. I'll submit the changes for fetch `size_hint` to improve performance though.
author: marcosc90

body: We're not ignoring max. We're just ignoring max for pre-allocation. But we do use max to prevent over-allocation for example. Under-allocation is always better than over-allocation, as over-allocation can hard crash the process with an OOM error.
author: lucacasonato

body:
> We're not ignoring max. We're just ignoring max for pre-allocation. But we do use max to prevent over-allocation for example. Under-allocation is always better than over-allocation, as over-allocation can hard crash the process with an OOM error.

My point is that if `max` causes over-allocation, that resource `size_hint` is setting incorrect `max` which should be only set (that's why it's `Option`) when ~maximum~ exact body size is known.

body:
While `SafePromiseAll` is good for preventing tampering of `Promise.prototype.then`, we need to be careful to use it as it seems having performance penalty compared to `Promise.all`.

I think the usages of `SafePromiseAll` in this PR doesn't affect hot paths of benchmarks we've been tracking in the benchmark page.
author: kt3k

body: Great to see it's landed! Is it also good to have some lint rule to enforce (or at least make developers be aware of) the use of these new `SafePromise` stuff?
author: magurotuna

body:
> Is it also good to have some lint rule to enforce (or at least make developers be aware of) the use of these new SafePromise stuff?

That makes sense to me. `SafePromiseAll` is added in this PR and I think that can be checked by the linter. I'm not sure if we can do similar check for `PromisePrototypeThen` as it probably requires the type inference of objects..
author: kt3k

body:
> I'm not sure if we can do similar check for PromisePrototypeThen as it probably requires the type inference of objects..

Yeah that's true. My rudimentary idea is that we have the linter check for all `.then(...)` and warn them. Yes, this approach would produce lots of false positives, i.e. the linter would warn the call `.then(...)` even if this `then` method is not for Promise.
In my opinion, however, false positives are much better than false negatives in this case. When false positives occur we can suppress them, saying "hey linter, trust me, this is a false positive." However, when it comes to false negatives, we can never notice that there is problematic code that's prone to pollution from malicious users.

> I don't think this works. If the A side reads the stream, the B side gets nothing because the underlying resource has been read now.

To avoid that, I placed the fast path inside `ReadableByteStreamController.pull`, so `controller.enqueue` can be used so the read chunks are enqueued correctly to the other branch.

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/d65f4b3d99dd3b0aa4d1e51027c9381c15187238/ext/web/06_streams.js#L684-L696

body:
@sigmaSd 

> I think it should prompt for vscode code settings (like fresh does) not everyone uses vscode

There's no support from the rest of the team to add interactive prompts, so I'm trying to land on some sane defaults - since 80% Deno users report using VSCode, I think it's fine to generate that file by default.

> Also what about adding deno_bench.ts or its early for that since its unstable ?

Good point, I think we should stabilize `Deno.bench` first (which we should do for v1.28 as well).
author: bartlomieju

body:
> > Also what about adding deno_bench.ts or its early for that since its unstable ?

> Good point, I think we should stabilize Deno.bench first (which we should do for v1.28 as well).

`deno bench` and `Deno.bench()` API are now stable. I'd welcome suggested addition in a follow up PR.
author: bartlomieju

body:
> There's no support from the rest of the team to add interactive prompts, so I'm trying to land on some sane defaults - since 80% Deno users report using VSCode, I think it's fine to generate that file by default.

If there won't be an interactive prompt, an opt-out cli option would be very appreciated. Less annoying to do `deno init --no-vscode` (or something similar) than `deno init && rm -r .vscode`
author: ThePotatoChronicler

body: I myself would do the other way round, `deno init --include-vscode`. It seems that it should be generic for everyone and options for particular features
author: avalero

body:
> I myself would do the other way round, `deno init --include-vscode`. It seems that it should be generic for everyone and options for particular features

Personally I mostly use nvim, so I initially that's what I advocated as well, but the argument of the maintainers is realistic, 
- Deno users are mostly vscode users
- deno init point is to make deno developement as friction-less as possible, currently without this, you have to each time you create a project, click on `Enable deno in this workspace`, changing this to just deno init, is a noticeable difference in DX
- And not only vscode can parse .settings.json, even nvim can with the right plugin
author: sigmaSd

body:
> Should we remove --lock-write flag?

No, it allows to overwrite the lockfile completely - eg. when you get into a situation where there's integrity mismatch, but want to update the lockfile, then specifying this flag will do the job. Error message suggests it:

```
error: Integrity check failed for npm package: "cowsay@1.5.0".
  Cache has "sha512-8Ipzr54Z8zROr/62C8f0PdhQcDusS05gKTS87xxdji8VbWefWly0k8BwGK7+VqamOrkv3eGsCkPtvlHzrhWsCA==" and lockfile has "foobar".
  Use "--lock-write" flag to update the lockfile.
```

> What about a default value for --lock?

What would be the default value? `CWD/deno.lock`?

> If I change the lock file and then run again with --lock=deno.json I get this error:

Can you provide contents of `deno.lock` and `main.ts`?

body:
I was also thinking, we might want to make the `toSpliced` follow the same suggested technique as `with` by allowing new types to be included in the array as the resultant array would be considered a new entity.

Edit: these changes can be upstreamed afterwards as well: https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/50333
author: sno2

body:
(Copied from resolved comment thread)

> built-in typings arent ever complex typings like that, so we will leave it to any.

I'm not sure if it's that simple why there aren't complex typings. Many of the array methods were implemented in far older type systems so they did not have the ability to use these new type features without breaking old code. I'd consider this an inflection point in improving the user-experience with types. Also, if you type this as any, then no one who cares about sufficiently safe code will want to use this new feature. Please reconsider.

Edit: There has also been a shift in the official typescript typings towards using more complex types in an effort to improve DX. Some examples:

- https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/blob/main/lib/lib.es2021.promise.d.ts#L42
- https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/blob/main/lib/lib.es2020.promise.d.ts#L40
- https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/blob/main/lib/lib.es2022.sharedmemory.d.ts#L26
author: sno2

body:
> (Copied from resolved comment thread)
> 
> > built-in typings arent ever complex typings like that, so we will leave it to any.
> 
> I'm not sure if it's that simple why there aren't complex typings. Many of the array methods were implemented in far older type systems so they did not have the ability to use these new type features without breaking old code. I'd consider this an inflection point in improving the user-experience with types. Also, if you type this as any, then no one who cares about sufficiently safe code will want to use this new feature. Please reconsider.
> 
> Edit: There has also been a shift in the official typescript typings towards using more complex types in an effort to improve DX. Some examples:
> 
> * https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/blob/main/lib/lib.es2021.promise.d.ts#L42
> * https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/blob/main/lib/lib.es2020.promise.d.ts#L40
> * https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/blob/main/lib/lib.es2022.sharedmemory.d.ts#L26

@sno2 this is a stop-gap solution until TypeScript supports this feature officially. I believe it's better to use looser types now and narrow them later, than be too strict right now.
author: bartlomieju

body:
> I believe it's better to use looser types now and narrow them later, than be too strict right now.

Why release loose types only to break libraries and codebases a few months later?  You can always add looser behavior later if the typescript team somehow chooses to use `any` typing instead.
author: sno2

body:
> > I believe it's better to use looser types now and narrow them later, than be too strict right now.
> 
> Why release loose types only to break libraries and codebases a few months later?

Okay, after confering with other team members it seems it's better to go with too strict than too loose types here. Thanks for bringing this up @sno2!

body:
> @andreubotella it feels like we should have a custom `TaskRunner` implementation that could wake up event loop to handle this case, do you have any knowledge about inner working of the runners in V8?

I don't know too much about the V8 platform abstraction, but it seems like `TaskRunner::PostTask` could be implemented by simply calling `tokio::spawn`, and similarly with `TaskRunner::PostDelayedTask`. I'm not sure about the details of task nesting and idle tasks, but you can opt out of that by having the corresponding `Enabled` methods return false.

But it seems like in order to use a `TaskRunner`, you'd need a `Platform` implementation, and that might be more complicated to implement right. Although things like `Platform::CallOnWorkerThread` might map easily to `tokio::task::spawn_blocking`.
author: andreubotella

body: I just want to point out that allowing ancestor paths for ffi makes it possible to give read, write and dlopen permissions to the same path, which can be exploited to write your own custom dylib onto the disk and then load it.
author: aapoalas

body:
> I just want to point out that allowing ancestor paths for ffi makes it possible to give read, write and open permissions to the same path, which can be exploited to write your own custom dylib onto the disk and then load it.

Is it not the case already even without this pr, If I have --allow-read-write-ffi=lib.so, I can at runtime rewrite the library and execute it.

I think that the combination of flags is already a footgun, this pr doesn't address security ,its purely for improving convenience in the hope that developer stops writing -A

Personally I I think --allow-run and --allow-ffi will always a be a hole in the deno sandbox, I think the only real future solution is to use WASI, wasmer.io alredy showed that it can be done, interacting with the outside world while keeping the sandbox
author: sigmaSd

body:
> Is it not the case already even without this pr, If I have --allow-read-write-ffi=lib.so, I can at runtime rewrite the library and execute it.

Yes, this is definitely the case already, only it's a tad bit less likely to happen. Though, the most common usage pattern is probably something like `--allow-read=. --allow-write=. --allow-ffi=./lib/mylib.so` which includes this exact issue so... eh. I just wanted to point out the danger.

body:
Maybe `__proto__` reads should show a warning too then? (With or without actually returning the property)

I feel like silently creating/returning `__proto__` keys is a worse solution since it leads to headaches in debugging, especially for people who don't know that `__proto__` isn't implemented

It's also possible to emit the warning only once, with a longer explanation on why the error occurred and what the user can do -- perhaps that's a better solution?

Something along the lines of

> An attempt to access/set `__proto__` was made within your application or one of its dependencies.
> `__proto__` is not implemented in Deno due to security concerns over unsanitized inputs setting prototypes maliciously
> 1. If your own code uses `__proto__`, replace it with `Object.getPrototypeOf` or `Object.setPrototypeOf`
> 2. If one of your dependencies uses or depends on something that uses `__proto__`, you will need to suggest changes and ask the dependency maintainer to update their code
> 3. If `__proto__` has been replaced in a newer version of a module that is depended upon by a module you depend upon, you will need to suggest changes and ask them to update their dependencies.
