body: This will be problematic if/when I get my Fast API void pointer CL merged to V8, as we'll probably want to use pointers to represent that... Except! Yes, this is good. Represent Uint8Array's pointer using `*mut u8`, u32 for Uint32Array etc. and use `*mut c_void` for Externals.
author: aapoalas

body:
> *mut c_void for Externals.

@aapoalas IMO i'd be nice to have a magic type `Resource<T>` for when externals are supported, since 99% of cases will dereference it to T anyways. For FFI, yes we can do *mut c_void
author: littledivy

body:
> > *mut c_void for Externals.
> 
> @aapoalas IMO i'd be nice to have a magic type `Resource<T>` for when externals are supported, since 99% of cases will dereference it to T anyways. For FFI, yes we can do *mut c_void

Yeah. One thing that we will need to be careful about is the "substitution safety" required by V8's external pointer table, see this code comment and Samuel Groß's comment on it: https://chromium-review.googlesource.com/c/v8/v8/+/3957815/18/include/v8-internal.h#387
Essentially, Deno should make sure that either it's safe to call a given API with an `External` of the wrong type (`Resource<T>` would accomplish this if each call verifies its Resource type, though FFI spoils that since then there are unknown pointers floating around as well) or makes sure that it's impossible to call the raw APIs directly. `ops.op_some_resource_call`'s being exposed makes this impossible as well...

It's a bit of a complicated situation that probably cannot be resolved easily but should be taken up as a future goal. The reason for aiming for this is that pointer swapping enables memory corruption attacks, which may at worst lead to exploits and sandbox escapes, as opposed to just segfaulting.

I feel like a lockfile should mostly help with that if that's a common issue. That said, there's pretty much no scenario where I would recommend pinning system libs because while in this case the issue was for consumers of deno, it could easily happen the other way around. All it takes is one dependency that pulls libc to define a higher version than what deno defines.

I also think that with libc the chances of patch versions breaking stuff are rather low. So that should probably help out too.

That said, since I'm personally not using `cli` directly it doesn't really matter to me, just my 2 cents.

That is true. However, the two tasks serve as a starting point, and I think there's more to gain by having the configuration file:
1. Provides a way for newcomers to familiarise themselves with the repo alongside the contributing reference.
2. Centralises task definitions for both CI and local-machine-run tasks.
3. It's cheap, adding almost no managerial overhead.

It's worked great in the Standard Library, and I'm sure it could evolve to be of use here.

body:
Previously, only `Deno.permissions.query()` calls were covered. Now, prompt interactions are covered. Preliminary tests on my local machine look good.

This is done by adding `is_all: bool` to the return value of `PermissionState.check()`, which resembles whether the permission given in the prompt interaction is for one permission value (`y` or `n`) or all (`a` or `d`) of the same permission type (`env`, `read`, etc.)

Is this a sound approach?
author: iuioiua

body:
Tested locally, seems to be working very well.

> Previously, only `Deno.permissions.query()` calls were covered. Now, prompt interactions are covered. Preliminary tests on my local machine look good.
> 
> This is done by adding `is_all: bool` to the return value of `PermissionState.check()`, which resembles whether the permission given in the prompt interaction is for one permission value (`y` or `n`) or all (`a` or `d`) of the same permission type (`env`, `read`, etc.)
> 
> Is this a sound approach?

I'm not fully grepping it - `is_all` seems very ambigious, what are the situations where this flag would be `false`?

We should probably bike shed a bit on the prompt; I'm thinking that instead of `Allow? [y/n/a/d] (y = yes, allow; n = no, deny; a = yes to all, allow all; d = no to all, deny all) >` we could do `Allow? [y/n/a/d] (y = yes, allow; n = no, deny; a = allow all; d = deny all) >`
author: bartlomieju

body: I find the wording of the question a bit confusing. We need to make clear that _a = allow all **env vars**_ for example, not _all permissions in general_. Anyone have ideas to clarify?
author: lucacasonato

body:
Current:
```
[y/n/a/d] (y = yes, allow; n = no, deny; a = yes to all, allow all; d = no to all, deny all)
```

Suggestion (80 characters max. for `write` permissions):
```
[y/n/a/d] (y = yes; n = no; a = allow all write perms; d = deny all write perms)
```
author: iuioiua

body:
> I'm not fully grepping it - `is_all` seems very ambigious, what are the situations where this flag would be `false`?

I've renamed `is_all` to `is_for_all_perm_values`. It'd be false when the user enters `y` for a single permission value.

Other suggestions:

- prompt:

Allow? [y/n/A] (y = yes, allow; n = no, deny; A = allow all read/write/env... permissions) >
```

body:
@aapoalas  While implementing the extension->media-type mapping function in deno_ast, I've noticed a potential future problem this PR is introducing.

I'm using `deno run` as an example, this affect all subcommands with `--ext`.

We want `deno run file.xxx`  to have the same effect as `deno run --ext xxx file`.  

`deno run file.ts` ends up at `MediaType::from(module_specifier)`, which maps **file extension to `MediaType`**.  
`deno run --ext ts file` ends up at `MediaType::from_content_type(module_specifier, content_type)`, which maps the **media type string to `MediaType`**.

There isn't a media-type string for every file extension, particularly the `.d.*` file extensions don't have their own media-type strings.  
These file extensions are mapped to an ambiguous media-type such as `text/typescript`, and behavior could differ compared to invoking `deno` with a file with extension. This could result in failures, eg. in deno_ast's `parsing.rs get_syntax()`, which has different handling for `MediaType::TypeScript` and `MediaType::Dts`.

`.d.*` files aren't relevant right now, because we're limiting the possible values for `--ext` with clap.  
As far as I can tell, the values currently allowed for `--ext` have unique media-type strings, meaning this is not a problem right now. Should the list of allowed `--ext` values be extended in the future, this could be a bug with no quick solution.

---

The limiting factor is the media-type string. I chose to use the media-type strings, because that's how `FetchCacher` could communicate types to `deno_graph` already. Ditching the media-type strings and finding a new way to transport `MediaType` to `deno_graph` directly could solve this problem. I can't estimate the feasibility for this change with my current knowledge.

The impact right now are some comments in deno_ast explaining why some `MediaType`s have unique mappings to media-type strings, while others don't. I don't have that PR ready yet.

I thought I'd let you know about this issue so you have a chance to revoke your approval before this hits in deno_ast.

body:
Implementation wise - looks good. However when adding new APIs to Deno namespace, we also start with making them unstable (requiring `--unstable` flag to be present) and only gathering some feedback we stabilize the API. Could you change the PR in such a way to this API requires the unstable check and the types are only available in `lib.deno_unstable.d.ts`? EDIT: That way we can land and release this unstable API in the next patch release, without having to wait for next minor release.

> There's also process.uptime, but I believe Deno.uptime should report stats for the OS itself, not the process, and we can always add Deno.processUptime or similar method when it'll become necessary.

Seems it's not needed - `performance.now()` returns a number of ms from the start of the process, so it could be used for that purpose.

body:
@hawkinsw Sorry for the very late reply. Let me elaborte:

A couple of months ago, Chrome wanted to ship request body streaming in `fetch`. Chrome did not want to support duplex streaming though (streaming both upload and download at the same time). The current version of the `fetch` spec specifies only this half duplex streaming. In Deno however, we have supported full duplex streaming since we added streaming support to Fetch. This meant that for the same `ReadableStream` request body, there would be differing behavior between Chrome and Deno.

At this point I suggested we introduce the `duplex` flag to the request constructor to let users explicitly specify that they were aware they were using half duplex, and to ensure that there is a path for Chrome to support full duplex in the future. The result of this was, that to use upload streaming in Chrome, users would have to specify `duplex: "half"` in their request constructor.

Deno however already defaulted to `duplex: "full"` (which is not yet specified). As such for us it doesn't make any sense to introduce the `duplex` flag, until we either specify `duplex: "full"` or we implement support for half duplex behind the `duplex: "half"` flag.

Additionally, because of backwards compatibility, we can't add the requirement that users must specify `duplex: "full"` for full duplex, because our current behavior is to default to full duplex _without_ the flag being specified.

So what does that mean for this PR? I see two paths forward:

1. we introduce `duplex` now and default it to "full" if it is not specified explicitly (going against spec), and we throw on `duplex: "half"` (because it is not implemented)
2. we don't do anything until `duplex: "full"` is specified in the spec

I am sort of leaning towards option 2.
author: lucacasonato

body: For the record, it doesn't seem like `duplex: "full"` will be specified in the WHATWG fetch spec anytime soon. However, [WinterCG](https://wintercg.org/) is working on [a fork of the fetch spec](https://github.com/wintercg/fetch) meant for server-side runtimes which will most likely include `duplex: "full"` at some point.
author: andreubotella

body:
Thank you to both of you for the engagement. I am mostly concerned with
being able to use a standard set of code to do feature detection of support
for request body streaming. I am helping to get this used by the Go wasm
backend and we had to write code to detect whether the runtime supports it
(see https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/458395). We used the
detection code posted by Jake (
https://developer.chrome.com/articles/fetch-streaming-requests/) (after
fixing a bug, which I am sure you immediately see). In order to use that
code we needed to add this patch to Deno.

So, my goals are twofold:

1. To help Deno in any way that I can (!!)
2. Find a reasonable way to implement feature detection for streaming
request bodies that we can incorporate into the Go wasm backend.

I am sorry that is such a circuitous response. You two are the experts. I
am just here to help!
Will

body:
main:
```
test bench_op_async   ... bench:     576,647 ns/iter (+/- 35,813)
```

this PR:
```
test bench_op_async   ... bench:     613,201 ns/iter (+/- 32,075)
```

There seems to be a clear decrease in performance in this benchmark. Now, `bench_op_async` only goes through the ready and non-deferred match arm of `queue_async_op`, so this points finely enough at a perf issue in the change, but it also leaves the rest of branches unchecked. Are there some other specific async op benchmarks that I can use for the other codepaths?

body:
@kamilogorek it's most likely coming from these lines:
```
osUptime: __bootstrap.os.osUptime(ops.op_node_unstable_os_uptime),
```
This is directly calling `osUptime` op during bootstrapping process. I think we need to remove `__bootstrap.os.osUptime` and replace it with `__bootstrap.os.createOsUptime()` that will accept `opFn` as an argument

body:
AFAIU ref/unref do the same thing as release/aquire functions but the later also consumes/takes ownership of the tsfn handle.

https://nodejs.org/api/n-api.html#napi_unref_threadsafe_function
author: littledivy

body:
>AFAIU ref/unref do the same thing as release/aquire functions but the later also consumes/takes ownership of the tsfn handle.

In Node `ref`/`unref` is completely unrelated to `acquire`/`release` API — they do not affect `ThreadsafeFunction` reference counter at all.  [Source for `unref`](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/main/src/node_api.cc#L316) vs [`release`](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/main/src/node_api.cc#L254).
author: SevInf

body:
> > AFAIU ref/unref do the same thing as release/aquire functions but the later also consumes/takes ownership of the tsfn handle.
> 
> In Node `ref`/`unref` is completely unrelated to `acquire`/`release` API — they do not affect `ThreadsafeFunction` reference counter at all. [Source for `unref`](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/main/src/node_api.cc#L316) vs [`release`](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/main/src/node_api.cc#L254).

Thanks for pointing this out, we discussed this offline earlier today and decided that this integration needs to be mostly rewritten. I'll look into that.

body:
> The total coverage % is calculated by getting the average branch and line coverage and applying some weighting to it (20% branches, 80% lines).

Is there a prior art to that?
author: bartlomieju

body:
> > The total coverage % is calculated by getting the average branch and line coverage and applying some weighting to it (20% branches, 80% lines).
> 
> Is there a prior art to that?

Nope, I was trying to simulate how Coveralls calculates this but I got it wrong. What they actually use is:
$aggregate\ coverage = (lines\ hit + branches\ hit) / (relevant\ lines + relevant\ branches)$
([source](https://github.com/lemurheavy/coveralls-public/issues/1631#issuecomment-1302712793))

Alternatively, `nyc/istanbul` calculate the individual coverages for lines/branches/functions/statements and then if any of those fall under a threshold it throws. So I guess we could go either way.
author: GJZwiers

body: Thanks for approving @aapoalas! I am OK with landing this as is, although I had a few concerns left which is why I kept it as a draft. The current implementation works but is also fairly basic, other implementations have separate flags for each type of coverage e.g. `--branches` while I used a more general `--threshold` flag. I can image Deno may want to improve on that in the future, also by somehow collecting statement coverage and including it. Furthermore, I found some comments in an older PR that stated the approach was too tighly coupled to the reporter: https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/9826#pullrequestreview-615948006, and I think I took more-or-less the same approach. It was two years ago though, so perhaps ideas on that have changed a bit.

body:
> I think it's safe to skip handling of modules in non-global realms, would you agree @andreubotella?

I guess that depends on the use case for module snapshotting. For `deno compile` / Deploy use cases, a module's initial evaluation might create `ShadowRealm`s which would need to persist state in the snapshot, which might include module state. But if that's not a significant use case for now, then it's fine.

However, in this use case it would probably be good to make sure that any non-GC'd realms have an empty module map when snapshotting. If this PR lands before #15760 I'll make sure to add that check.

body:
Ok, this passes, and unfortunately we can't use PathBuf::canonicalize(), since it follows symlinks. So, using the URL module will have to remain to canonicalize the string only. But this _does_ use argv[0] if present as exe_name, and joins it to cwd if it's relative (say, './foo').

The major risk here is changing behavior someone might be relying on. On linux, symlinks resolve to their target instead of themselves. The upside is that it will behave the same across platforms, now.

body: Would it not make more sense to reuse the `importMap` key?: It already exists, reusing it would make a lot of sense as a "link or embed" type of key, and reuse would also avoid the tricky question of precedence. On the other end, `dependencies` key is more familiar to the intended audience for the aimed `imports` usage. Finally, this top-level embedding offers no clear route to possible spec changes with regards to eg. multiple import maps.
author: aapoalas

body:
> Would it not make more sense to reuse the importMap key?: It already exists, reusing it would make a lot of sense as a "link or embed" type of key

I disagree, overloading the meaning of the value with either and object or string to a path would be more confusing.

> and reuse would also avoid the tricky question of precedence.

It's not important, nor tricky. `"imports"` in `deno.json` will be the recommended pattern - everything else is side cases.

> On the other end, dependencies key is more familiar to the intended audience for the aimed imports usage. Finally, this top-level embedding offers no clear route to possible spec changes with regards to eg. multiple import maps.

I don't see the import map spec adding a `"fmt"` or `"tasks"` fields. I think it'll be fine and people will appreciate typing less.
author: ry

body:
> I don't see the import map spec adding a `"fmt"` or `"tasks"` fields. I think it'll be fine and people will appreciate typing less.

No, but the spec is definitely still actively looking to support multiple import maps being automatically combined. With `"importMap"` it would be a simple thing to provide the option of defining an array of import maps, or possibly an array of paths. With `"imports"` its not immediately clear how multiple import maps' import objects would be supported. If / when multiple import maps then do become supported one way or another, it will call into question how `"imports"` and `"importMap"` interact with each other: Should they be combined, or do they remain as separate things?

If / when multiple import maps do become spec, the likely use case will of course be one where most of the import maps are referred ones; import maps defined by dependencies that the application simply uses to "understand" the dependency ES modules (their imports). Only a single import map will likely belong to the application itself. In this case one logical setup would be an array of import maps where all but one are references of some kind (eg. string paths) to the dependency import maps, and then one of them would be an "embedded" import map belonging to the application itself. `deno.json` would then need to somehow handle combining some sort of list of import maps with the `"imports"` and `"scopes"` keys, including the order since the order of import maps will likely be important to the composition algorithm.
author: aapoalas

body:
> No, but the spec is definitely still actively looking to support multiple import maps being automatically combined. With `"importMap"` it would be a simple thing to provide the option of defining an array of import maps, or possibly an array of paths. With `"imports"` its not immediately clear how multiple import maps' import objects would be supported. If / when multiple import maps then do become supported one way or another, it will call into question how `"imports"` and `"importMap"` interact with each other: Should they be combined, or do they remain as separate things?

@aapoalas I think the deno.json could be treated as the first import map, but that said if we run into problems we can deprecate this in the future.
author: dsherret

body:
> @aapoalas I think the deno.json could be treated as the first import map, but that said if we run into problems we can deprecate this in the future.

I haven't read the actual "most probable" composition algorithm but I think the first import map is the "weakest", ie. working similarly to how an object spread works: `{ ...first, ...second }`. That being said, as I tied to mention but apparently failed to make clear enough, the order of import maps matters and there is at least a chance that a applications might have different needs for how they order their import maps, so choosing "first" or "last" may not work for everyone. It's at least possible that someone might even want a specific location, eg. third out of five or whatever.

I do indeed foresee that this choice will not last once (if / when) the import maps spec finally settles on how composition is to be done: I guess then eg. `"importMaps"` can be added (`--import-map` will need to become multi-configurable but that's a simple change), and then the order of precedence would maybe be command line argument > `"imports"` & `"scopes"` > `"importMaps"` > `"importMap"`.

But, seeing as this was merged I guess that's for me to simply wait and see. Deprecations are always easy anyway.

> A bit sad to see N-API become stable before FFI, but on the other hand FFI is nowhere near stable so I think this is fine :D

Yeah - I kinda agree with that, but the thing is Node-API is defined and if something doesn't work as expected is simply a bug. FFI on the other hand is still being worked on (mostly by you 😛) so I think it's fair that it would still remain unstable. 

I'm still thinking if it should use other flag than `--allow-ffi`
author: bartlomieju

body:
> Yeah - I kinda agree with that, but the thing is Node-API is defined and if something doesn't work as expected is simply a bug. FFI on the other hand is still being worked on (mostly by you stuck_out_tongue) so I think it's fair that it would still remain unstable.

Yeah, N-API simply _is_ a stable interface to implement, there's no way around it :)

> I'm still thinking if it should use other flag than `--allow-ffi`

This may probably a good idea.

I'm bouncing between ideas of splitting `--allow-ffi` internally into two (`--allow-ffi-extend` for opening new libraries and creating new functions from pointers, and `--allow-ffi-use` for the rest, roughly) and implementing some sort of "key" API to enable usage of some FFI APIs with a "key" created using the `--allow-ffi` permission which can then be ditched.

@aapoalas On the output format. 
I choose a flat structure because IMO that will be a usual way to consume results: 

- Run bench on CI
- Save results to the Elastic/Prometheus/etc
- Query that data to build charts/send alerts/etc

A flat structure is much better in use cases like that. 

But I am ready to change it if my assumptions are wrong.

body:
Absolutely incredible how much difference a single atomic load can do... wow...

https://github.com/rust-lang/futures-rs/blob/9c6c9a7c1fe10229d29a8f7da6aab4b3fe2d32cd/futures-util/src/stream/futures_unordered/mod.rs#L157
author: lucacasonato

body:
> Absolutely incredible how much difference a single atomic load can do... wow...
> 
> https://github.com/rust-lang/futures-rs/blob/9c6c9a7c1fe10229d29a8f7da6aab4b3fe2d32cd/futures-util/src/stream/futures_unordered/mod.rs#L157

I remember Ben complaining about `FuturesUnordered` in the past. We should think about having our own version of that struct - we only need it to handle single-thread runtime so all these atomics won't be necessary. @littledivy would you be interested in looking into it?
author: bartlomieju

body:
@lucacasonato @bartlomieju 

> Absolutely incredible how much difference a single atomic load can do... wow...

You are misunderstanding what have_unpolled_ops is for. When an (op) future is created we need to make sure that it gets polled at least once to set up the waker etc. So have_unpolled_ops is *only* true when there are ops just added to pending_ops that have never been polled.

pending_ops.is_empty() will be false when there are any ongoing ops; it’ll almost never be true because with no pending ops deno would exit.

So this has nothing to do with the cost of an atomic load.
author: piscisaureus

body:
> You are misunderstanding what have_unpolled_ops is for. When an (op) future is created we need to make sure that it gets polled at least once to set up the waker etc. So have_unpolled_ops is _only_ true when there are ops just added to pending_ops that have never been polled.
> 
> pending_ops.is_empty() will be false when there are any ongoing ops; it’ll almost never be true because with no pending ops deno would exit.
> 
> So this has nothing to do with the cost of an atomic load.

Regarding this, should the bool perhaps be renamed? Or at least a comment added. Tough the `unpolled` naming is technically correct, it's a bit easy to misunderstand in this way. My mind immediately goes to `uninitialized` which is of course technically kind of hand-wavy but might serve as a more factual representation of what the value actually stands for.

Though, a doc comment would probably do that just as well or better.

Thanks. This seems like a reasonable suggestion, but I think we could revisit this later after we get initial feedback on the issue. `--side-module` flag would be valid only in case of self-contained binary, and we currently have no easy way to change error messages depending if we're running `deno run <>` or self-contained binary.
author: bartlomieju

body: Tested this locally and it works as expected! This is a great feature that I think many users will find useful.
author: bartlomieju

body:
> Thanks. This seems like a reasonable suggestion, but I think we could revisit this later after we get initial feedback on the issue. `--side-module` flag would be valid only in case of self-contained binary, and we currently have no easy way to change error messages depending if we're running `deno run <>` or self-contained binary.

The error thrown if a module is not present on the eszip is thrown from the `EmbeddedModuleLoader`, so we know that can only happen from a self-contained binary.

My concern was about whether a self-contained binary that's shipped to end users with no access to the sources should have instructions on how to fix a bug that the end users will not be able to fix. But I guess that is also an issue that can happen with permissions flags.

body:
I agree with @andreubotella that the flag would be useful for non-analysable imports but... It's actually also possible to do that without the flag:
```ts
const EXTRA_SIDE_MODULES = [
  () => import("./side-module-1.ts"),
  () => import("../root-side-module.ts"),
];
```

It's probably a matter of taste which is more elegant: Use the existing language features that we have, or add a configuration flag or option.

For some reason, all our walking/traversing of the graph in the CLI skips over statically analyzable dynamic imports, but they're in the `ModuleGraph`. I'll review this now.

Thanks. You should review commit 4d8e192ea8a881efb3a51ec82d6bf58587c43e2b, since the previous ones come from #17657. I built this PR off of that one not because the actual code changes need to be based on that PR, but because that way I can add to one of the worker tests from that PR.

> I agree with @andreubotella that the flag would be useful for non-analysable imports but... It's actually also possible to do that without the flag:
> 
> ```ts
> const EXTRA_SIDE_MODULES = [
>   () => import("./side-module-1.ts"),
>   () => import("../root-side-module.ts"),
> ];
> ```
> 
> It's probably a matter of taste which is more elegant: Use the existing language features that we have, or add a configuration flag or option.

This seems like a hard-to-explain non-obvious hack, if you're reading the code. It feels a lot cleaner to me to say "`deno compile` will try to detect dynamic imports, but it can't always, so use this flag just in case".
author: andreubotella

body:
> For some reason, all our walking/traversing of the graph in the CLI skips over statically analyzable dynamic imports

Not sure if you're talking about somewhere specific, but the logic of skipping over (even statically analyzable) dynamic imports exists for example in startup graph validation because we don't want to elevate dynamic module slot errors until the runtime import of that module happens.
author: nayeemrmn

body:
> Not sure if you're talking about somewhere specific, but the logic of skipping over (even statically analyzable) dynamic imports exists for example in startup graph validation because we don't want to elevate dynamic module slot errors until the runtime import of that module happens.

Yeah, why is that done? I was thinking that maybe someone would be downloading code on the fly, but that wouldn't work. It seems to shift an error that could be spotted at startup to being surfaced later on. Anyway, in this case we can make it work by updating eszip to not skip over dynamic imports: https://github.com/denoland/eszip/pull/115
author: dsherret

body:
> Yeah, why is that done? I was thinking that maybe someone would be downloading code on the fly, but that wouldn't work. It seems to shift an error that could be spotted at startup to being surfaced later on.

Because the import might not happen, and even be conditioned on whether or not it would error:
```ts
if (fs.exists("plugin.ts")) {
  await import("./plugin.ts")
}
```

Yeah the eszip patch should be sufficient for the `deno compile` packaging issue
author: nayeemrmn

body: @nayeemrmn that makes sense. Thanks for the background!

body:
Thanks for the review @bartlomieju, I tried to add tests and discovered another inconsistency/bug. Let's consider this code using dynamic import with an invalid name in try-catch:
```js
try { await import('npm:does-not-exist-and-will-fail') } catch (e) { console.log('This should print'); }
```
In theory, `import()` should throw an exception which should be caught and program execution should continue. But it does not, unfortunately. Please note that importing invalid `node:` module like that does throw properly.

Example:
```js
Deno 1.30.3+0aeb8bc
exit using ctrl+d, ctrl+c, or close()
REPL is running with all permissions allowed.
To specify permissions, run `deno repl` with allow flags.
> try { await import('node:does-not-exist-and-will-fail') } catch (e) { console.log('This prints'); } console.log('This prints too');
This prints
This prints too
undefined
> try { await import('npm:does-not-exist-and-will-fail') } catch (e) { console.log('This does not print'); } console.log('This does not print');
error: npm package 'does-not-exist-and-will-fail' does not exist
> 
```
author: bershanskiy

body: While debugging this I noticed that deno internally does not really track who requested what so it appears to fail the whole module if a single import failed. That makes sense for static import at the top of the file, but does not seem to be semantically correct for dynamic imports. I spent some time the last two days looking into this, but I don't think I can create a one-liner fix for it: either I'm looking in the wrong places (most likely, since I never interacted with deno internals) or fix would require substantial logic change.

author: aapoalas

> 
> You recently changed the extension names to use static external string resources, right? Would it be possible to "pull those back" to avoid string deserialisation and access the static string pointer directly? (I'm presuming that the hefty amount of strings here would be the extension names.)

That's a good question, we should investigate that. I think we might be able to use `v8::String::write_one_byte`
author: bartlomieju

body:
> That's a good question, we should investigate that. I think we might be able to use `v8::String::write_one_byte`

With the C++ API it's possible to do one better:
1. Get the external one byte string resource from a String: https://v8docs.nodesource.com/node-18.2/d2/db3/classv8_1_1_string.html#a790ceedd8a57ed7b12b1c2c2e6775faf
2. Get the data pointer and length of the one byte string contained within the resource: https://v8docs.nodesource.com/node-18.2/d9/db3/classv8_1_1_string_1_1_external_one_byte_string_resource.html#ad21f4b84b5c0731695992e6701defaa1
3. Use the string as you see fit without a single copy.

The Rust API does not currently offer the necessary APIs to get a resource from a String but it would not be hard to implement those. For future reference it would be a good idea to somehow implement a C++/Rust bridging Resource base / trait that could be used by both Deno and Embedders.

One mistake in the C++ API that Node unfortunately exposes is that there is no way to tell apart different subclasses of the OneByteStringResource. Thus, if an embedder uses this subclass and extends its interface in their own way (say, they link it with a reference counted string struct), then they must keep track of each of the pointers of these instances they've created. Otherwise they cannot safely "downcast" lest they accidentally operate on an instance that was not created by them but by the runtime itself.

body:
while `cargo test --package deno --bin deno -- args::config_file::tests` passes,

some `cargo test` fails with

<details>
<summary>missing expeced `{name}` in serde error</summary>

```
---- integration::bench::bench_with_malformed_config stdout ----
deno_exe path /home/scarf/repo/deno/target/debug/deno
deno_exe args bench --config bench/collect/deno.malformed.jsonc
deno_exe cwd "/home/scarf/repo/deno/cli/tests/testdata"
output path /home/scarf/repo/deno/cli/tests/testdata/bench/collect_with_malformed_config.out
thread 'integration::bench::bench_with_malformed_config' panicked at 'assertion failed: `(left == right)`

Diff < left / right > :
 error: Failed to parse "bench" configuration
 
 Caused by:
<    unknown field `dont_know_this_field`
>    unknown field `dont_know_this_field`, expected `files`
```

```
---- integration::fmt::fmt_with_malformed_config stdout ----
deno_exe path /home/scarf/repo/deno/target/debug/deno
deno_exe args fmt --config fmt/deno.malformed.jsonc
deno_exe cwd "/home/scarf/repo/deno/cli/tests/testdata"
output path /home/scarf/repo/deno/cli/tests/testdata/fmt/fmt_with_malformed_config.out
thread 'integration::fmt::fmt_with_malformed_config' panicked at 'assertion failed: `(left == right)`

Diff < left / right > :
 error: Failed to parse "fmt" configuration
 
 Caused by:
<    unknown field `dont_know_this_field`
>    unknown field `dont_know_this_field`, expected `options` or `files` 
```

</details>

it's caused because [`deny_unknown_fields` is not compatible with `flatten`](https://serde.rs/attr-flatten.html).
also related issue: https://github.com/serde-rs/serde/issues/1957

i guess there could be following solutions:
1. do not use "flatten". should work, but at the expense of larger code
2. keep it as is. cons: it will confuse users when they have a missing field.
3. try to 'fix' serde. most likely not possible.
author: scarf005

body: Hmm, I guess I'd go with option #1: Define the flat structure manually to get better error reporting out of serde.

body:
The main argument for separate v4/v6 methods is that TS then knows whether you got back a `MulticastV4Membership` or `MulticastV6Membership`, the latter having no `setTTL` function.

This could also be done with overloads, but this feels less ergonomic to me as it's not hugely obvious that joining an IPv6 multicast group uses a scope ID rather than an IP address, and it would be really easy to run `listener.joinMulticast("ff02::fb", "::1")` (where the two string params would indicate the IPv4 variant) and be confused why it's not working.

body:
I have decided that this is not a good idea: Adding something like 80 bytes to an already fairly large static size of `Buffer` AKA `Uint8Array` plus the internal `ArrayBuffer`, for what is a fairly small performance upside (and even some downsides, especially in the more necessary parts).

The proper way to improve the performance of `Buffer` read and write APIs would be to implement them in Rust.

body: @20k-ultra Your benchmarks are measuring total execution time, only a very small part of it is dirname execution time. Please add a benchmark in `cli/bench`. Example: https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/cli/bench/op_now.js
author: littledivy

body:
Summary of this PR is that progress is blocked by limitations of string handling between JS and Rust. The issue is that given the computation being pretty straight forward, the passing of the string is the hottest part of the port to Rust so any algorithm changes have diminishing returns.

For example, the existing Javascript function ran with the benchmark produces:

```bash
$ deno (pure JS) run cli/bench/node/path/op_node_path_posix_dirname.js
time 148 ms rate 67567567
time 143 ms rate 69930069
time 115 ms rate 86956521
time 117 ms rate 85470085
time 117 ms rate 85470085
time 116 ms rate 86206896
time 117 ms rate 85470085
time 117 ms rate 85470085
```

simply calling an op without passing a string in the parameters and returning `()` right away for the Result yields:

```bash
$ deno_no_str_passed_return_ok run cli/bench/node/path/op_node_path_posix_dirname.js
time 165 ms rate 60606060
time 170 ms rate 58823529
time 158 ms rate 63291139
time 160 ms rate 62500000
time 161 ms rate 62111801
time 161 ms rate 62111801
time 161 ms rate 62111801
time 160 ms rate 62500000
```

Keep in mind this is over 10 million calls but the results still indicate this is already slower without even doing anything or passing a string.

Not performing any algorithm on the data still yields poor results, even with a few recent string optimizations such as 489cafd5ba4ecd3fc9d8fa393b17e50015ec4707, 38555a6a0fe9a7235776afe0ebfb2a24dc518391, and most importantly, passing `&str` instead of having serde deserialize to a `&Value` type (this was to allow us to port `assert_path` function) We get:

```bash
$ deno_pass_str_return_ok_with_18051 run cli/bench/node/path/op_node_path_posix_dirname.js
time 766 ms rate 13054830
time 777 ms rate 12870012
time 776 ms rate 12886597
time 778 ms rate 12853470
time 783 ms rate 12771392
time 799 ms rate 12515644
time 803 ms rate 12453300
time 798 ms rate 12531328
```

---

I didn't want to change anything from the original algorithm but I have a few optimization I wanted to try but we still won't get to match the JS runtime.

First optimization, we can skip counting the length of chars by just naively getting the first char (which we do anyways to check for a root). If checking the char_at_index returns None, we know since we just checked the first index that the slice is therefore empty. Side note; `len()` is constant and `count()` is linear because count consumes the iterator.

```rust
  let has_root = match char_at_index(path, 0) {
    Some(char) => char as u32 == constants::CHAR_FORWARD_SLASH,
    None => return Ok(String::from(".")), <--- since we failed to get the fist char, we know the slice is empty
  };
```

Secondly, the `path.char_indices().rev()` is searching the path slice for a `/`, starting from the end of the slice. This is a naive search implementation and so using Rust's builtin [rfind](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/string/struct.String.html#method.rfind) is likely faster. Plus, removes a lot of LOC. *Might need a bit more work to pass tests.*

```rust
    let end = match path.rfind('/') {
        Some(0) => None, <-- avoid matching to the first /
        Some(n) => Some(n),
        _ => None,
    };
```

Finally, the match block at the end creates a string into heap which is slow. Using a string literal will avoid this

```rust
  match (end, has_root) {
    (None, true) => Ok("/".to_string()),
    (None, false) => Ok(".".to_string()),
    (Some(1), true) => Ok("//".to_string()),
    (Some(ending), _) => Ok(path.chars().take(ending).collect()),
  }
```

---

Some other optimizations...using a String with a fixed length will add gains since the String's size will be known and makes memory allocation easier.

```rust
    let mut dirname = String::with_capacity(path.len());

    match (end, has_root) {
        (None, true) => dirname.push_str("/"),
        (None, false) => dirname.push_str("."),
        (Some(1), true) => dirname.push_str("//"),
        (Some(ending), _) => dirname.extend(path.chars().take(ending)),
    };

    Ok(dirname)
```

The final result of these optimizations are about ~150 ms which was fun to get, they are in https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/17993/commits/98ed781acb738100d10559189bf48ca625aaa47a.

```bash
$ latest_main_17993 run cli/bench/node/path/op_node_path_posix_dirname.js
time 1453 ms rate 6882312
time 1470 ms rate 6802721
time 1477 ms rate 6770480
time 1484 ms rate 6738544
time 1491 ms rate 6706908
time 1495 ms rate 6688963
time 1507 ms rate 6635700

$ latest_main_17993_optimizations run cli/bench/node/path/op_node_path_posix_dirname.js 
time 1294 ms rate 7727975
time 1310 ms rate 7633587
time 1315 ms rate 7604562
time 1321 ms rate 7570022
time 1324 ms rate 7552870
time 1327 ms rate 7535795
time 1325 ms rate 7547169
```

@aapoalas if you have any comments regarding my comment here. Would love to hear what you think.

I'll probably close this PR soon.
author: 20k-ultra

body:
Hey, sorry it has taken me time to come and comment. I don't have much to give, except that the lowest baseline level I've ever gotten out of a no-parameter-no-return ops was around 6 or 7 nanoseconds per call. Extrapolating from that directly would give about 60 ms per 10 million calls, which could then theoretically overtake the JS benchmarks.

But, that is of course with doing no work and passing no values one way or another. If returning a string is needed, like with `dirname`, `join` or `basename` then we're already stuck in a situation that is really hard to win: For JS code, at its core the code can just create a new struct that defines a "rope" ie. a combination of slices of strings. No copying is needed. With the `&str` parameter type we can get zero-copy parameters (assuming the strïng is Latin-1 and encoded as OneByte in V8) but any returns must make a copy.

Functions like `isAbsolute` might get close or perhaps even overtake a plain JS call since they need not return any copies. Even then, unfortunately, the zero-copy passing of strings would again be conditional on the strings actually being OneByte encoded in V8, which may not be the case even if the passed-in string consists only of Latin-1 characters. (eg. Taking a substring from a string with unicode characters will not re-encode from UTF-16 to OneByte (Latin-1), so you can end up with a fully OneByte string slice that is still UTF-16 encoded.)

author: bartlomieju

body:
I have a couple of small concerns here:

 * First is w/potential for data corruption with no journal enabled. In the case where the database is un-openable, does the user have enough information to know to delete the correct file? While the journal is no guarantee of freedom-from-corruption, it's possible that no journaling at all might present some additional risk of corruption that even a more basic journal might protect against.

 * Second, I'm not sure if this is the correct way to disable the WAL for an existing database. From what I can understand from research, we're supposed to migrate to WAL-disabled by issuing `pragma journal_mode = DELETE`. I am not certain if `journal_mode = OFF` silently drops the WAL (which in theory should just force us to re-cache a bunch of data) or if it is ignored and continues to use the WAL.

I'm building a test database to investigate further.

body:
> I think we should be wary of introducing too much meta-programming. This patch is a net reduction of ~150 lines. But it introduces a lot of complex in macro programming. Because there's no runtime benefit, it's not obvious to me this is a clear win.

There's no runtime benefit in this PR, but there will be in a follow up - we will reduce amount of vector allocations significantly when registering extensions on startup and thus make it cheaper to create the main worker instance - there's not gonna be that much difference but I expect to see around 0.5ms improvement in the startup time.
author: bartlomieju

body:
@bartlomieju The static ops list can done without this macro too.

Moreover, there are places where static array won't work. https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/e6fe1630f0e6cbc16ccfda5a58cbe8ecdfea2694/core/runtime.rs#L765 Places where the list is mutated or collected into a new Vec...so I think we should first figure out `&'static [OpDecl]`.
author: littledivy

body:
> @bartlomieju The static ops list can done without this macro too.
> 
> Moreover, there are places where static array won't work.
> 
> https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/e6fe1630f0e6cbc16ccfda5a58cbe8ecdfea2694/core/runtime.rs#L765
> 
> Places where the list is mutated or collected into a new Vec...so I think we should first figure out `&'static [OpDecl]`.

The `&'static [OpDecl]` problem is pretty hairy to tackle without doing this macro and cleanup first, I think.

Barring `const` OpDecls being available, we have three options:

 * Create a large allocation at boot time to hold all the OpDecls, and leak that allocation to create a static lifetime reference
 * Use #[ctor] to initialize the ops before rust's main starts0
 * Use OnceCell to initialize a static mut array on first access

I'd lean towards the first one, but regardless of which option we choose, it'll require a massive refactoring across the codebase unless we have all the `OpDecl`s handled in one uniform way as we do with this PR. As all the "fast" extensions in the snapshot now have a common implementation, it would be trivial for us to interrogate the size of the `OpDecl` list in a `const` manner, allocate a single chunk of memory for all of them (either at runtime or in the compiler) and fill that chunk of memory in the most efficient way possible.

Without this PR landing in every case, we'd basically be making changes to ~20+ op lists across the whole codebase.

Either way, having a macro that's not perfect and exactly what we'll want six months from now is better than nothing, as iterating and experimenting becomes 20x cheaper.

body:
> once we install listener for `ctrl+c` then even if it's dropped the process won't exit anymore on SIGINT.
> 
> Is there a chance this can happen with this change?

The only consequence is it's no longer possible to interrupt the process in the window between the test runner finishing up and the process normally ending after that. Or the transition window for `deno test --watch`.

If there was a real reason to overcome this upstream limitation, it's the effect on `Deno.removeSignalHandler("SIGINT")` and having compatible behaviour for Node's `process.on("SIGINT")` IMO.

> Also how will it interact if the code in tests registers a signal listener for SIGINT event?

The user's handler doesn't run because the process exits in our handler. I think that's fine, they should use subprocesses / integration tests for things like that.
author: nayeemrmn

body:
Thanks for detailed explanation, totally makes sense.

> If there was a real reason to overcome this upstream limitation, it's the effect on Deno.removeSignalHandler("SIGINT") and having compatible behaviour for Node's process.on("SIGINT") IMO.

Agreed, we should have one "custom" listener that just switches its actions once `Deno.removeSignalHandler("SIGINT")` is called.

In addition, if `opts.hostname` is not specified and `:port` is included in `opts.host`, there will also be a throw error in node.js, so we only need to adjust the priority of the `host` value.

body:
> I'm not sure if there's a blob test for this already, but if not could you also test that importing the same blob URL in the same run twice gets the same module?
> 
> Something like this:
> 
> ```ts
> export let data = 3;
> export const setData = d => { data = d; };
> ```

> maybe it would also mean that blob URL imports become unique instances.

@aapoalas I'm not sure what you're suggesting to test,
- To check that it's the same _instance_ we could just do `await import(blobUrl) == await import(blobUrl)`, but that's an engine/`deno_core` guarantee which doesn't query CLI's module loader.
- We could check that `await import(blobUrl)` and `await import(blobUrl + "#1")` give modules with the same behaviour... still seems esoteric. That implies the blob url might have been unbound after the first import for some reason (if anything that would occur right after creation leading to the first import failing).

Note that file URLs already opt out of `FileFetcher`'s cache, it's a really high level one with like two beneath and one above it.

Actually, I've questioned why `FileFetcher::cache` is active for standard execution (e.g. `run`/`test` without `--watch`) given `ProcState::graph_container`. Pretty sure the only usage that really needs it is the LSP's `ModuleRegistry`. That would be a worthwhile investigation.

body: I haven't looked into the root cause much, Why do the FFI uncaught exceptions not let the event loop quit gently?
author: littledivy

body:
> I haven't looked into the root cause much, Why do the FFI uncaught exceptions not let the event loop quit gently?

This is not about uncaught exceptions coming from FFI at all – notice how the test I added has an exception being thrown from the main module, completely separate from the FFI call. This is also not at all about the *Deno* event loop, which AFAIK deals with FFI just fine, but about the *tokio* event loop.

When a tokio runtime is dropped, it will usually wait for any blocking spawned tasks to finish. But for FFI non-blocking calls, this might literally take forever. `shutdown_timeout` sets a maximum timeout to wait for those tasks, and if they still haven't finished by that point, it just returns. It doesn't kill those tasks, since apparently it's impossible to kill a thread without that thread's cooperation, but what else can you do at that point. If we're exiting from the main web worker in `deno run`, we will reach the end of the `main` function shortly after dropping the tokio runtime anyway, which will kill those threads.

If it's not possible to kill a thread without its cooperation, there's really nothing we can do to kill a non-blocking FFI call. But I don't know the specifics of thread-killing across OS's.

body:
> I wonder if we really need selection by arrow keys. That's not aligned to what we do with the permission prompt.

This is also with the goal of consistency with the browser where possible — the browser shows the options and allows selecting between them, albeit with Tab rather than arrow keys (I think using Tab would be too far outside what's expected of console apps though). It's also with the secondary goal of being beginner-friendly, as the native modals are frequently used in beginner tutorials as a simple way of getting user input.

If consensus is still that this isn't necessary, what would be the ideal interaction and message text? E.g. message text = [Yn], Y/y/Enter writes "y" and continues, N/n/Esc writes "n" and cancels, all other button presses are swallowed (nothing written to stdout)?
author: lionel-rowe

body:
I'm in favor of changing the default selection and adding `ESC` handling, as suggested in #18233, but I don't see enough motivation to other subtle interaction changes.

> the browser shows the options and allows selecting between them, albeit with Tab rather than arrow keys

I can't confirm this browser behavior with chrome, firefox, safari on my mac. Tab key (or any keys) doesn't seem to allow navigating between Ok/Cancel options. Or am I missing something?
author: kt3k

body:
> I can't confirm this browser behavior with chrome, firefox, safari on my mac. Tab key (or any keys) doesn't seem to allow navigating between Ok/Cancel options. Or am I missing something?

That's weird, maybe Mac's different from Windows in this respect?

Actually I just checked, and all 3 pairs of Tab/Shift+Tab, Left/Right, and Up/Down work in both Chrome and Firefox on Windows. Chrome captures the focus within the modal, whereas Firefox allows tabbing away to other browser UI elements (but not page UI elements).

It'd be surprising if Mac didn't allow _any_ keyboard-based way of switching focus between the buttons, as that'd be a pretty major accessibility problem.
author: lionel-rowe

body: Firefox and Safari on Mac allow moving focus between the buttons only when keyboard navigation is turned on in System Settings. I'd expect the same behavior from Chrome.

> Thank you for the PR @lionel-rowe and sorry for a slow turnaround. I'm interested in landing it for v1.37 (beginning of September). Could you please rebase it?

@bartlomieju No worries, I also dropped the ball on this! Happy to rebase, but I need a steer on what else needs changing. Specifically, per various comments from @kt3k: 

> 1. Returning undefined here is aligned to what browsers do when dialog boxes are disabled. ref. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/7507#pullrequestreview-489404682
> 2. NO_COLOR env var doesn't look respected?
> 3. I wonder if we really need selection by arrow keys. That's not aligned to what we do with the permission prompt.
> ...
> I can't confirm this browser behavior with chrome, firefox, safari on my mac. Tab key (or any keys) doesn't seem to allow navigating between Ok/Cancel options. Or am I missing something?

Does this list of corresponding changes look good, and is there anything else to add?

1. In non-interactive environments, return `undefined` (`alert`), `null` (`prompt`), and `false` (`confirm`), without throwing.
2. Respect `NO_COLOR` env var.
3. Remove arrow selection functionality. New interaction for `confirm` as follows:
   * Message text shows `[Yn]`
   * Pressing Y/y/Enter writes "y" to stdout and returns `true`
   * Pressing N/n/Esc writes "n" to stdout and returns `false`
   * All other button presses (arrow keys, tab, any other letter) are swallowed (nothing happens and nothing is written to stdout)
author: lionel-rowe

Pressing N/n/Esc writes "n" to stdout and returns false
All other button presses (arrow keys, tab, any other letter) are swallowed (nothing happens and nothing is written to stdout)

Yes, these all make sense to me 👍

body:
@GJZwiers

I still think should do some quoting.

I guess `.base_url().domain()` is something like js `parsedURL.host` or `parsedURL.hostname`. 

Not my case, but what about below cases?

```sh
> NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY=https://mirror deno ...
```

```sh
> NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY=https://mirror:1234/sub/path deno ...
```

```sh
> NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY=https://mirror:1234/sub/path/ deno ...
```

If user code path includes `mirror`, current PR implement will be too wide.

That's why I think need to be more specific, because npm registry is mutable, or said not-hardcoded.

```js
e.url.startsWith(
  url.pathToFileURL(
    path.join(deno_cache_dir(), 'npm', partial_parsed_NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY()) + path.sep
  )
)
```

Does this mean that REPL will still look for package.json even before there's an import? Why does it need to do that?
author: ry

body:
> Does this mean that REPL will still look for package.json even before there's an import? Why does it need to do that?

Yes, it will still do that. It's because the repl internally uses the lsp and the lsp eagerly loads it. The lsp eagerly loads it in order to have consistent/deterministic npm resolution regardless of what files are currently open or loaded.

body:
> @aapoalas For promises, we currently batch multiple completed operations in one single `opresolve` call. With callbacks for each one, it will be multiple entries into JS. I think we did investigate this some time ago.

No I mean for starting the op: What the opAsync call essentially does is that it injects the promiseid argument to the actual call's arguments. If instead it took as parameter a single callback that it called with the promiseid then the caller of opAsync could in that callback call the actual op binding function without spreading args.

body: And the point is not that ada is better or worse at WPT or anything else like that. Ada seems cool, and is specifically not my point of contention. The problem is that if there are URLs where ada and rust-url disagree on how they parse a string into the URL representation, then two different parts of the system might disagree on, for example, what the host name is. If the permissions system says the hostname is `x.com` and checks this, and the `fetch` implementation thinks the hostname is `y-x.com` and connects there, we have a problem.

body: behaviour wise, I believe we currently always give string values, never undefined, so this wouldnt match what we currently do
author: crowlKats
dsherret

body:
An example of the behaviour I am referring to which this is actually linked to is:
```ts
const pattern = new URLPattern("/books/:id?", "https://example.com");
pattern.exec("https://example.com/books")
```
The output is:
```
{
  inputs: [ "https://example.com/books" ],
  protocol: { input: "https", groups: {} },
  username: { input: "", groups: {} },
  password: { input: "", groups: {} },
  hostname: { input: "example.com", groups: {} },
  port: { input: "", groups: {} },
  pathname: { input: "/books", groups: { id: "" } },
  search: { input: "", groups: {} },
  hash: { input: "", groups: {} }
}
```
`pathname.groups.id` is not undefined, even though it should. this didnt use to be the case, and we havent implemented this change from the spec yet.

Here the spec issue: https://github.com/WICG/urlpattern/issues/162
author: crowlKats

body: Currently you can access arbitrary properties on `group` and it won't raise a typescript diagnostic, but it should so this is a fix for that. I think the `""` needing to be `undefined` is a related but separate issue?
author: dsherret

body: I would say this is definitively related as its the reason the spec changed the type from <string, string> to <string, string | undefined>, but i guess it would make sense to have this regardless of the spec change.
author: crowlKats

body: Thinking about it more, I can see how the type doesn't exactly match the current behaviour, but considering we don't have `noUncheckedIndexedAccess` enabled by default and this will eventually have properties that have a key with possibly an undefined value in the future, then let's just merge it (but in 1.33 since it might cause some annoyance).

> Are you sure this is better then using spread for arguments? This is creating an Array for every tick while arguments passed are already in an array.

I'm not sure I understand - the array was created before this PR as well - I just moved that array to be the first argument instead of relying on implicit `arguments` arg. Using spread operator shows up on flamegraphs for `opAsync()` so I'm pretty sure it will show up here as well.

I will try Matt's suggestion about using two separate arrays before landing this one.

body: This seems more like a bandaid rather than fixing the underlying problem - I believe the root cause is the one described in https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/16928 that I wanted to fix in the coming days (we now have all the ingredients to properly fix it). I'm not in favor by altering the behavior of `beforeunload` events in `deno bench`/`deno test`. What do you say I try to fix it properly with #16928 first?
author: bartlomieju

body:
I don't think it's a band-aid. It's possible there are _other_ issues that lead to the following block being needed: https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/10ae5ee26557107b22524b1a84ebb56ed7d23fb4/ext/node/polyfills/process.ts#L736-L742
But while investigating it I noticed the inconsistency of whether or not the event loop is allowed to run to completion after a test run. This PR consolidates on the typical case where it doesn't, rather than only doing it if there's a `beforeunload` handler which prevents default. It happens to fix that issue.
author: nayeemrmn

body: The only alternative to this as I described in https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/18910#issuecomment-1528771561 is to always call `worker.run_event_loop()` after the test loop. But I don't like that because it can lead to hanging with no info even though test results have been fully gathered.
author: nayeemrmn

body:
> I don't think it's a band-aid. It's possible there are _other_ issues that lead to the following block being needed:
> 
> https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/10ae5ee26557107b22524b1a84ebb56ed7d23fb4/ext/node/polyfills/process.ts#L736-L742
> 
> 
> But while investigating it I noticed the inconsistency of whether or not the event loop is allowed to run to completion after a test run. This PR consolidates on the typical case where it doesn't, rather than only doing it if there's a `beforeunload` handler which prevents default. It happens to fix that issue.

Not really - there are no other issues that led to this block looking like it does, besides I had no other way to polyfill this event when this code lived in `std/node`. I'm fairly certain that I can rework it to not clash with "beforeunload".

> At the end of the test run, it's true that there is more work in the event loop because the child process is still open. But for normal test runs we don't attempt to run the event loop unless a JS beforeunload handler calls e.preventDefault() (which the Node compat layer does I assume for good reason). This is kind of strange.

Again, there was no good reason, besides the fact that I wasn't able to polyfill it in any other way at the time of writing that code, which as the linked issue described is actually buggy.

> We should either attempt to complete the event loop before dispatching beforeunload no matter what, or be ruthless and not allow the user to keep a worker alive once the tests from that module are finished i.e. ignore the defaultPrevented for beforeunload in test runs. I'm strongly leaning to the latter.

Maybe, still it feels a little strange that in `deno run` one can keep the event loop alive with `beforeunload` handler as long as needed/wanted but `deno test` and `deno bench` would prevent that. 

@dsherret @lucacasonato thoughts on this?
author: bartlomieju
nayeemrmn

body:
> I was referring to https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/18910. Does the reproduction you're using for https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/16928 also use deno test? Otherwise I do think it's unrelated to this change and can continue to be tracked there.

No, the reproduction uses `deno run`. 

> I think the first one is disruptive because existing code which might rely on the worker and its open resources being dropped after tests are finished would start to hang with no info. The second is what would normally happen currently but with the edge case smoothed off. Thoughts?

Sorry for slow response. Wouldn't the first case immediately break the loop once `beforeunload` event is disptached and then dispatch `unload` event?. To be honest I don't fully understand why there's a difference how these loops look between `deno run`/`deno bench`/`deno test`.

I asked other team members to take a look at this PR. If there are no objections I'll land it as is. We can change the behavior if needed once I fix #16928.
author: bartlomieju

body:
> Wouldn't the first case immediately break the loop once `beforeunload` event is disptached and then dispatch `unload` event?. To be honest I don't fully understand why there's a difference how these loops look between `deno run`/`deno bench`/`deno test`.

The relevant line is the invariant `worker.run_event_loop()` added at the top. Currently that's not there.
author: nayeemrmn

body:
> > Wouldn't the first case immediately break the loop once `beforeunload` event is disptached and then dispatch `unload` event?. To be honest I don't fully understand why there's a difference how these loops look between `deno run`/`deno bench`/`deno test`.
> 
> The relevant line is the invariant `worker.run_event_loop()` added at the top. Currently that's not there.

Exactly :D how does it even work currently if we're not polling the event loop? Am I missing something simple here? I guess it's because of `worker.js_runtime.call_and_await`? But in such case if there's a setup code at top level that uses async APIs it's not polled before the first test case runs, right?
author: bartlomieju

body:
> I guess it's because of `worker.js_runtime.call_and_await`? But in such case if there's a setup code at top level that uses async APIs it's not polled before the first test case runs, right?

Yep, the same was true since `runTests()` in JS. There's no opportunity to run the event loop until awaiting the first test result and you can't do `worker.run_event_loop()` before running tests because it would never resolve if say you started a server for testing.
author: nayeemrmn

body: I wonder if we should set `name` to `node` to be fully compatible with node. But that feels like lying
author: yardenshoham

body:
> I wonder if we should set name to node to be fully compatible with node. But that feels like lying

@yardenshoham if it was changed to 'deno' that wouldn't address the original issue I filed which was an exisiting `npm:` scoped library using the process.release === 'node' to detect environment (browser vs worker vs node). The `node` check would still fail (which makes that commonjs library not usable in Deno in this case).

Guess it depends on deno's intent with the `node:` namespace. If the intention is strictly to be a compatibility layer then I'd expect `process.release` to report `node`. Seems like Deno's intent is exisiting npm libraries can be used in Deno without updates. Not to make the `node:` namespace a first class API target, i.e. the browser API's are Deno's first class APIs that users should be encouraged to target, so would only focus `node:` on compatibility.

If I was a library author trying to support Deno and Node and needed conditional logic / environment specific behaviors then I'd be checking for the `Deno` global existence. But everything under the `node:` namespace would be trying to emulate the node environment as closely as possible. i.e. don't want to encourage deno authors to be writing against the `node:` namespace so Deno wouldn't expose deno-specific behavior there.
author: rajsite

body:
> I wonder if we should set `name` to `node` to be fully compatible with node. But that feels like lying

Or to put it another way. I think for `node:` and `npm:` scoped packages Deno actively wants to lie and make it look like a normal node environment to the modules.

body:
@bartlomieju I will nevertheless add the watcher tests soon, just so they're there.

While I was working on this, I was wondering if maybe this would make more sense as a sort of `--watch --child-process` option, where instead of restarting the main worker, Deno could be managing a separate child process, and then restart the child process when it detects changes?

That child process would then basically be executed using the same command line, except for `--watch`, `--child-process`, and potentially a number of other flags.

That way, we could cover more than just resetting the environment, but also other potential resources, not sure what, maybe file handles and such?

body:
> running `cargo test integration::run::permissions::_061_permissions_request_sync` fails

Two things:
1. There's a bug on main that we unknowing fixed in this PR:
    ```ts
    Deno.permissions.request({ name: "read" });
    ```
    ```
    ┌ ⚠️  Deno requests read access.
    ├ Requested by `Deno.permissions.query()` API.
    ...
    ```
    Now the API name is corrected:
    ```
    ┌ ⚠️  Deno requests read access.
    ├ Requested by `Deno.permissions.request()` API.
    ...
    ```
    So we have to fix those expectations in the tests.
2. I forgot to preserve the `display_path` thing for request prompts (though I'm not sure why we need it there). Fix: https://github.com/nayeemrmn/deno/commit/e0316bcec3383946ae663cbfc3cd27f283972247.

body:
https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/18804#issuecomment-1545167941 Also we have to change APIs like `Deno.watchFs()` , `Deno.remove(_, { recursive: true })` and `Deno.readDir()` to check non-partial permissions. Add `PermissionContainer::check_{read,write}_non_partial()` and replace the permission checks on those ops with these.

**We need to carefully check any APIs which do any sort of recursive access, add tests for when `--deny` flags are specified within their scopes and ensure the `--deny` flags aren't ignored.**
author: nayeemrmn

body:
@iuioiua List of remaining APIs that should use non partial permission checks:
```ts
Deno.readDirSync()
Deno.rename()
Deno.renameSync()
Deno.link()
Deno.linkSync()
```
The checks on `Deno.rename()` and `Deno.link()` are there to ensure that moving paths can't be used to bypass permission checks, all of these apply recursively.

You can search for `\.check_read|\.check_write` across the repo to find the checks and double check the list.
author: nayeemrmn

body:
> [#18804 (comment)](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/18804#issuecomment-1545167941) Also we have to change APIs like `Deno.watchFs()` , `Deno.remove(_, { recursive: true })` and `Deno.readDir()` to check non-partial permissions. Add `PermissionContainer::check_{read,write}_non_partial()` and replace the permission checks on those ops with these.

As per offline discussions with Nayeem, we've renamed `check_non_partial()` to the default `check()`, making it stricter and requiring fully granted permissions. The old `check()` has been renamed to `check_partial()` which passes when permissions are fully or partially granted. I've also taken the liberty, in the last commit, to make use of `perms.ffi.check()` more consistent with its path-related permission counterparts, `perms.read.check()` and `perms.write.check()`.

> We need to carefully check any APIs which do any sort of recursive access, add tests for when `--deny` flags are specified within their scopes and ensure the `--deny` flags aren't ignored.

`permissions::tests::test_query` takes decent care of this, no?
author: iuioiua

body:
> > We need to carefully check any APIs which do any sort of recursive access, add tests for when `--deny` flags are specified within their scopes and ensure the `--deny` flags aren't ignored.
> 
> `permissions::tests::test_query` takes decent care of this, no?

Well, either way now that we've renamed the default to be the safer one I'm not as concerned about regressions there.

body:
> IMO, there should also be a `--deny-all`. It'd be an alias for `--no-prompt`.

Let's put that off for now, I think the primary use case we unlock with this is carve-outs like `--allow-read=foo/ --deny-read=foo/secret`. Each global flag we add like `--deny-all` will add a little overhead and IMO there should be a good use for it.

body: Doesn't seem so, `--deny` flags take precedent over and override `--allow` flags whereas `--no-prompt` only applies to ungranted permissions.
author: nayeemrmn

body: If that's the case, agreed.

body:
I just wanted to add, I was getting the error "Hit the language server document preload limit of 1000" while having only about 100 TypeScript source files.

It turns out it's because I dropped a frontend bundle into a `./public` gitignored directory of my project, which contains over 2,000 JS files on its own. So I had to use `deno.enablePaths` to whitelist my source directories as a workaround, even though this is entirely a Deno project and not a monorepo. The docs seem to point to monorepos being the only use-case of it. I also wonder if gitignored directories should be ignored by the LSP by default.

body:
Taking a look now. I like the idea of limiting FFI to use tokens instead, but I wonder if they should have an unforgeable bitmask with permissions on them to limit their scope.

In addition, we could potentially look at adding "this" arg validation support to ops to ensure that the functions cannot be torn off and exposed accidentally.
author: mmastrac

body:
> Taking a look now. I like the idea of limiting FFI to use tokens instead, but I wonder if they should have an unforgeable bitmask with permissions on them to limit their scope.

Do you mean that eg. a user would create a token requesting Pointer and PointerView but no Callback or FnPointer and would then receive just the APIs they want while internally the token would also record those APIs to be the only ones allowed?

I considered that but thought it might make for a somewhat awkward API to use which would then make users more likely to export and import tokens to avoid the creation bother. Not to say that I wholly disagree with the idea, it might be a good idea despite complicating the API somewhat.

> In addition, we could potentially look at adding "this" arg validation support to ops to ensure that the functions cannot be torn off and exposed accidentally.

That would definitely make these APIs somewhat safer I think... Though, now that I think about it that can and should be done on the JS side: It's basically just WebIDL's brand assertion. No need to take that to the ops level.

The Pointer functions are all static so I'm not sure if it makes sense there.

Where this checking in ops would make sense is with mutations to the `core.ops` object but there then `this` can be fairly trivially bound to the proper ops object so the check doesn't really help.
author: aapoalas

body: Discussed offline with @aapoalas, since the FFI got stabilized in Deno 2, we are not going to change it at this point. We might revisit this topic in the future.

body:
The actual PR LGTM.

One thing I am concerned about is the type signature of the method. I don't recall another API in which the options argument is first. iirc there was a PR to remove this option, but perhaps `serve(handler, options)` would make more sense? Just trying to get some clarity on this decision, don't really have a strong opinion either way.
author: lino-levan

body:
> The actual PR LGTM.
> 
> One thing I am concerned about is the type signature of the method. I don't recall another API in which the options argument is first. iirc there was a PR to remove this option, but perhaps `serve(handler, options)` would make more sense? Just trying to get some clarity on this decision, don't really have a strong opinion either way.

IIRC no other `Deno` API accepts a callback, in case of `Deno.serve()` lots of people define handler inline, which often spans multiple lines. In such case it's very easy to miss that after the handler there's also an options bag.

> In such case it's very easy to miss that after the handler there's also an options bag.

`serve` is still listed in `denoNsUnstable` and is not present at runtime without `--unstable`.

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/686ec85f5220327933d1dfae0e73dabbfc33bc45/runtime/js/90_deno_ns.js#L174

```shellsession
$ deno --version
deno 1.34.3+686ec85 (canary, x86_64-apple-darwin)
v8 11.6.189.7
typescript 5.1.6
$ deno eval 'console.log(Deno.serve)'
undefined
$ deno eval --unstable 'console.log(Deno.serve)'
[Function: serve]
```

body:
@mmastrac 

I've noticed that the change in the return value of the `open` function from `number` to `FileHandle` is causing many tests to fail. It appears that a significant number of the failing tests are obtained from the node repository.

This raises the question of whether node is expecting `open` to return a number. However, according to the ['fsPromises.open' documentation](https://nodejs.org/api/fs.html#fspromisesopenpath-flags-mode), it seems correct that 'open' should return a `FileHandle`.

I'm hoping to fix these tests to accommodate the change. However, I would greatly appreciate any advice on the best strategy for doing so.
author: k-nasa

body: Hey @k-nasa, I will review your PR tonight.
author: bartlomieju

body:
> @mmastrac
> 
> I've noticed that the change in the return value of the `open` function from `number` to `FileHandle` is causing many tests to fail. It appears that a significant number of the failing tests are obtained from the node repository.
> 
> This raises the question of whether node is expecting `open` to return a number. However, according to the ['fsPromises.open' documentation](https://nodejs.org/api/fs.html#fspromisesopenpath-flags-mode), it seems correct that 'open' should return a `FileHandle`.
> 
> I'm hoping to fix these tests to accommodate the change. However, I would greatly appreciate any advice on the best strategy for doing so.

@k-nasa  Note that the event-based API for `createReadStream` uses `Number`s, while the `open` API uses a `FileHandle`. There's probably some code assuming that it can emit the result of `open` directly.

https://nodejs.org/api/fs.html#event-open
author: mmastrac

Thank you both! Indeed, I misunderstood that the interfaces of fs.open and fsPromise.open were similar. 
I've fixed it. I would be grateful if you could review the code again.

author: eggpogg

body: What happens when I add a new property to a global in Deno and try to use it inside an npm package? Would that work? Mainly thinking of scenarios like #19286 .
author: marvinhagemeister

body:
The behaviour is as follows:


For the purposes of this PR, execution environment is split into two parts: Node-mode and Deno-mode. Node-mode is all execution of modules inside of a (virtual) node_modules folder. Deno-mode is all other execution.

**`globalThis` has unchanged behaviour**, except for these globals: https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/9d9ff2b406308a713a09390a5bf206900164969f/ext/node/global.rs#L26-L38.

These are "managed" globals. Even though they look to be properties (or accessors, or whatever the user wants) on `globalThis`, they are actually not. For each of these magic properties, only the mode that they were set in can see them. For example, if execution in Node-mode sets `globalThis.setTimeout` (and this is unset in Deno-mode), `"setTimeout" in globalThis` is only true in Node-mode.

If you were to model this behaviour using JS objects, there would be three objects. `globalThis` `nodeInner` and `denoInner`. It would a little bit like as if `globalThis` was an object that depending on the execution mode inherits from either `nodeInner` or `denoInner`, and both of those objects had getter/setter pairs for all magic properties that store their values in a private field. (Except that in this implementation there is no change to inheritance, enumerability works, and definers and deleters work correctly).

There are some caveats here. For all managed globals:
- prototypical shadowing is bit messed up (as in prototype properties shadow managed `globalThis` properties):
  ```ts
  setTimeout; // [Function setTimeout]
  Object.prototype.setTimeout = 1;
  setTimeout; // 1

  // still correct for non managed properties
  fetch; // [Function fetch]
  Object.prototype.fetch = 1;
  fetch; // [Function fetch]
  ```
- enumeration ordering of managed globals is incorrect
  ```ts
  Object.keys(globalThis); // [...]
  globalThis.aaaa = 1;
  Object.keys(globalThis); // [..., a, ...] (should be [..., a])
  ```
- all access is up to two orders of magnitude slower
