All committers have signed the CLA.
author: CLAassistant

body: @dsherret This seems to work pretty well. Adding an option might be a controversial thing though, do you know the process/next steps to have this functionality land?
author: marcushultman

body: Wondering if a flag would make more sense rather than a config file option.
author: lino-levan

body:
> Wondering if a flag would make more sense rather than a config file option.

@lino-levan That wouldn't work for the LSP, or how would you suggest to solve that? I solved that in a previous iteration of this PR where the url was given to the LSP via a setting (so in VS Code you could specify the url).
author: marcushultman

body: I'm a fan of making it an option in the vscode extension, feels a bit iffy to have yet another top level config option for this. Wonder if it's worth making all of the npm/node stuff under a single option?
author: lino-levan

body:
> I'm a fan of making it an option in the vscode extension, feels a bit iffy to have yet another top level config option for this. Wonder if it's worth making all of the npm/node stuff under a single option?

I do think it makes sense to use a config option in this case, since it's critical for both the LSP and the runtime. If it was only relevant to one or the other, I can see how a flag (+optional config option) or a LSP setting would be preferable. And as for the syntactics, I'm unopinionated. I just want to make it work for the company-internal npm mirror.
author: marcushultman

body: I did not realize that I could just start the VS Code process and locally override the `NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY` env var. So this feature is not strictly required to make things work, but it does ease the usage.
author: marcushultman

body:
> I'm a fan of making it an option in the vscode extension, feels a bit iffy to have yet another top level config option for this. Wonder if it's worth making all of the npm/node stuff under a single option?

Yeah, I agree because this is often a user setting. Let's implement https://github.com/denoland/vscode_deno/pull/858#issuecomment-1652672599 for now.
author: dsherret

body: @lucacasonato there has been a `no-window-prefix` lint rule for quite some time: https://lint.deno.land/?q=no-window-prefix#no-window-prefix
author: dsherret

body:
Oh, looks like we should make it more strict:

> ```ts
> // `alert` is allowed to call with `window` because it's not supported in Workers
> window.alert("🍣");
> ```

Opened https://github.com/denoland/deno_lint/issues/1163
author: dsherret

body: This is stale, so going to close.
author: dsherret

body: Sure, would this test go under `cli\tools\test.rs`?
author: ghost

body: `cli/tests/integration/bench_tests.rs`
author: crowlKats

body: Wouldn't that be a standard rust test case that would be included in said file addressed as #[test]? I do not see any of the test cases from `cli/tests/integration/bench_tests.rs` being run in `cargo test`
author: ghost

body:
> Wouldn't that be a standard rust test case that would be included in said file addressed as #[test]? I do not see any of the test cases from `cli/tests/integration/bench_tests.rs` being run in `cargo test`

They are definitely run, if you issue `cargo test integration::bench` you will see all tests from `cli/tests/integration/bench_test.rs` being run. `itest!` is our own helper macro that is expanded into a regular `#[test]`.
author: bartlomieju

body: I see now. Thanks for pointing me in the right direction I'll have a test for this tonight or tomorrow.
author: ghost

body: `test integration::js_unit_tests::read_text_file_test` isn't failing on my local machine. Is there a way to retrigger the build without doing a commit?
author: ghost

body: @WingZer0o unfortunately only maintainers can retrigger specific CI pipeline steps. I've merged `main` branch into your PR to retrigger CI.
author: bartlomieju

body: No problem. Looks like it passed this time, might be a flaky test. Let me know if anything needs to be revised.

author: mmastrac

body:
Note: std's snapshot testing feature uses `Deno.inspect` as the serializer of target object. ref: https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/blob/fdabe55e2dd29df626ecb5b4496b091478298eaf/testing/snapshot.ts#L199-L208

The way of `Deno.inspect` to serialize long string values are changed in https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/18691 and now the string snapshots have a lot of noise and very uncomfortable to compare.

This PR tries to restore the previous behavior optionally and prepares for addressing https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/issues/3404

body:
I'll take a look at this later today and overall I agree but as a heads up I strongly prefer avoiding boolean types, even if we are just replacing them with multiple enums that are unwrapped quickly.

Booleans are often a footgun and enums are exactly as cheap, semantically more meaningful, and allow us to document the exact meaning of each value.

author: CLAassistant

body:
Does this bug appear in the reverse? If you remove a listener when firing an event?

Partially sounds to me like the listener list should be shallowe copied upon add/remove of listeners, and triggers should take an object reference to keep "snapshotted" list
author: aapoalas

body:
Hey @aapoalas! No, it does not happen in reverse; it would if the bug existed in the general case, but that's not true. The optimization done (see link in PR description) was just "no shallow copy if there is only one listener attached" which breaks in the example from the test in this PR, but this also means that when adding two or more listeners, things behave like they should. That means that this bug does not exist in the "removal" variant, because the buggy path only exists for when there is one event listener in the first place.

The solution here (after the suggested change) is to
 - If there are more than one listeners, shallow copy (as before);
 - Otherwise, if there is only one, loop through only the first item.

This is functionally equivalent to shallow copying, except avoids shallow copying in the case where there is one listener only (as a performance optimization).

Sorry for bringing this up very late, but is `listen` correct verb for this API? `listen` sounds like it starts a server, but `DatagramConn` works as both client and server depending on the later API calls (`send` or `receive`)

Node.js calls the similar API `dgram.createSocket` which seems expressing its dual (`sender` & `receiver`) nature more naturally. What do you think?

(Side note: I had the above question after seeing this PR https://github.com/denoland/denobyexample/pull/80)

Comparing the canary releases for https://github.com/denoland/deno/commit/a181ceb0e3791c842db6e8e6f528cf9ce320642a and https://github.com/denoland/deno/commit/28a4f3d0f5383695b1d49ccdc8b0f799a715b2c2, I get 291 both before and after (a file was added in #19223). I assume we're both searching with the copyright header.

Last thing I can think of is the duplicate calls to `load_side_module()` on cli snapshot creation which ends up calling `.instantiate_module()` for each module handle again. It shouldn't do anything for a kEvaluated module but who knows.

author: nayeemrmn

body:
> I think you're using the debug binary to get 308. Comparing the canary releases for https://github.com/denoland/deno/commit/a181ceb0e3791c842db6e8e6f528cf9ce320642a and https://github.com/denoland/deno/commit/28a4f3d0f5383695b1d49ccdc8b0f799a715b2c2, I get 291 both before and after (a file was added in https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/19223). I assume we're both searching with the copyright header.

Yikes, that might have been it. I compared it to the latest released version. And yes, I was looking at the copyright headers in the produced binary.

> Last thing I can think of is the duplicate calls to load_side_module() on cli snapshot creation which ends up calling .instantiate_module() for each module handle again. It shouldn't do anything for a kEvaluated module but who knows.
Edit: Nope not this.

That's a good pointer, but I believe V8 is smart enough, not to create a duplicate module entries.

Let's wait a few hours before we dig into it more - I think we will abandon the idea of not snapshotting `ext/node` (because of some other work and decisions). While I think this PR cleans up a lot, the fact that we can't establish where this binary increase is coming from is really worrisome.

body: I'm not thrilled about this feature. First of all, it's not enabled by default, so it's only going to be used by like 1% of users. It's not something we would likely turn on by default, because it would be live updating an executable - for which there is little presidence.
author: ry

body: I agree @ry but one usefulness I can think of is for the case of patches or minor non breaking changes. We can set upgrade by default for that and have levels like 1/2/3 for different level of upgrade opt in.
author: vedant-pandey

body:
Thank you for all the @vedant-pandey but after further discussion we decided not to land this feature. 

`deno upgrade` works well and there are no surprises to its behavior. Having additional logic to do automatic upgrades before running user code, it tricky to test and adds additional maintenance overhead.

body:
> How could it not? All shorthands do; you can't preserve much meaning with a single letter.

Exactly. That's a huge issue for people who aren't aware of what the shorthand flags mean because this relates to Deno's security model. Looking at a deno command on the command line should be easily auditable for what permissions are being allowed.
author: dsherret

body: I'd argue that shorthands are as easily auditable as their longer form the more people use them. That's why some people aren't even aware of `ls --all`.  Once people get a hand of shorthands they will prefer to use them. This is a similar situation to vim or tailwind.
author: samyosm

body:
> I'd argue that shorthands are as easily auditable as their longer form the more people use them. That's why some people aren't even aware of ls --all. Once people get a hand of shorthands they will prefer to use them. This is a similar situation to vim or tailwind.

I disagree with the ls, vim, and tailwind comparison because those don't expand a security sandbox.

I think it's less auditable because it requires looking up or knowing what each shorthand does. It also requires people to be on their toes about new shorthand flags that could open up the security sandbox. It's especially harmful to beginners who might accidentally open their security sandbox for scripts they don't want to because they never realized Deno even had short non-descriptive flags that opened the security sandbox and they just copy and pasted some command.
author: dsherret

body: I guess you are right. I'll wait the ending words of #19530 before closing this PR.
author: samyosm

body:
I don't think this should land as stated by reviewer, but if its going to happen somehow, instead of reserving new ones I think these short flags make more sense:

| Short | Long |
|--------|--------|
| -Ae | --allow-env |
| -Af | --allow-ffi |
| -At | --allow-hrtime |
| -An | --allow-network |
| -Ar | --allow-read |
| -Ax | --allow-run |
| -As | --allow-sys |
| -Aw | --allow-write |
author: catuhana

body: Thank you for the PR @samyosm, but as it stands there's no support for this features in the core team. We are exploring alternative ways of making it more convenient to specify which permissions to apply including #12763. I'm going to close it without a merge.

It is mostly a copy of the original test in [deno_std](https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/blob/b4239898d6c6b4cdbfd659a4ea1838cf4e656336/node/https_test.ts).

But for some reason [both assertions of the https_request.ts file](https://github.com/fbaltor/deno/blob/74694a1e538451e209a85e072119b9da534b9acf/cli/tests/unit_node/testdata/https_request.ts#L8-L11) regarding the "authorized" part of the server response are failing. Indeed, the `res.socket` object is missing it's `authorized` property.

I suspect it has something to do with a misconfiguration in the key and cert parts ([link](https://github.com/fbaltor/deno/blob/74694a1e538451e209a85e072119b9da534b9acf/cli/tests/unit_node/https_test.ts#L11-L12)) but I'm not sure.

body:
AssertionError class is available from `node:assert` module (`import { AssertionError } from "node:assert"`). Please use that version. Porting non-test code to `cli/tests/unit_node/` doesn't make sense. Also copying source code under `node/internal/` and `node/internal_binding` doesn't make much sense. Please revert those changes (These files are already ported to `ext/node/polyfills` and should be accessible somehow via `node:` specifier).

Regarding the APIs `copyError`, `createErrDiff`, `inspectValue`, these are not public APIs of Node.js and not available from `node:assert`. I'd suggest we should remove test cases of those.

author: mmastrac

body:
Wouldn't it be better to have an `allowHost` option to allow specifying a `Host` header in the request without the filtering? I know this doesn't currently work because the `Deno.HttpClient` is only passed on the call to `fetch`, rather than on request construction, but it seems like `client` could be part of `RequestInit` just fine. Note that response headers aren't filtered in Deno.

Note that this issue might be relevant to the work we're doing in WinterCG with forking the Fetch spec to cover the needs of server-side runtimes.
author: andreubotella

body: Good point @andreubotella, I'm gonna defer to @lucacasonato to decide.
author: bartlomieju

body:
I agree with the `allowHost` option. It should be passed on the `Deno.HttpClient`. `Request` should not filter out the `Host` header in JS, but rather it should be filtered in Rust based on whether the client has this option set or not.

Because of this it should not matter that `Request` does not have a `client` option, because it would never affect the JS side of `Request`, only ever the `fetch` itself.

FYI on the footgun that caused us to block this in the first place: https://github.com/denoland/deploy_feedback/issues/52

body:
I think the representation is being unpacked that way since `xunit-viewer` casting the repeated property to an array. Since steps are non-standard, I think this will be represented a lot of different ways by a lot of different systems.

The issue comparing with mocha is that our internal representation is different. `describe` is not a test case, it's a test suite, hence why everything ends up nested under a suite called `#indexOf()`. `Deno.test` is not a test suite, it's a test case that can have other test cases nested into it, so we're in a really uncomfortable situation. If we un-nest every step as it's own test-case, we'll start double counting everything which will cause deceptive execution times and failure counts.

author: bartlomieju

body:
> LGTM, is there a test that could be added to avoid regressions in the future?

We might be able to create a timing comparison test between GZip and Brotli but I worry it might be flaky. Maybe a heuristic like "does this compress within 10x the wall-clock time of GZip", but that could be a challenge to find a good set of parameters that detect the problem and are sensitive enough to trigger if this code gets lost. I wouldn't recommend using a fixed wall-clock timeout for this because that will almost certainly trigger too often.

I think I should punt on this one because it's probably a bit too much work.

body:
The obvious test cases I thought of was asserting the following behavior ([as described by the spec](https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#dom-request-clone)):

> The clone() method steps are:
> 
> 1. If [this](https://webidl.spec.whatwg.org/#this) is [unusable](https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#body-unusable), then [throw](https://webidl.spec.whatwg.org/#dfn-throw) a [TypeError](https://webidl.spec.whatwg.org/#exceptiondef-typeerror).
> 
> 2. Let clonedRequest be the result of [cloning](https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#concept-request-clone) [this](https://webidl.spec.whatwg.org/#this)’s [request](https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#concept-request-request).
> 
> 3. [Assert](https://infra.spec.whatwg.org/#assert): [this](https://webidl.spec.whatwg.org/#this)’s [signal](https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#request-signal) is non-null.
> 
> 4. Let clonedSignal be the result of [creating a dependent abort signal](https://dom.spec.whatwg.org/#create-a-dependent-abort-signal) from « [this](https://webidl.spec.whatwg.org/#this)’s [signal](https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#request-signal) », using [AbortSignal](https://dom.spec.whatwg.org/#abortsignal) and [this](https://webidl.spec.whatwg.org/#this)’s [relevant realm](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/webappapis.html#concept-relevant-realm).
> 
> 5. Let clonedRequestObject be the result of [creating](https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#request-create) a [Request](https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#request) object, given clonedRequest, [this](https://webidl.spec.whatwg.org/#this)’s [headers](https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#request-headers)’s [guard](https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#concept-headers-guard), clonedSignal and [this](https://webidl.spec.whatwg.org/#this)’s [relevant realm](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/webappapis.html#concept-relevant-realm).
> 
> 6. Return clonedRequestObject.
> 

Does this make sense or you had other tests in mind, @mmastrac?

If that test was correct then why the behavior reported in the issue wasn't?
author: bartlomieju

body:
This incorrect behavior is that the screen is not cleared when the `--no-clear-screen` flag is not passed by default, and the screen is cleared when the `--no-clear-screen` flag is passed true.

This is because we passed the opposite value to the `file_watcher::watch_func` function, incorrectly using ` ! ` for the inverse.

```rust
  file_watcher::watch_func(
    flags,
    file_watcher::PrintConfig {
      job_name: "Test".to_string(),
      clear_screen: !test_flags
        .watch
        .as_ref()
        .map(|w| !w.no_clear_screen)
        .unwrap_or(true),
    },
    ...
  )
  .await?;
```

So much so that `watch_func` gets the wrong `PrintConfig.clear_screen` parameter, performing the opposite action.

The value of `test_flags` is correct, so test was also correct.

Fantastic work @SyrupThinker, I love that you didn't bring any additional dependency for it.

This PR closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/14390.

Could you provide some more context on the "minor incompatibilities that need to be patched manually"? What are those?
author: bartlomieju

body:
> Could you provide some more context on the "minor incompatibilities that need to be patched manually"? What are those?

Sure, this is what I encountered:
- `faucet` unconditionally requires `npm-which`, which searches for `npm` in `PATH`,
  this obviously fails if you don't have NodeJS installed.
  My local fix is to just defer the import until it would actually be needed for the   builtin test runner, which is not used here.
- `tap-spec` fails to format error output (using `chalk`), I did not investigate further, just removed the offending formatting.
  <details>

  ```
  error: Uncaught TypeError: format.red.bold is not a function
    at formatErrors (file:///Users/syrup/Library/Application%20Support/XDG/state/stav/deno/.dir/npm/registry.npmjs.org/tap-spec/5.0.0/index.js:116:37)
    at PassThrough.<anonymous> (file:///Users/syrup/Library/Application%20Support/XDG/state/stav/deno/.dir/npm/registry.npmjs.org/tap-spec/5.0.0/index.js:84:19)
    at PassThrough.emit (ext:deno_node/_stream.mjs:1852:9)
    at Stream.<anonymous> (file:///Users/syrup/Library/Application%20Support/XDG/state/stav/deno/.dir/npm/registry.npmjs.org/tap-out/2.1.0/index.js:246:14)
    at Stream.emit (ext:deno_node/_stream.mjs:1857:11)
    at Stream.stream.destroy (file:///Users/syrup/Library/Application%20Support/XDG/state/stav/deno/.dir/npm/registry.npmjs.org/through/2.3.8/index.js:84:12)
    at _end (file:///Users/syrup/Library/Application%20Support/XDG/state/stav/deno/.dir/npm/registry.npmjs.org/through/2.3.8/index.js:67:14)
    at Stream.stream.end (file:///Users/syrup/Library/Application%20Support/XDG/state/stav/deno/.dir/npm/registry.npmjs.org/through/2.3.8/index.js:74:5)
    at PassThrough.onend (file:///Users/syrup/Library/Application%20Support/XDG/state/stav/deno/.dir/npm/registry.npmjs.org/readable-stream/2.2.9/lib/_stream_readable.js:499:10)
    at Object.onceWrapper (ext:deno_node/_stream.mjs:1927:32)

author: sigmaSd

body:
Looking again it'll be more complicated to not break current and older versions of the extension. The command registration at https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/20111/files#diff-36f322c6b36e52165bbafb69fd8f4ae78d5f7e36deebc6c8d9d30e62759651b1R122 will conflict with the command registration in the extension.

We'll probably have to do a check on both sides but perhaps they make sense on their own. I'll patch the extension to check that a command isn't already registered before it attempts to register one (probably should anyway), and send `"enableBuiltinCommands": true` in the initialization options. The LSP won't register this command until it gets that option.

from ~2m iter/s to ~27m iter/s

body: Hello guys, I am facing a problem that only happens on deno 1.36.2, I was taking a look in the repository trying to figure which could be the change that potentially caused this and due to my lack of knowledge in rust I selected this one cause the title is close enough to what I've done to fix the issue so far. The problem is that the `transfer-encoding` was changed to `chunked` (previously no transfer-encoding was set by deno) when a `new Response(body, {headers, status})` is used using a previously created response as a parameter. Looks like our front-end framework (fresh, currently) is not handling well the idea of receiving this data chunked. Check my PR changes to make it work if it could help: https://github.com/deco-cx/deco/pull/392/files Tested with + deno 1.36.1 ```ts Deno.serve((_req) => { const resp = new Response("hello, world"); return new Response(resp.body); }); ``` Then ```sh curl http://localhost:8000 -vvv ``` Now with deno 1.36.2 (used `deno upgrade 1.36.2`) cc: @mmastrac @bartlomieju

body: @bartlomieju More context. We wanted to reduce the number of env permission prompts in node compat (invoked from `process.env[NAME]`) in the past because, for example, `npm:express` asks excessive number of envs (like ~10, and most are somethings nobody cares about) and it was very annoying. We started checking permission before actually calling `Deno.env.get` in https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/pull/3178, and only access to it when permission is already given. That resulted in another issue such as #18665

body:
Just as an update, running the test resulted in this error:
 `error: TS7053 [ERROR]: Element implicitly has an 'any' type because expression of type 'symbol' can't be used to index type '() => never'.
testFn[Symbol.for("Deno.test.location")] = "foo/bar/fizz.ts:100:200";`

Giving a quick look for [similar issues](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/57086672/element-implicitly-has-an-any-type-because-expression-of-type-string-cant-b), it feels like the type of something in `test.fn` needs to be explicitly declared. I haven't come across a similar issue involving symbols, so I feel like that's the next thing I'll look into. Before I try to code up a solution.

body:
It's not entirely clear to me why this is necessary -- it should be entirely possible to build without this explicit dependency. The version required for `deno` needs to be exactly the one specified in `deno_core`, so this is purely redundant.

FWIW, it's probably better if we create a "local dependency" build feature for deno, deno_core and rusty_v8, since the homebrew build appears to be pulling the `deno_core` crates from crates.io AFAICT.

If it's because of this line in the brew file, you should insert the line after `[workspace.dependencies]` instead:

```
    inreplace "Cargo.toml",
              /^v8 = { version = ("[\d.]+"),.*}$/,
              "v8 = { version = \\1, path = \"./v8\" }"
```

body:
Just to explain my motivations in short;

From my understanding, the long-term goal is to return a `WebSocketStream` from `upgradeWebSocket` directly. How long-term we are talking here seems to be dependent upon when a `WebSocketStream` spec might be nailed down, which is highly uncertain.

Deno already provides an unstable `WebSocketStream` implementation, and so _consistency_ is one of my motivations for providing this (also unstable) `upgradeWebSocketStream` function; If it is available for experimental testing on the client, why not on the server?

However, my main motivation is that how-/whenever the `WebSocketStream` interface might be properly spec'ed in the end, it _could_ benefit from in-production experimental usage before-hand, so I think it would be a proper win-win to make it available under the `--unstable` flag as early as possible.

Going further, it might not be a crazy idea to keep both `upgradeWebSocket` _and_ `upgradeWebSocketStream` as permanent alternatives, as users will always have differing preferences. Whether this is the way forward or not, may also be shed additional light on by making the `upgradeWebSocketStream` alternative available before rather than later.

body:
How does this change fit in with the dotenv std module?  There is obviously overlap, loading from a .env file.  Some thoughts:

* Is this needed if all this functionality and more can be achieved with a single line of code (import of std/dotenv/load.ts)?
* Is it confusing having this functionality available in both CLI flag and std module?
* Conversely, with a .env.example and .env.defaults files and the ability to access the key/value pairs without exporting to the process environment there is an argument for keeping the std dotenv module as it provides added value which dotenvy doesn't offer
* Do dotenvy and std/dotenv parse a .env file in the same way with the same rules and capabilities?  (Does it matter?)
* I do like the explicit command line reference to loading a .env file,  this makes it more explicit that you are dependent on env variables
author: cknight

body:
> How does this change fit in with the dotenv std module? There is obviously overlap, loading from a .env file. 
Some thoughts:
> 
> * Is this needed if all this functionality and more can be achieved with a single line of code (import of std/dotenv/load.ts)?

There is some overlap, however with `std/dotenv` you can't influence any of `DENO_*` env vars without spawning a subprocess, while with `--env` flag these env variables will be applied before Deno checks for values of these env vars.

> * Is it confusing having this functionality available in both CLI flag and std module?

Probably yes, however `--env` is way more convenient than having to import module from the standard library, plus see previous comment.

> * Conversely, with a .env.example and .env.defaults files and the ability to access the key/value pairs without exporting to the process environment there is an argument for keeping the std dotenv module as it provides added value which dotenvy doesn't offer

We would certainly keep the `std/dotenv` module for people who need more control or flexibility. The `--env` flag is meant to serve the 80-90% use case.

> * Do dotenvy and std/dotenv parse a .env file in the same way with the same rules and capabilities?  (Does it matter?)

Not sure, and I'm not sure if that matters. We will need to check that. It's not 100% agreed that we'll use `dotenvy` crate - AFAIK it can't do variable substitution which is one of the requirements for the `--env` flag. We already have some infrastructure for dealing with env vars in `deno_task_shell` and we might go with fork/inspired implementation there instead of using a 3rd part crate.

> * I do like the explicit command line reference to loading a .env file,  this makes it more explicit that you are dependent on env variables

That was also one of the requirements for this feature - no magic discovery, not walking up the file system. In more detail the requirements for this feature are as follows:

- require `--env` flag, don't traverse up the CWD if the file is not found
- multiline variables support
- variable substitution
- conflict handling - this is the least specified, I think we just need to decide if the conflicting env var should raise an error or overrwrite (or make it configurable via a flag)
author: bartlomieju

body: Thanks @bartlomieju, makes sense.
author: cknight

LGTM! 

> an adapter layer in this module to map a Node.js Brotli API to the Rust API would end up being a little more complicated.

Yes, thats why this module was written using the C API. We can use the Rust API for decompression but compression has a lot more flags https://nodejs.org/api/zlib.html#class-brotlioptions and no way to pass them using the Rust API.

A couple approaches:

* [`jupyter_kernel_test`](https://github.com/jupyter/jupyter_kernel_test), which would require writing some python for each segment of conformance testing you want to evaluate
* Integration testing using [`papermill`](https://papermill.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) against a suite of notebooks

body:
Just a heads up, my colleague @kafonek found a few spec mismatches in execvr - https://github.com/evcxr/evcxr/issues/280 and https://github.com/evcxr/evcxr/issues/281.

I think getting the basics of this out there for people to try will be good and we'll be able to help evaluate conformance. All Jupyter frontend work and jupyterlab extensions are done in Typescript now along with some node tooling, so this will definitely be a welcome addition to the community. Excited to tell more and more people about it!
author: rgbkrk

body: @rgbkrk I put together https://github.com/kafonek/demo-evcxr-sidecar to show how I use `kernel-sidecar` to test out messaging over ZMQ to evcxr, if you wanted to try to replicate with deno. I don't know if it'll necessarily be easier to test this way or with a frontend though. Just throwing the option out there.
author: kafonek

body:
> > Do you have any tips regarding testing a kernel implementation?
> 
> A couple approaches:
> 
> * [`jupyter_kernel_test`](https://github.com/jupyter/jupyter_kernel_test), which would require writing some python for each segment of conformance testing you want to evaluate
> * Integration testing using [`papermill`](https://papermill.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) against a suite of notebooks
> 
> I remember when the jupyter kernel test work was in its infancy. Now, it looks sufficiently advanced and well-supported enough to rely on.

Thanks for the pointer, I guess I could a simple integration test that spawns a notebook subprocess and runs all cells then checks if the content of the notebook has changed. 



> Just a heads up, my colleague @kafonek found a few spec mismatches in execvr - [evcxr/evcxr#280](https://github.com/evcxr/evcxr/issues/280) and [evcxr/evcxr#281](https://github.com/evcxr/evcxr/issues/281).
> 
> I think getting the basics of this out there for people to try will be good and we'll be able to help evaluate conformance. All Jupyter frontend work and jupyterlab extensions are done in Typescript now along with some node tooling, so this will definitely be a welcome addition to the community. Excited to tell more and more people about it!

Another good pointer, I will update `language_info` reply, though could you suggest what should be the proper values for `pygments_lexer` and `nbconverter_exporter`?



> @rgbkrk I put together https://github.com/kafonek/demo-evcxr-sidecar to show how I use `kernel-sidecar` to test out messaging over ZMQ to evcxr, if you wanted to try to replicate with deno. I don't know if it'll necessarily be easier to test this way or with a frontend though. Just throwing the option out there.

Thanks for the suggestions, is there by any chance a binary executable that could be installed instead of relying on `poetry` here? (Trying to keep our CI times to a minimum, so I would like to avoid installing Python deps if possible).

----

I have a couple more questions:
- kernel install/uninstall - is it fine to shell out to `jupyter kernelspec (un)install` for this purpose, or should we try to put connection spec file directly in the user files directory?
- is it required to handle `interrupt_request` if Deno reacts to Ctrl/Cmd+C properly?
author: bartlomieju

body:
> is it required to handle `interrupt_request` if Deno reacts to Ctrl/Cmd+C properly?

You need handle both SIGINT and `interrupt_request` to work in all environments. However, it's probably a fine assumption for the time being to just handle SIGINT.

> Thanks for the suggestions, is there by any chance a binary executable that could be installed instead of relying on poetry here?

All of these tools rely on installing `python`, though I think using papermill might be the easiest for this because you can `pip install` it with the system python and use that to run the notebooks.

Since I'm just now diving in to rust, it's really hard for me to not just go and build all the usual jupyter tools _again_ in rust. Tempting. So tempting. In nteract we created a lot of node tooling for working with zeromq and jupyter connections that I haven't properly touched in a while, even though I know it's in production at companies like CoCalc (and the nteract desktop app).

> kernel install/uninstall - is it fine to shell out to `jupyter kernelspec (un)install` for this purpose, or should we try to put connection spec file directly in the user files directory?

I would prefer that folks rely on `jupyter kernelspec` due to the variety of ways in which people want to install kernels (system, user, environment levels). Instead of having to track that and adjust, let Jupyter do the heavy lifting.
author: rgbkrk

body: Thanks for the pointers. I'll try to finish this over the weekend.
author: bartlomieju
bartlomieju

body:
We're in agreement that we can land it for Deno v1.37.

Blockers for landing this PR: 
- [ ] any kind of test, even one that can be run manually by contributor and relying on having `jupyter` on their path
- [ ] measurement of CI time increase
- [x] measurement of binary size increase with this change (96->97Mb on macOS, way less than 1Mb increase)
- [ ] verify it works as expected on all platforms
- [ ] decide on the subcommand - `deno jupyter` will be run by the `jupyter notebook`; having user do `deno jupyter --install` (which is the only subcommand run by users) seems less ideal than doing `deno jupyter` that will set up the kernel and `deno jupyter --run` being invoked by the notebook
author: bartlomieju

body:
> For pygments_lexer I think I can use typescript (or skip it since the messaging spec says it can be omitted if it's the same as name

[That's correct](https://pygments.org/docs/lexers/#pygments.lexers.javascript.TypeScriptLexer). Anything deeper would require writing a lexer for pygments for `deno`. That's only necessary for nbconvert though.

> what about `nbconvert_exporter`

`nbconvert_exporter` should be `script`. There's no custom exporter for Typescript, let alone JavaScript. It will convert notebooks using the `file_extension` property instead (creating typescript files).

body:
One other thing I've noticed. Doing a run all results in stdout being grouped in the last cell run.

![image](https://github.com/denoland/deno/assets/836375/7204f25f-0b5a-489a-b127-d82e184a6bc2)

If I run these individually they come in order.

Based on how Jupyter works, this means that the stdout messages got associated to the most recent execution (like `header.msg_id = parent_header.msg_id`). This makes sense in an asynchronous perspective as the originator may not be known. In other cases, like a notebook with separate `console.log` lines, perhaps the stdout and stderr buffers need to be flushed when an execution request finishes.

body: I tested the latest version of this PR and it's working well for a few of my tests, but exceptions don't appear to be visible in the notebook. First impressions: - We should support `!` syntax for running shell commands like the ipython kernel. - Exceptions don't appear to be surfaced in the notebook (see screenshot) - Permissions should probably be either wide open, or configurable in the first cell (ie: `%permissions --allow-net` like https://ipython.readthedocs.io/en/stable/interactive/magics.html) - It would be great if `[Symbol.for("Jupyter.display")]` could support buffers, which would allow us to use the `https://deno.land/x/canvas/mod.ts` module to render images.

author: osddeitf

body:
The previous behavior of Deno `node:http` is to send request when the class `ClientRequest` is constructed.
What I modified is to delay the request until the first body chunk get sent, or when `request.end()`.
That also fixes the methods `setHeader()`, `flushHeader()` and `removeHeader()`.

This is my fix attempt:
- [724c966](https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/20127/commits/724c96674f8044540267105d0bfa017b620316ed): assert only relevant headers.
- [1a98037](https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/20127/commits/1a98037f1f8e5fd10ee4bbf55d8d9dbe46782713): close http server when there's an `AssertionError` (if the server still running, the test can't stop).
- [aca7b73](https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/20127/commits/aca7b73cfd51f187cbffa7250645eb7d4e59279c): the test couldn't fail so I store the `AssertionError` and throw when the server is closed.

author: bartlomieju

body:
@bartlomieju let me report on what I found (again, sorry if it's too long):
- The error message indicates a `Promise.all()` rejection with index `0`, I was able to pinpoint the error at `ClientRequest.end()` in `node:http`:

  ```typescript
    (async () => {
      try {
        const [res, _] = await Promise.all([
          core.opAsync("op_fetch_send", this._req.requestRid),
  ```
  Note that the promise is `await`ed, and it is wrapped inside an `async` IIFE.
- Deno implementation of `http.Server`:
  - server.listen(): [http.ts#L1621](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/v1.36.4/ext/node/polyfills/http.ts#L1621).
  - server.close(): [http.ts#L1679](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/v1.36.4/ext/node/polyfills/http.ts#L1679).

  Note that the server is abruptly closed.
- Node implementation of `http.Server` (kinda complicated):
  - `http.Server` inherit `net.Server`
  - Calling `server.listen()` -> [`server.listenInCluster()`](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/v18.17.1/lib/net.js#L1887) -> [`server._listen2()`](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/v18.17.1/lib/net.js#L1827) -> [`onconnection()`](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/v18.17.1/lib/net.js#L1742) -> [`increase connections count`](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/v18.17.1/lib/net.js#L2033).
  - Calling `server.close()` -> [`server._emitCloseIfDrained()`](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/v18.17.1/lib/net.js#L2124) -> [`don't close when there are connection(s)`](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/v18.17.1/lib/net.js#L2133).

  Note that the server gracefully shutdown, waiting for connections to close.
- Error message `Connection reset by peer (os error 54)`, indicates that the request did get to the server, but the server suddenly closed.

So I surmise that inside the test, the server get closed (when `countdown` reached `0`) before `res.end()` complete, and as a result, the request get closed abrubtly.
author: osddeitf

body:
In Nodejs docs [server.close([callback])](https://nodejs.org/dist/latest-v18.x/docs/api/http.html#serverclosecallback):

> Stops the server from accepting new connections. See [net.Server.close()](https://nodejs.org/dist/latest-v18.x/docs/api/net.html#serverclosecallback).

So I think this is indeed an issue of `Server.close()` in Deno implementation.
author: osddeitf

body: @osddeitf no worries. Nice investigation. Let's wait for https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/20387 to land and then it should be an easy fix 👌

body:
Hmm... I'm not completely sold on this. Fast call would pass Latin-1, whereas slow call would pass UTF-8: These do not agree on all characters so the receiver would need to basically check if they receive valid UTF-8 and if not then parse the data as Latin-1.

Furthermore since this is mostly copying data, except for fast calls, then the performance in the general case is either equivalent or worse than a manual TextDecoder use, as with the manual case you can manually deduplicate copies when you know you're passing the same string over and over again.
author: aapoalas

body:
My particular use case is SQlite3 when binding TEXT values. The reciever (sqlite) in this case does not care about the encoding and will happily store/give it back as TEXT - good example of the GIGO (Garbage-In Garbage Out) rule.

And for the slow case, one fallback op to encode and call is faster than using TextEncoder#encode on the binding value again and again.
author: littledivy

body: Are you sure there isn't an edge case where a Latin-1 string that is not ASCII gets read from sqlite and gets interpreted as UTF-8, leading to corrupted data?
author: aapoalas
littledivy

body:
> @aapoalas
> 
> https://sqlite.org/invalidutf.html#best_effort_to_preserve_text
> 
> > Generally speaking, if you insert invalid UTF into SQLite, you will get the exact same byte sequence back out, as long as you do not ask SQLite to transform the text in any way.

Yes, now when we get this Latin-1 encoded string back from sqlite, what will we do with it?

There are three possibilities that seem to be coming up now:
1. We get an ASCII string back from sqlite (or any FFI API). In this case it's safe to read it as OneByte or as UTF-8.
2. We get a UTF-8 string from it. This can be only read as UTF-8.
3. We get a non-ASCII Latin-1 string. This can be only read as OneByte.

Can we tell these three (or rather the two latter) cases apart? Sqlite will happily save both encodings and will not give us any hint on what was saved specifically, it's indeed garbage in and out. But can we actually safely use the garbage we get out?
author: aapoalas
mmastrac

author: littledivy

body:
> @aapoalas wouldn't that make things slow?

It would slow some things down but returns would be a lot faster since they could take the fast path now.

I'm sort of thinking that eg. pointer offsets would go back to being 32 bit integers at the op layer to keep those as numbers since it's very very improbable for a higher offset to be needed. But in general I think bigints would make things simpler in the limit:

eg. Right now the way you write a pointer into a buffer is a weird mix of numbers and bigints due to `setBigUint64` API and `Deno.UnsafePointer.value` API disagreeing on what representation they want to use.

body:
@rgbkrk this adds `Deno.jupyter.sendIo` API as we discussed in the issue, however sending anything (eg. `await Deno.jupyter.sendIo({ "text/html": "

Hello there!

" });`) has no effect, it's just `null` that's printed in the notebook. Am I doing something wrong, or did I misunderstand how this is supposed to work?
author: bartlomieju

body: It looks like you need more of the payload (data, metadata). I'll dive in shortly with this branch.
author: rgbkrk

body:
Ooops, you're right! Even if I change the invocation to `await Deno.jupyter.sendIo({ "data": {"text/plain": "

Hello there!!!

" } });` I still don't get anything printed :/ will continue to look what's wrong here. EDIT: Ah, got it: ``` await Deno.jupyter.sendIo({ "data": {"text/html": "

Hello there!!!

" }, "metadata": { "text/htmll": {"foo": true } }}); ``` Adding "metadata" does the trick and the message gets displayed:
author: bartlomieju

body:
I think it's ok to show `null` as opposed to `undefined`. For me at least what would be helpful is getting back the created message to know that it made what I needed. The simpler layer on top gets built.

I still need some way to send `update_display_data` which has the same payload but the message type is different.
author: rgbkrk

body: PR to add the ability to send an arbitrary IOPub message: https://github.com/bartlomieju/deno/pull/4
author: rgbkrk

body:
This makes it nice and easy to build progress bars in the notebook and any other kind of report for users. Excited to see this coming in.

![updating-displays](https://github.com/denoland/deno/assets/836375/7b550b32-2ec2-4a18-b788-78ed6dcb3295)
author: rgbkrk

body: I made sure other iopub messages worked: ## `clear_output` ![clear_output](https://github.com/denoland/deno/assets/836375/f7252779-0047-4fcf-b33a-c864dd785459) ## `stream` ## `display_data` with `application/json` ## `comm_open` and `comm_msg` Of course, I can't tell how properly comm sending is working unless this is connected up with a receiver on the frontend. ## `execute_result` ----- Generally I think this is great. I'm not a big fan of the name `sendIo`. It can look like `lo` in some fonts. I would at least capitalize the `O` as well in `IO` so it reads as `sendIO`. Some alternatives I'm a fan of: `Deno.jupyter.iopub.send(...)` `Deno.jupyter.broadcast(...)` `Deno.jupyter.publishIO(...)` However, I don't wish to hold things up for the name of the function. Let's get this out there!

body:
I tried `deno run --watch --inspect foo.js` and both vscode as well as dedicated DevTools for node seemed to automatically reconnect after a change.

But unsurprisingly, connecting to a specific thread using `chrome://inspect/#devices` didn't automatically reconnect.
In fact, it caused Deno to panic
```
============================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Platform: macos aarch64
Version: 1.37.0
Args: ["./target/debug/deno", "run", "--watch", "--inspect", "inspecttest/foo.js"]

thread '<unnamed>' panicked at 'all branches are disabled and there is no else branch', runtime/inspector_server.rs:339:5
```
Although I can't seem to reproduce this anymore now.

Another issue might be that if the debugger is paused, making a change doesn't restart the application until the debugger is resumed.

@bartlomieju Are these the pointers you were referring to? Or was this about showing the console message?
author: jespertheend

body:
@jespertheend nice work! The panic you are hitting is somewhat expected - before this change, we didn't allow using `--inspect` and `--watch` together, as the inspector server assumed that it would be closed down when the process finishes. Now we need to handle closure of in-process WS pipe more gracefully, by shutting down the appropriate task. 

I think for starters, you can try to add an "else" branch to the `tokio::select!` macro in `pump_websocket_messages` that will break out of the loop and finish the spawned task. Looking at this code, I can say that it hasn't been updated much in the recent months, I might want to budget a couple hours this week to clean it up and refresh to make the code paths more obvious.

body: Just gave this a go locally, and it's pretty nice to be able to use JSX in the repl. Some things I ran into: The classic JSX transform works with the `@jsx` pragma, but I couldn't get `@jsxImportSource` to work. Seems to be a combination of a few things: - The `jsx_import_source` option is not passed to `.transpile()` - When `jsx_import_source` is set, then we also need to flip `jsx_automatic` to `true`. That option is basically the switch between the "classic" jsx pragma transform vs the new import source one. - But doing so leads to import statements ending up in the transpiled source, which breaks evaluation since the repl code is wrapped in an IIFE - Following that, it looks like the `var_decl_imports` option is supposed to take care of that by turning import statements into `await import()` calls. My assumption is that the ordering of transpilation passes is wrong in `deno_ast` here https://github.com/denoland/deno_ast/blob/03ba6141a4f503feb489203e072bd999a6bd0b73/src/transpiling/mod.rs#L288-L291 . When I correct the order then it does successfully convert import statements to `await import()` calls, but it weirdly renames the imported identifier too which it shouldn't do. I'm not sure why that happens though. Overall this is really close to getting this working 👍 EDIT: Pushed a commit that fixes the jsx import source setting not being forwarded. Not sure how to fix `var_decl_imports`.
author: marvinhagemeister
dsherret

body:
@dsherret and I had a call together and we came to the conclusion that the easiest way for now is to stub out the new transform with the classic one behind the scenes. Reason being that the new transform requires some modification to work properly in the repl. The way it's done now should solve most of the common cases in the repl.

In the future we might revisit this.

body:
> * Local variables aren't remembered between cells. They're essentially different modules.

Interesting update! It seems the regular node/ts server does the same thing, for each cell that contains `import` or `export`. It just tries to keep the rest in script mode. But even then it doesn't validate the ordering at all.

With that in mind I think this is ready to land as an MVP.
author: nayeemrmn

body:
> * Sometimes you get a silent tsc error because the cell URLs don't end with a `.ts` extension. We have a specifier denormalization layer for that but it's tricky because we currently don't normalize everywhere we should, and it needs to take into account the out-of-band media type provided with `textDocument/didOpen`.

Done now: https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/20719/commits/3d5e434dc21b878b83535ebbf8d4c29a217327b7. This normalizes all module specifiers that come out of tsc, and denormalizes notebook cell specifiers that go into tsc (by converting them to file URLs and forcing a file extension).

This basically has parity with modules now. All quick-fixes, local and remote import completion, auto-imports etc. work as far as I can see. It's in a pretty complete state apart from:

> * Local variables aren't remembered between cells. They're essentially different modules.

which is the same case with the node/tsc server.

body: Now `CliOption#unstable_bare_node_builtins` includes all necessary checks for the feature flag. The flag is passed to `FeatureChecker` at the initialization of it. `CliGraphResolver` uses `FeatureChecker#check_feature` for the availability of the feature.

body:
As the API states, it is not the same as the number of logical cores i.e `navigator.hardwareConcurrency`:

https://nodejs.org/api/os.html#oscpus

> os.cpus().length should not be used to calculate the amount of parallelism available to an application. Use [os.availableParallelism()](https://nodejs.org/api/os.html#osavailableparallelism) for this purpose.
author: littledivy

body:
> As the API states, it is not the same as the number of logical cores i.e `navigator.hardwareConcurrency`:
> 
> https://nodejs.org/api/os.html#oscpus
> 
> > os.cpus().length should not be used to calculate the amount of parallelism available to an application. Use [os.availableParallelism()](https://nodejs.org/api/os.html#osavailableparallelism) for this purpose.

@littledivy Yes. But `navigator.hardwareConcurrency` is not logical cores -- it has already considered available concurrency. I've tested it with cpu affinity masks.
author: ruihe774

body: IIRC In Deno, navigator.hardwardConcurrency is simply the core count: https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/11448
author: littledivy

BTW, `available_parallelism` is in Rust std. Why we need a crate?

body:
> Yeah, It was stabalised recently in rustc 1.59.0. We can get rid of numcpus now.

Ok, I found Deno is actually already using `available_parallelism`. See https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/9017e789df59aa8fba400f3ad8ae3b8b8290b4a8/runtime/worker_bootstrap.rs#L66

The `num_cpus` dependency is introduced by tokio.

Ideally I'd like to get to the point where the HttpRecord lives on the service future and those futures are pooled, but I'm not sure that's quite possible without async drop since the service future might be dropped midway (e.g. on TCP connection closed) but the HttpRecord must live until the JS serve handler completes.

body:
Note the change to op_http_close to only wait for connections to drain on graceful shutdown: https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/20809/commits/2d02f4e30fb8a6329bc8466435c6963d07146a39

This Is necessary for the "Throw if disturbed" test to pass which otherwise deadlocks waiting for `server.finished` while with the pooling change the server refcount lives until `HttpRecord.complete` when the record is recycled.

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/cli/tests/unit/serve_test.ts#L3323-L3374

EDIT: In https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/20822 I've added a `tokio::task::yield_now().await` to the non-graceful shutdown path to give the streaming response body resources a chance to close.
author: lrowe

body:
The change to `op_http_close` exposed a race condition where `HttpRecord::recycle` would not be called when the future was cancelled between `HttpRecord::complete` calling `waker.wake()` and the async scheduler resuming execution of the async fn.

No actual impact as the pool was about to be destroyed anyway, but it's easier to reason about when we ensure `HttpRecord::recycle` is called for every HttpRecord.

Fixed in https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/20809/commits/908df832459481a222034bb1b02ff6d7a524f31a.

body:
> How can I keep the type information around? Also, I'd love to be able to have documentation of these functions, which is not clear to me on how we expose it for these conditionally hidden functions (since they're only available when running the deno jupyter kernel).

Could you elaborate what do you mean by type information here? In general when new APIs are added then the type declarations shipped with Deno should be updated - the `Deno.jupyter` namespace is defined in `cli/tsc/dts/lib.deno.unstable.d.ts` around line 2080.

> Could we migrate to emitting the execute result inside of the evaluation result handling directly? Then we wouldn't have to do the extra serializing and deserializing of the media bundles.

Yes, I think we can do that with `Deno.jupyter.broadcast` API.

> How can I includes tests for display, format, etc? I wrote tests for them in https://github.com/rgbkrk/display.js as deno package development itself makes it really simple.

We can add `Deno.jupyter.format` to `internals` symbol and then use it in a test file. This is already done in `40_jupyter.js`:
```
internals.enableJupyter = enableJupyter;
```

Then in a test file you can do: `const jupyterFormat = Deno[Deno.internal].jupyterFormat` to get access to this function.
author: bartlomieju

body:
> In general when new APIs are added then the type declarations shipped with Deno should be updated - the `Deno.jupyter` namespace is defined in `cli/tsc/dts/lib.deno.unstable.d.ts` around line 2080.

I can update the type definitions. Are we not able to write typescript that would get transpiled and then included in the resulting Deno binary?

> > Could we migrate to emitting the execute result inside of the evaluation result handling directly? Then we wouldn't have to do the extra serializing and deserializing of the media bundles.
> 
> Yes, I think we can do that with `Deno.jupyter.broadcast` API.

What's the best way for me to pass the execution count into the `Runtime.callFunctionOn` call? 

> Then in a test file you can do: `const jupyterFormat = Deno[Deno.internal].jupyterFormat` to get access to this function.

Awesome, I'll do that.
author: rgbkrk

body:
> I can update the type definitions. Are we not able to write typescript that would get transpiled and then included in the resulting Deno binary?

You can write TypeScript in internal code and it will get transpiled automatically, but for public APIs (think all `Deno.*` APIs) we need to write these type defs manually anyway.

> What's the best way for me to pass the execution count into the Runtime.callFunctionOn call?

Create a `CallArgument` like so:
```rust
CallArgument {
  value: Some(Value::Number(execution_count)),
  ..Default::default()
}
```
and pass it in `arguments` vector to `Runtime.callFunctionOn`.

body:
> Maybe there is a way anywidget could also integrate more deeply but for now I think it makes sense to keep the APIs in userland.

The big piece we likely need for `anywidget` is going to be the comm sending and receiving piece. Given deno's commitment to web standards it seems like we'd build on `EventTarget` and likely expose both a CommManager (Registry?) as well as a simple Comm class. Some portion of it probably belongs in userland for speed of iteration and minimalism in the kernel, however I think the Comm stuff is a bit inescapable for needing kernel side implementation.
author: rgbkrk

body:
It's worth taking a close look at https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/20822/commits/6548cc8f93163c0da9f009b3279702f58c122e79. I'm not sure if we should delay `HttpRecord::recycle` until after the `InnerRequest.close()` in the `op_http_track` promise handler.

Potentially there could be a data race which could lead to a use after free between the recycle and the op_http_track promise handler running. However I'm not sure this is actually possible given it is single threaded.

To avoid this possibility we would add an op call in the promise handler where Rust would explicitly take ownership of the external and delay the `HttpRecord::recycle` until then.

EDIT: Updated with this approach which avoids the possibility of data race / use after free.

author: bartlomieju

body:
I think this is ready for review.

I removed `op_http_track` but the idea behind it lives on in `op_http_set_response_body_resource` which is now async, resolving when the body has finished streaming or been cancelled. This is used to prevent the data race by having JS follow up with a call to `op_http_close_after_finish`. The non-graceful shutdown path now calls `tokio::task::yield_now().await` to provide the extra tick in which `op_http_close_after_finish` is called. (I initially tried adding an `await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r))` in JS but that caused problems with the leaking timers in the node shim tests.)

author: bartlomieju

body:
@bartlomieju Awesome, got it to work with the latest changes you pushed to the branch 👍  Played around with it and it's already amazing even in its early stages! It's soo much better than just having watch mode 💯 

Some notes:
- HMR seems to only work with `.js` files. We'd probably want to support the other extensions too `.ts`, `.tsx`, `.jsx`
- I really like that this only re-evaluates the current module and not the whole graph. Evaluating the whole graph breaks in scenarios where you have a `setInterval` running and print a value that you imported from another module. This just works in this PR 👍 
- HMR doesn't work when you change the value of a top level export like `export const foo = "asdf"`. This is totally fine and a fair tradeoff, but if hmr fails it should just print that it failed and do a full reload like it does in "normal" watch mode. Right now when it fails it just keeps running the previous code and won't accept any further updates anymore.
- Bonus: There might be a sneaky way to make top level ESM exports work by wrapping them in an iife like `export const foo = (() => "asdf")()` through transpilation. I've tried that locally and it accepts the update, but it doesn't re-evaluate the the parent module that imported this value. But then again even without this, the changes in the PR are already incredibly useful. This should in no way block merging this PR.
- Seems like this limitation exists for anything top-level. Changing something inside a function works.

So, in conclusion this is likely a fair tradeoff to make. From a product standpoint the only thing I'd add before shipping is:

- support `.ts`, `.jsx`, `.tsx`
- fall back to full reload similar to normal watch when hot update could not be applied. But still print the current error message so that the user is made aware of that.
author: marvinhagemeister

body:
Thanks for trying this out!

> So, in conclusion this is likely a fair tradeoff to make. From a product standpoint the only thing I'd add before shipping is:
>
> - support .ts, .jsx, .tsx
> - fall back to full reload similar to normal watch when hot update could not be applied. But still print the current error message so that the user is made aware of that.

Very reasonable and I believe we just need a few hours of works to provide all this functionality.

In terms of DX - I really don't like `deno run --watch --hot-reload foo.js` - I think we should go with `deno run --hmr foo.js` - thoughts on this? How do we describe the difference between `--watch` and `--hmr`? Maybe `--watch` should just do HMR by default?
author: bartlomieju

body:
> In terms of DX - I really don't like `deno run --watch --hot-reload foo.js` - I think we should go with `deno run --hmr foo.js` - thoughts on this? How do we describe the difference between `--watch` and `--hmr`? Maybe `--watch` should just do HMR by default?

I guess this depends on how confident we are in this. Long term it makes sense to combine the two. For now going with a separate `--watch` and `--hmr/--hot` flag seems like the safest option to go with. All bundlers I know of treat HMR as an opt-in thing separately from watch, so this would match already learned behavior.

My vote is for `deno run --hmr foo.js` or `deno run --hot foo.js`.

I think the difference can be described well:

- `--watch`: restarts the process whenever a file change is detected
- `--hmr`: reload the file that changed while keeping the process alive. If the HMR update cannot be applied it falls back to restarting the process
author: marvinhagemeister
marvinhagemeister

author: petamoriken

body: This is a great result, but can we do it without creating another crate? CLI already depends on the runtime. What if we split op registration from JS registration?
author: mmastrac

body:
@mmastrac Sure, what do you propose? 

IMO Ideally, in the startup path new ops should **not** be registered when the snapshot is loaded (no work == no time). It's necessary for these ops to live in another crate so we can reference from the build script that does the snapshot.

I think this was something we wanted to do during the early extension refactors but turned out to be harder. 

The option to load ops with a snapshot still exists, I'll open a deno_core PR that this PR depends on.
author: littledivy

body:
This is probably another good argument for getting rid of the build-time snapshots TBH, but I guess I don't really understand why we can't do the snapshot work in the cli crate.

I would probably start by moving as much of the code as possible out of cli and runtime's build.rs into a new snapshot_utils mod inside of runtime, and then call that code from cli's build.rs.

We could probably remove the build.rs script from runtime entirely.
author: mmastrac

body:
>  I don't really understand why we can't do the snapshot work in the cli crate.

@mmastrac The full CLI snapshot does happen in the CLI crate (both CLI and runtime code):

- https://github.com/littledivy/deno/blob/no_op_initialzation/cli/build.rs#L411-L415
- https://github.com/littledivy/deno/blob/735d2dd08122c04219b4b538e28935c56091c583/cli/Cargo.toml#L58

using deno_runtime's `dont_create_runtime_snapshot` feature flag

> I would probably start by moving as much of the code as possible out of cli and runtime's build.rs into a new snapshot_utils mod inside of runtime, and then call that code from cli's build.rs.

This was attempted before https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14794 but we reverted it because it broke publishing: https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15076#pullrequestreview-1027904413
author: littledivy

body:
In this case the crate would be upstream? CLI would pull in code from runtime, not the other way around, which will be fine from a publishing perspective.

I am not totally convinced that we can't do it that way and tbh this snapshot code is so complex and hairy I think it's worth exploring this fully before we add even more complexity to an already overcomplex system.

body:
Could you add a test case for this?

This should be sufficient (it'll trigger the resource sanitizer for tests):

```
new TextDecoder('utf8').decode(new Uint8Array(), { stream: true });
```
author: mmastrac

body:
> Could you add a test case for this?

I can, though I'm not satisfied with the fix yet as this recreates the internal decoder resource for every chunk of the stream. I'm going to look further into the root cause of this, hence the draft status of the PR. I will update this PR and add a test once I have (hopefully) come up with a better solution.

From what I've found out so far the problem is that the decoder from `encoding_rs` requires a final call of a `decode_*` function with the `last` argument set to `true`. The deno `TextDecoder` provides the `last` argument as the negated value of the `stream` option, so we have to call `TextDecoder.decode()` at least once without the `stream` option to free the resource. This is what `TextDecoderStream` does in its `flush` method, I'm yet to find the proper way of handling this for an aborted async iterator.
author: egfx-notifications

body: @egfx-notifications FYI as of recently, there is a `TransformStream` `cancel` option (#20741)
author: crowlKats

body:
> @egfx-notifications FYI as of recently, there is a `TransformStream` `cancel` option (#20741)

Thank you! That saved me quite some time.

I just pushed a version using the `cancel` option and it works fine for my test case. Feel free to have a look, this is my first deep dive into deno code, so I may be missing something.

I'm going to test this properly and also add a minimal test case sometime next week. The test case suggested by @mmastrac is a bit too minimal as I consider the problem to be a cancelled stream not cleaning up its internal resources. Explicitly calling `TextDecoder.decode()` with the `stream` option without actually using it in a stream is not a case I want to solve, especially as the `deno test` output already mentions how to close the TextDecoder resource properly.

body:
I've updated the PR with the following commit:
```
When setting `allow-net` to a host and port, if the port is 80, then
all ports are allowed. During the parsing of the host and port,
the scheme was assumed to be `http` which makes the port redundant.
In this case, Url::parse assumed `None` for the port, which allowed
for all ports.

This change-set uses the term `unknown` for the scheme as this has
no effect on the parsing of the host and port and ensures that the
specified port is used.

Fixes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/21220
```
author: irbull

body: Looks like when you call `Url::parse` on `http://` the parse will return an error (EmptyHost) but if you use a custom scheme `unknown://` it won't.
author: irbull

body: Updated the change-set to explicitly return a `url::ParseError::EmptyHost` if the host is not set. This is what `Url::Parse` does for well known schemes. It's also probably a bit safer anyways, instead of blindly calling `unwrap`.

body:
I did some benchmarking and here's what I found:
1. Creating an `AbortController` is quite fast - it takes roughly 20ns on my machine
2. Using `createDependentAbortSignal` is slow - doing that in combination with creating an AC is about 2us, which is 100x flower.
3. The slow part in `createDependentAbortSignal` is `WeakSet` handling - we spend a lot of time in `add()` function.

So I think we should look into adding an internal fast path equivalent of `createDependentAbortSignal` that only ever has 1 dependent signal and preferably skips `WeakSet` completely, instead using `WeakRef`. @crowlKats is it something you could look at?
author: bartlomieju

body:
I think we can do this with minimal impact if we can do something like this:

 - make `signal` on `Request` lazy so that we don't create it unless it's asked for (we do something similar with the headers that are passed in -- maybe this could live on the InnerRequest as well rather than being a separate arg to `fromInnerRequest`).
 - If someone asks for it, stash the instantiated signal in the `00_serve.js` `InnerRequest` associated with the Request
 - In InnerRequest.close, if there's a signal, abort it

body:
> Are there more places where we could use this new API?

This was the only place I found using `createHostObject`.

The only objects that are currently transferable are `MessagePort` and `ArrayBuffer` (for which v8::ValueSerializer::Delegate has separate methods), see: https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/6718be87c8468b8ed27548e350c62ad86287a900/ext/web/message_port.rs#L167-L187

Hopefully this change will make it possible to make other objects transferable since it removes the substantial performance penalty from using traditional host objects created outside JS.

body:
~~If I did the type assertion like [this](https://github.com/kt3k/deno/commit/bb1d893468c2fddd6f672e47c8228cb9f6458dbe), then the type errors disappeared, but I'm not sure that's a desired fix.~~ Reordering of the overload signatures seems solving the issue.

It looks like 'Unix' overload types need to be before the non-Unix types to make them have more priority in type inference. Because all fields in `ServeOptions` is optional, `serve(options: ServeInit & ServeOptions)` can even matches to `serve({ path: "some/path", handler })` call and that seems causing trouble in type checking.

body:
In std, we use a [types.d.ts](https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/blob/84a1373b394a34e69cb86f0be208d6047e5fc541/io/types.d.ts) file which copies these IO interfaces exactly. Sub-modules that use these identical interfaces from types.d.ts and are not deprecated include:
* `std/archive` - https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/pull/1985 aims to migrate to the Web Streams API.
* `std/log`

Yep, I've already created an issue to do that [here](https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/issues/3907).

Agreed.

author: ceifa

body:
I am no longer working on this PR.

If I remember correctly, the Deno language server does not have proper notebook support but treats every cell like a separate document. This is the default fallback mechanism for notebooks if the language server does not support notebooks. That behavior causes some problems; for example, the type of variables from the previous cell is unknown in the subsequent cells. Fixing this would require the language server to treat all cells as a single file, but that would come with other issues, as the cells are not necessarily executed from top to bottom. However, that would still be more useful than the current implementation. Afaik, no language server for a statically typed language currently has _good_ support for notebooks.

The language server protocol supports notebooks, but we need `tower-lsp` to do the same. I opened a PR over there (ebkalderon/tower-lsp#401), but that is blocked by gluon-lang/lsp-types#266, which is blocked by gluon-lang/lsp-types#266.

I am closing this PR because I have given up on using deno for notebooks.
author: zebreus

body:
> I cannot figure out how to add a test. The right level to test would be `inspectArgs` (or higher), since it's the function that builds the arguments for `cssToAnsi`, but all the `inspectArgs` tests in `cli/tests/unit/console_test.ts` call `stripColor`.

You could set the `colors` option on `inspectArgs` to `true`, that would force colors on, and then just dont call `stripColor`
author: crowlKats

body: Whether to print colours or not with `%c` is controlled by the `noColor` variable (https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/543718629c1dd082e2a7288e3a24fd29f102e412/ext/console/01_console.js#L3138), which as far as I understand is not affected by `options.colors` (https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/543718629c1dd082e2a7288e3a24fd29f102e412/ext/console/01_console.js#L3095)
